I have position and the hand has good equity against a typical 3-betting range. I will call the 900 difference to see a flop and utilize my positional advantage on later streets.

I was the preflop 3-bettor to 1,200 and have AhQs on 9h 3d 9s. As the aggressor my range is strong, and this paired, fairly dry flop will miss a lot of the villain’s flat-call range (Kx/broadways/some Axs). I want to charge those hands and deny cheap runouts while keeping the pot manageable with only Ace-high. A small-to-medium continuation bet (~1/3 pot) accomplishes that: it folds many unpaired hands, gets value from worse Ax/Kx that will call, but is cheap enough that I can fold to a sizable check‑raise from sets/9x. I’ll bet 800 into the 2,400 pot. If Flash calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he raises large I’ll release this Ace-high (he will often have a 9 or a set when check‑raising aggressively).

The flop of 9h-3d-9s is a paired, dry board. Minni has led with a 1/3 pot bet of 800 into 2,400 after 3-betting pre-flop. While I didn't hit this flop, King-Jack high still has decent equity against a 3-betting range that includes many ace-high hands and under-pairs. Folding getting 4:1 pot odds would be too tight. I will call to see a turn and evaluate based on the board progression and Minni's continued aggression.

I am the preflop 3-bettor and c-bet the dry, paired flop with A♥Q♠ but I haven’t improved. The turn (9h 3d 9s 8d) is a relatively blank card for the villain’s flop-call range (weaker A’s, Kx, broadways), and as the aggressor I can credibly represent a 9/overpair or better. Betting here gets folds from a lot of marginal hands and charges any diamond/backdoor draws while keeping the pot manageable given the effective stacks (~14.8k) and SPR (~3.7). I don’t want to bloat the pot with mere Ace-high, so I’ll make a medium-sized continuation bet (~1/2 pot) to take it down or set up a controlled river. If Flash check-raises substantially (polarized line), I’ll release this Ace-high since that line usually indicates 9x/sets. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river.

On a board of 9h-3d-9s-8d, Minni has followed up a 1/3 pot flop continuation bet with a 1/3 pot turn bet (2,000 into 6,000) after 3-betting pre-flop. My King-high has not improved, and the 8d doesn't help my hand but could potentially help a semi-bluff range (like 76s, T7s, or JTs). While the odds are 4:1 to call, Minni's 3-betting range pre-flop and continued aggression on this board texture suggests they either have a strong hand (like an overpair or a 9) or are committed to the bluff. With only King-high and no real draw other than two overcards that might not be clean outs, continuing against this sustained pressure is risky. However, given the pot odds and the fact that Minni has been very aggressive in previous hands, I could consider a call, but my hand strength is fundamentally just King-high. Looking back at Hand #34 and Hand #29, Minni is capable of multi-barrel aggression. I will fold here to preserve my stack and find a better spot, as I am currently losing to all pairs and even Ace-high.

Heads-up on the button (I’m also the small blind) with Js-Tc — a strong, well-connected hand that plays very well postflop in position. Limping is too passive; I want to take the initiative, build a pot with fold equity, and avoid giving a cheap flop to speculative hands. Min raise is 200, but I’ll open to a standard 3xBB sizing to 300 total. This sizing keeps my range wide, gives good postflop maneuverability with deep stacks, and sets me up to c-bet many flops or apply pressure if Flash shows weakness.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qs 3s. The opponent, Minni, has raised to 300 from the Button (Small Blind). The pot is currently 400, and it costs me 200 more to call, giving me 2:1 pot odds. While Q3s is not a premium hand, in a heads-up situation against a wide button-opening range, it is strong enough to defend. My hand is suited, which adds to its post-flop playability. Given Minni's recent aggressive play, I will call to see a flop and attempt to realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position.

I have bottom pair (Threes) with a Queen kicker on a low board. The flop is 8d-3d-2h. I am out of position against Minni. In a heads-up pot where I defended the big blind, checking to the aggressor is the standard play to see how they react. If Minni bets, I have enough equity and a pair to continue to at least one bet, especially since I block some straight draws and have a back-door spade draw, although the board is diamonds. I will check and evaluate based on Minni's bet sizing.

I raised preflop and have position with Js-Tc. The flop (8d-3d-2h) is very dry but has two diamonds — a board I can c-bet extensively as the preflop aggressor. Flash checked, showing weakness or a draw; I want to take the initiative, deny equity to non-diamond overcards and small pairs, and keep the pot manageable with deep stacks. A 1/3-pot continuation bet (~200 into 600) achieves that: it folds many hands that miss, keeps draws and better hands in so I can exploit them later, and leaves room to barrel or pot-control on the turn depending on the card. Plan: if called, re-evaluate on turn (fire again on safe cards, check back versus obvious diamond/paired turns).

I have bottom pair (Threes) on a dry, low board. Minni has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 600. I am getting 4:1 odds to call. This small sizing often indicates a range-wide continuation bet or a thin value hand. Since I have a pair, I cannot fold to such a small bet, especially heads-up. I will call to see the turn and evaluate if Minni continues her aggression. Raising here is unnecessary as it would only force out hands I beat and get called by better.

On the turn, the board has developed to 8d-3d-2h-5h. I still hold bottom pair (threes) with a Queen kicker. While I have some showdown value, my hand is relatively weak and does not benefit from betting into the opponent's range. Checking is the best strategy here to keep the pot small and potentially see a cheap showdown or induce a bluff from a wider range. I am out of position, and checking allows me to evaluate Minni's next move.

I raised pre and c-bet the very dry 8♦-3♦-2♥ flop with JsTc and was called. Turn 5♥ is a safe, non-diamond, non-pair card and Flash checks — a sign of weakness (or a missed draw). As the in-position aggressor I should continue to pressure his calling range (A-high, Kx, small pairs, missed draws) and deny free cards. I’ll fire a half-pot second barrel to fold out weaker pairs and A-high hands while keeping some draws and worse hands in — size chosen to get good fold equity but not commit the stacks. If I’m raised strongly I’ll typically relinquish since I have no pair or draw; if called I’ll re-evaluate river.

I am holding bottom pair (threes) with a Queen kicker. The turn card (5h) adds some straight possibilities (like 6-4 or A-4), but generally doesn't shift the board texture drastically against a wide button-opening range. Minni's bet of 500 into a 1000 pot gives me 3:1 pot odds. In a heads-up match, folding bottom pair to a second barrel can be overly conservative, especially against an aggressive opponent who frequently bets. I have enough equity to call one more bet to see if I can improve on the river or reach a showdown against her bluffs and semi-bluffs, such as diamond draws.

I have improved to three-of-a-kind (threes) on the river with the 3c. While this is a very strong hand, I have played the hand passively so far by check-calling. If I lead out now, I risk folding out Minni's bluffs (like missed diamond draws or straight draws). By checking, I induce Minni to continue her aggressive line or value bet a weaker hand like an eight. I plan to check-call or check-raise depending on her bet size.

Check. I have JsTc with no pair and very little showdown value. I barreled flop and turn and was called both times, which tells me Flash’s continuing range contains a lot of hands that beat me (small/medium pairs, 8x, some 2x, and possibly hands that improved on the river). The river 3 pairs the board and actually improves any single-3 holdings to trips, so my fold equity is lower than it looked on the turn. To make a profitable bluff here I’d need a sizable fold frequency (e.g. a 1,000 chip bet into a 2,000 pot requires >33% folds), and he’s shown a sticky calling line — not the opponent I expect to fold often. Betting risks a lot of chips for little chance of success; checking preserves my stack and realizes the small showdown equity I have. If he leads after my check I’ll re-evaluate (fold most sizes; only consider calling a very small bet if the sizing and read strongly indicate air).

I have Ace-Seven of Hearts on the button in a heads-up situation against Minni. This is a strong starting hand (suited Ace) that is well ahead of an average range. I'll raise to build the pot and take control of the hand from position. A standard 3x open is effective here.

In the big blind with 5♦6♥ facing a 300 open from the button. I already have 100 in and need 200 more to call; the pot is 400 so calling gives ~3:1 pot odds (~25% equity needed). Heads‑up the opener’s range is wide, so 5‑6 offsuit has reasonable equity and playability. I’d rather see a flop cheaply than 3‑bet this marginal, offsuit connector out of position (a 3‑bet to 500 should be used with stronger hands or specific blockers). Folding would be too tight given the price. Plan: call to see the flop and continue only with meaningful connections (pair, two‑pair, open‑ender, or good equity), otherwise proceed cautiously vs aggression.

I have top pair (6s) with a weak-ish 5 kicker on a low, somewhat dry flop (6c 4d 2d). Flash opened wide from the button preflop, so his range contains a lot of missed overcards and some small pairs. I'm out of position and I want to both get value from worse pairs/paired boards and deny free cards to his draws (gutshot to a 3 and the unlikely diamond-run). Betting protects my hand and extracts value from Ax, small pairs (55,44,22), and draws that will pay to see another card. A half‑pot c‑bet (~300 into 600) is a good, standard size here: it gives fold equity, charges draws, and keeps worse hands and floats in the pot. If he raises big I will re‑evaluate (he could have a set/66/strong draw) and proceed cautiously; but for now bet 300 to build value and protect.

I have Ace-high with Ace-Seven of hearts on a low, somewhat disconnected board (6-4-2). Minni's lead (donk bet) for half the pot on this texture can represent a variety of hands, including small pairs, straight draws (5-3, 7-5), or even air trying to take advantage of the fact that this board misses a standard pre-flop raising range. Ace-high is often the best hand here, and I have two overcards and back-door equity. Folding would be too tight given the pot odds and my position. I will call to see a turn and evaluate based on the next card.

I have 5♦6♥ on 6♣-4♦-2♦-8♥ — top pair (6) with a weak 5-kicker. Pot is 1,200 and Flash opened wide preflop and then simply called my flop c-bet, so his continuing range is wide (small pairs like 4s/2s/5s, floats with overcards, and some backdoor draws). The 8 on the turn is an overcard to my kicker but it doesn't add a diamond draw; I want to charge floating overcards and the small pairs that will call while denying free cards. I'll fire a value/protection bet of roughly 2/3 pot (800). If he jams/raises big it's usually a hand that beats me (8x/sets) and I'll give it up; if he just calls I'll re-evaluate on the river and plan to extract value from worse one-pair hands.

On the turn, the board is 6c-4d-2d-8h. Minni has followed up her flop donk-bet with a larger bet of 800 into a 1200 pot. My Ace-high (Ah7h) has a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5), but the 8h is a card that likely connects with Minni's range (potentially 8x hands or 75 for an open-ended draw). Given the pot odds, I need roughly 28.5% equity to call. With only 4 outs for a straight and potentially 3 outs for an Ace (which might not even be good if she has two pair or a set), my equity is approximately 15-20%. Chasing this draw out of position against continued aggression is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button (SB) with Jc8c — a playable, suited hand in heads‑up. Pot is 150 and I only need 50 to call; the min raise is to 200. In heads‑up I want to take the initiative and exploit the BB’s wide defending range. A raise to 200 (2x BB) applies pressure, keeps the pot controllable with deep stacks (18300 vs 21550), and preserves positional advantage postflop. If Flash folds I scoop the blinds; if he calls I’ll use my suitedness and position to C‑bet favorable flops and control the pot. If he 3‑bets large I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to a big 3‑bet, consider calling a small one). Raise to 200 is the best default aggressive line here.

In Hand #44, I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Hearts and Seven of Hearts. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 200. I need to call 100 more to see the flop. With pot odds of 3:1 and a suited hand that has decent playability in a heads-up pot, calling is a standard and profitable move. I have a healthy stack and want to see how the flop develops.

I am in the Big Blind and acting first post-flop. The board is Jack-high (6h Jd 5d) and I hold Ten-high with a backdoor heart flush draw. I have no made hand and very little equity against a standard opening range. Checking is the standard play here to see how the opponent reacts; I have no reason to lead into the pre-flop aggressor with this hand.

I have top pair (Jc8c) on 6h Jd 5d and I’m in position. Flash checked, so I want to both extract value from worse Jx and protect against diamond draws/overcards. A half‑pot c‑bet keeps the initiative, denies equity to many of his calling hands, and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks. Plan: bet 200 into 400; if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (continue on safe cards, slow down on diamonds/high cards). If he raises big I’ll re‑assess (likely call small raises, fold to extreme aggression without a read).

On a board of Jd-6h-5d, I have 10h-7h, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8) and a backdoor heart draw. Minni bet 200 into a 400 pot, which is a half-pot continuation bet. I have roughly 16% equity if I count the four 8s as clean outs, and some minor equity from my overcards or the backdoor flush. Getting 3:1 on a call, it's a bit thin but acceptable to peel one card to see if I improve on the turn, especially since I have a stack advantage and can potentially outplay Minni on later streets or represent the diamond draw if it misses.

On the turn, the board is 6h-Jd-5d-4h. I hold 10h-7h, which gives me a flush draw (9 outs) and a gutshot straight draw (three 8s not including the 8h). With a total of 12 clean outs, I have approximately 27% equity. Since Minni was the pre-flop and flop aggressor, I will check to her. If she bets, I have the correct odds to call and see the river; if she checks back, I get a free card to potentially hit my hand.

I have top pair with a medium kicker (J♣8♣) on 6♥ J♦ 5♦ 4♥. Flash called my flop c‑bet and now checked the turn — his range is wide (worse Jx, small pairs 6x/5x, backdoor/now‑missed diamond draws, and single‑card straight draws like 7x/3x). I want to both extract value from worse pairs/Jx and charge the draws while keeping the pot controllable with a weak kicker and deep stacks. A half‑pot bet accomplishes that: it folds out pure air, charges diamonds and open‑enders, and gets value from worse hands without bloating the pot if I face aggression. If he check‑raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to a large raise); if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the river depending on the card.

On the turn, the board is 6h-Jd-5d-4h and I hold Th-7h. The 4h gives me a heart flush draw (9 outs) and I still have my gutshot straight draw (the three remaining 8s). This gives me approximately 12 outs, or roughly 26% equity. Minni has bet 400 into a pot of 800, giving me 3:1 pot odds (25% required equity to call). Since my equity is roughly equal to the odds offered and I have implied odds if I hit my flush or straight on the river, calling is a mathematically sound decision. I will call the bet and look to see if I improve on the river.

The river is the Queen of Hearts (Qh), completing my heart flush. The board now reads 6h-Jd-5d-4h-Qh. I have a flush, though it is a ten-high flush. Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand, betting pre-flop, on the flop, and on the turn. Since she checked on the river, it is likely she either had a marginal hand that she's now afraid of or she was bluffing. However, given my flush, I have the best hand most of the time. I will bet for value, hoping to get called by a Jack, a Queen, or a lower flush if she somehow stayed in with one. A bet of 1000 into a 1600 pot (about 60% pot) is a strong value bet that can still be called by weaker made hands.

I have top pair (J♣8♣) on 6♥ J♦ 5♦ 4♥ Q♥. The river Q is a scare card (gives Qx and some two‑pair/two‑pair combos), but Flash’s river bet is fairly small (1,000 into a 2,600 pot) — calling 1,000 gets ~3.6:1 pot odds (need ≈21.7% equity). Given he called my flop and turn, his range includes many worse one‑pair hands (weaker Jx, 6x/5x), missed draws and thin bluffs that I beat. Raising is thin with a medium kicker and would just bloats the pot against hands that already beat me (Qx/two‑pair/flush). I’ll call 1,000 to get value from worse and force bluffs to pay; if he now raises big I’ll fold and avoid committing more with a marginal kicker.
I have King-Ten suited on the button. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up or short-handed scenario. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot.

Facing a 300 open from the button with 4♦3♥ in the big blind. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (calling to 300 total); that makes the final pot 600 so I need ~33% equity to break even. In heads-up play the open-raising range is very wide and 4-3o has reasonable equity and great implied odds deep-stacked. 3-betting out of position with this raggy, unpaired hand is a leak — I have little fold equity and don’t want to bloat a pot OOP without a real hand. Folding is too tight heads-up. I’ll call to see a flop and play cautiously OOP: fold to heavy turn aggression without improvement, but continue when I hit a pair, two pair, or meaningful straight/flush draws or can realize implied odds.

I have a weak pair (trip? No — pair of threes with a 4 kicker) on a dry Q♦8♠3♦board. Flash opened wide heads-up, so his range contains lots of Qx/8x and many air hands that will c‑bet, but it also contains many hands that already beat me. I'm out of position, so I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal thin-value bet or bluff — checking controls the pot and lets me see how he reacts. Plan: check, call a small-to-medium c‑bet to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to large raise/overly heavy aggression unless I improve to two pair/trips, and if he checks back I get a free turn card to improve.

I have King-high with a backdoor flush draw on a dry Queen-high board. As the pre-flop aggressor, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet. A bet of half the pot (300) should be sufficient to fold out many of Minni's non-pairing hands and allow me to take down the pot immediately, while still leaving me with equity if called.

Call. I have a weak pair (3s with a 4 kicker) out of position on Qh‑8s‑3d. Flash’s flop sizing is small (~1/3 pot) and in heads‑up spots his c‑bet range is wide, so this price (~33% pot) gives me good immediate pot odds and reasonable implied odds with both stacks deep. Calling keeps worse hands and bluffs in, lets me realize equity, and preserves the option to fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve. Plan: call the flop, re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to large raises without improvement; continue/raise only with two pair/trips or clear redraws/solid turn action.

I just improved to trips on the turn (3h with 3d/3c on board). Trips is a very strong hand heads‑up, but it is vulnerable to full houses (pocket QQ or pocket 88 in the raiser’s preflop/c‑bet range). Flash has been opening wide and c‑betting small, so his range contains many Qx/8x and bluffs — there are far more hands I beat than hands that currently beat me. Checking controls the pot but gives him a free card and lets him realize equity; betting extracts value from worse Q/8/top‑pair hands and charging floats, and also denies a free river that could turn his one‑pair hands into a full house.

Plan: lead a modest value bet to get called by worse pairs and floats while keeping his range wide. A ~50% pot bet (600 into 1,200) balances value extraction and pot control OOP. If he calls I proceed to the river planning for value or pot control depending on the sizing; if he raises large and polarizes, I will strongly consider folding to an all‑in since trips can be crushed by full houses — but versus a small/medium raise I will call and evaluate the river.

On the turn, the board pairs with a 3c, and Minni leads out for 600 into an 1200 pot after checking the flop. This 'donk' lead often represents a 3, a slow-played Queen, or a bluff capitalizing on the board pairing. My King-Ten of spades has no real value here other than King-high, and I have very little equity to improve to the best hand if behind. Calling would be a floating play with no direct equity, and raising is too risky against a range that could include trips. The most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (I posted SB 50) with 9♠4♣. In heads-up play my opening range should be very wide — raising takes the initiative, builds pots when ahead, and uses my positional advantage. Calling 50 to see a flop is cheap, but open-raising gains fold equity and lets me dictate postflop play. I don’t want to inflate the pot too much with a marginal offsuit hand, but a standard-sized open will win many pots instantly and still allows me to fold to a sizable 3-bet. I will raise to 300 total (a 3x-ish open). If Flash 3-bets large I’ll respect that and likely fold; if he calls I’ll play positionally and c-bet selectively.

I am in the big blind holding Jd 5s and Minni has raised to 300 from the button/small blind. This hand is quite weak and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. While I have a large stack, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand against a standard raise from Minni, who has shown aggressive play in recent hands. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have Ace-Nine offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation against Minni. This is a strong starting hand for heads-up play, well above the average. My standard strategy from the button is to raise to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the hand. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that helps build the pot and defines the range.

I will call the extra 200 (total commit 300). Heads‑up vs the button, Flash opens very wide and I’m getting roughly 200 into a 400 pot (break‑even ~33%), so T2o is borderline but defendable — folding here would be too nitty and 3‑betting is a mistake with this hand (dominated and no real fold equity). Plan: play the flop cautiously out of position — check‑call small c‑bets with backdoor equity or a pair, fold to heavy turn pressure without improvement, and extract value or bloat the pot only if I actually pair the Ten/Two or pick up strong draws. Calling keeps the pot manageable and preserves stack while realizing equity vs a wide open range.

Check. I have a marginal made hand — bottom pair (2♦) with a weak Ten kicker on 9♠-2♥-5♦ — and I'm out of position versus a very wide-button opener. My preflop plan was to play this hand cautiously: control the pot and check‑call small continuation bets rather than lead or bloat the pot. Checking lets Flash bluff or c-bet worse hands while I realize equity; I'll call small-to-moderate c-bets (~100–400) and fold to heavy turn pressure if I don't improve. If Flash checks back I re-evaluate on the turn and only start building the pot if I make two‑pair/trips or pick up a strong draw. With stacks (16,950 vs 22,450) pot control is the priority here.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace kicker on a very dry board (9s-2h-5d). This is an excellent situation for a value bet. Minni has checked to me, so I will make a standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to get value from weaker nines, pocket pairs, and some gutshot draws like 6-7 or 3-4.

Call. I have bottom pair (2♦) with a weak Ten kicker and I'm out of position, but Flash is the button and opens very wide. That flop bet is small (300 into a 900 pot) and represents a lot of c-bets/bluffs — I’m being offered good pot odds to see another card. Calling 300 keeps the pot controlled, realizes equity vs a wide range, and preserves stack depth (stacks are deep enough that 300 is a small price). Plan: call and re-evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve, but continue vs small/medium turn bets and try to extract value or punish bluffs if I pick up two-pair/trips or a better hand.

I turned two pair (Tens and Twos) on the turn. Heads‑up vs Flash (who opens very wide and just checked the turn), this is a clear spot to extract value and deny free rivers to missed draws/overcards. Betting about half the pot (~600 into 1200) gets called by worse one‑pair hands (we beat most Tx, 9x, 5x, many broadways and draws) while keeping the pot manageable out of position. Plan: bet 600 for value; if he raises small I’ll call and re‑evaluate on a safe river, but respect a large check‑raise/shove as often representing trips or a lock hand and fold accordingly. On river I’ll size further value bets based on the card and his line (blank → value bet; scary card/large aggression → proceed cautiously).

I currently have a pair of Nines with an Ace kicker. On the turn, a Ten of Spades appears, which is an overcard to my pair. Minni has led out (donk-bet) for 600 into a 1200 pot. This lead often signifies a hand that has improved (like a Ten or Two Pair) or a blocking bet with a draw/marginal hand. Given the pot odds (calling 600 to win 2400, or 25% equity required) and the strength of my Ace-high second pair, I have a clear call. Raising would likely only be called by better hands, and folding is too tight given Minni's past aggression. I will call to see the river and evaluate.

On the turn I turned two pair and bet for value; Flash called. The river (9♦) pairs the board and is a very “scary” card for my line — any 9 in his flop‑betting range now has trips or a full house and will beat my two‑pair. Given Flash’s very wide opening and the fact he c‑bet the flop and then check‑called the turn, a sizable portion of his call range contains 9x combos. Betting now risks getting called (or raised) by hands that beat me while folding out the weaker pairs/bluffs I want to extract value from. Checking keeps pot control, induces bluffs, and lets me call modest river bets from missed hands while folding to large river aggression that usually represents trips/full houses. Plan: check; call small/medium river bets (roughly ≤ ~1/2 pot) to catch bluffs and weaker pairs, but fold to big shoves or large polarizing raises.

The river Nine of Diamonds has improved my hand to three-of-a-kind Nines with an Ace kicker. Minni led on the turn (a 'donk bet') when the Ten appeared, which often indicates she hit a pair of Tens. Her check on the river suggests she is either giving up on a bluff or, more likely, is in check-call mode with a Ten or a mid-pair now that the board has paired the Nine. Given the strength of my hand, I have a clear value bet. I will bet 1500 into the 2400 pot to extract value from a Ten or a pocket pair, as it's unlikely she has 10-9, 5-5, or 2-2 based on the betting sequence.

I call 1500. I turned two pair (Tens and Deuces) and checked the river to keep the pot manageable — Flash’s line (wide preflop opener, c-bet flop, call on my turn bet) contains a lot of bluffs and single-pair/overcard hands but also some 9x / T9 / TT combos that now beat me. Pot math: pot is 3900 and he bet 1500, so calling 1500 invests to win 6900 total — break‑even equity ≈ 21.7%. Versus a wide, river‑betting range my two‑pair comfortably exceeds that threshold. I also block several of his strongest full‑house combos (I hold a Ten and the Deuce), so the frequency of hands that absolutely crush me is limited. Raising would polarize and invite big action from full houses/trips; folding would be too nitty and surrender value. Therefore I make the call to catch bluffs and worse one‑pair hands.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) I want to be the aggressor with a very playable hand. Tc9c is a strong, position-friendly hand heads-up (suited connector, good equity and post-flop playability vs a wide BB range). Limping/calling 50 surrenders initiative and gives Flash cheap odds to see a flop; a standard open from the button applies pressure and builds pots where T9s performs well. I’ll open to a conventional size (300 total) — big enough to get fold equity and discourage marginal flat-calls, but small enough to keep postflop maneuverability with deep stacks. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (fold or defend depending on sizing); if he calls we play position and use our hand’s equity and suitedness to continue.

I am in the big blind with 9s 5h, a weak hand that is generally folded to a raise. Minni has raised to 300, and I need to call 200 more. Given the poor quality of my hand and the fact that I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand, folding is the most prudent long-term strategy to preserve my stack.

In a heads-up situation as the small blind/button with 4s Qh, this hand is generally too weak to raise profitably. However, since the cost to complete the big blind is only 50 chips, it's occasionally acceptable to limp, though folding is the safest play with a hand this weak. Given our large chip lead and the relatively small blinds, I will fold to avoid playing a marginal hand out of position post-flop if Minni raises, or in a bloated pot if she checks.

Heads-up on the button with A♣T♣ — a clear open-raise rather than just completing. I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3×BB). This builds value with a strong, well-blocking hand, creates fold equity against a wide BB defense, and preserves positional advantage for postflop play. Min-raise to 200 is an option, but 300 is standard here and avoids giving the BB too-good odds to see a cheap flop. Postflop plan: continuation bet most flops, apply pressure on favorable textures, call small 3-bets and re-evaluate on the turn, fold to very large 3-bets if I miss and don’t have enough equity.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s3s. Minni, who is currently the only other active player at the table, has raised to 300 from the small blind/button. 63s is a weak hand but has some playability in terms of flush and straight potential. However, out of position against Minni, who has been quite aggressive, this hand is generally a fold. Calling 200 more into a 400 pot gives me 3:1 odds, but realizing that equity out of position is difficult. Given the stack sizes and the tendency for Minni to play pots aggressively (as seen in Hand #47 and others), I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot.

In a heads-up situation as the small blind/button, Ten-Eight suited is a strong starting hand that should be played aggressively. Standard play is to open-raise to around 3x the big blind. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take advantage of my position throughout the hand.

I’m on the big blind with T♠3♠ facing a 300 button open. The pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call (about 25% break-even equity) — reasonable for a suited hand heads-up. T3s has usable equity and implied odds heads-up, but I’m out of position and stacks are deep, so a bluff 3-bet here is speculative and I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a weak hand. I’ll flat to see a flop, keep the pot controllable, and then proceed based on texture: fold to heavy aggression on dry A/K-high flops, but continue or apply pressure with a pair, strong draws, or disguised two-pair/sets. Put 300 total into the pot to call the raise.

Board is 3♦ A♠ J♠ and I hold T♠3♠ — I’ve hit bottom pair plus I hold two spades, giving me a one-card (9-out) spade flush draw and extra outs to make two pair/trips (3 tens + 2 threes). That’s solid equity (~50%+ to improve to two-pair or better by the river). Flash was the preflop raiser and will have a lot of A/J/broadway hands; I’m out of position and stacks are deep, so I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal made hand that can be easily dominated. Checking keeps the pot controllable and lets me call a routine c-bet to realize my equity (I’ll call small–medium bets and fold to highly polarized overbets/raises). If Flash checks back I get a free turn to continue; if I improve on the turn I can take the initiative then.

I have 10h 8h on a board of 3d As Js. I have no direct pair or draw, and the board texture is quite favorable for the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many Aces and Jacks. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage that includes the strongest hands (AA, AK, AJ). Checking back allows me to see a turn card for free and potentially realize equity if a Heart or an Eight/Ten falls, while also controlling the size of the pot with a weak hand.

Board is 3♦ A♠ J♠ J♣; I hold 3♠ T♠. I’ve got two-pair (Jacks and Threes) on the turn — a strong heads-up hand but vulnerable to Jx/Ax/3x. Flash checked flop and turn, so his range is wide and contains many hands worse than mine (medium pairs, K/Qx, missed aces/broadway, and spade/straight draws). I want to extract value and deny free rivers to spade/overcard draws, so I’ll make a value/protection bet of about 2/3 pot. If I face a large raise I’ll re-evaluate (likely behind to trips/full houses); if I get a single-call I can control the river.

On the turn, the board has paired Jacks (As Js 3d Jc). I have 10h 8h, which has no value and very little equity. Minni's lead of 400 into a 600 pot (making it 1000) shows she likely has at least some part of this board or is capitalizing on my flop check. Since I have no pair and no draw, and I'm facing a bet on a board that is better for her range of defending hands, I will fold.

Heads-up on the button with K♥4♦ — in position and deep (≈150BB). In this format the correct approach is to open a very wide range and take the initiative. K4o is marginal vs full ring but perfectly playable heads-up with position and fold equity. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3xBB) to build the pot, apply pressure to the big blind, and avoid letting them see a cheap flop. Plan: play postflop in position — c-bet favorable flops, check and evaluate on dry boards. Fold to a large 3-bet; consider calling small 3-bets depending on sizing and reads.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh4c. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Qh4c is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack sizes and the hand strength, folding is the most prudent long-term strategy here to preserve chips for better spots.

I am on the button with King-Four suited (Kh4h), which is a strong hand to open with in a heads-up or short-handed situation. I have a significant stack lead over Minni. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take the initiative and build the pot.

Heads-up vs a standard 3x open to 300 from the button. I have 7♦5♣ — a weak, unsuited, semi-connected hand but in heads-up play I should be calling much wider. Pot is 400 and it’s only 200 to call, giving me good pot odds (~200 into a 600 pot) to see a flop and realize equity. 3-betting out of position with 7‑5o is too thin and folding is overly tight; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me play post‑flop with implied odds. Plan: call to see the flop, proceed cautiously OOP — fold to heavy action on dry boards, continue with pairs/draws or use pot control against aggression.

Flop is J♦ J♠ 6♥ and I hold 7♦5♣ — I missed completely with no pair and essentially no real draw. Flash was the preflop raiser but hasn’t shown strength here (he checked), which can be either weakness or a slowplay; either way I’m OOP and have no reason to build the pot. Betting as a bluff is thin (villain can call with better hands or check‑raise), so the prudent play is pot control: check and see how he responds. Plan: check now, fold to any sizable bet on the flop, call a very small probe only if price is great, and re‑evaluate on the turn if I pick up equity or get a better read.

I have King-high with a flush draw on a paired board (J-J-6). Minni checked to me. While I haven't hit a pair, my hand has decent equity with the flush draw and overcards. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here to represent a Jack or a strong pair and to try and take the pot down immediately. I will bet 300 into the 600 pot.

Heads‑up vs a preflop raiser on J♦ J♠ 6♥ with 7♦5♣. I have no pair and no real draw — only 6 clean outs (three 7s + three 5s). Pot is 900 and it’s 300 to call, so I’m getting about 25% pot‑odds to see the turn (300/(900+300) ≈ 25%). My chance to hit by the river with 6 outs is ≈24.1%, so purely as a draw this is roughly break‑even to slightly -EV. Given the villain’s wide HU opening frequency and the small flop probe bet, calling to see a turn is the prudent play: it keeps the pot controllable, allows me to realize equity and gather information, and I can fold to substantial turn aggression. I will not bluff‑raise out of position with nothing. Plan: call the 300, fold to significant turn raises or big river pressure unless I improve to a real hand.

I have 7♦5♣ on J♦ J♠ 6♥ 3♣ — I completely missed. My only realistic out is a 4 (4 outs ≈ 8.7% to hit the river), so I have almost no equity versus a c-betting preflop aggressor. Flash opened and barreled the flop, so his range is heavy on Jx/broadways/thin value; building this pot out of position with nothing is -EV. Pot control is required: check and take a free river card. Plan: check the turn, fold to meaningful turn aggression, and only continue on the river if I actually make the straight or the board/reads give a good bluffing spot.

The turn card is the Trey of Clubs, which doesn't change much on this paired board of J-J-6. I still have King-high with a flush draw. Minni checked again, showing weakness or a willingness to pot-control with a marginal hand. By betting again, I maintain the initiative and put pressure on her. A second barrel can often fold out hands like small pocket pairs or weak Sixes that called the flop. If I get called, I still have the flush draw as an out, and potentially my King could be good. I'll bet 600 into the 1200 pot, a standard half-pot sized continuation bet.

I have 7♦5♣ on J♦ J♠ 6♥ 3♣ — I completely missed. There are only 4 clean outs (the 4s) ≈ 8.7% to hit the river. Pot is 1,800 and Flash is betting 600, so calling would require ~25% equity (600/(1800+600)=25%), far above my actual equity. Flash opened pre and barreled flop + turn, showing strength (likely Jx, better pairs or strong draws). Raising as a bluff OOP here is very thin and -EV. Following my earlier plan to check and fold to meaningful turn aggression, the correct play is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

Heads-up from the button (small blind) with A♥T♣ — a strong, open-raising hand. A standard 3x open to 300 builds the pot while keeping postflop maneuverability with deep stacks and preserves positional advantage. Plan: if called, continuation-bet favorable flops and play postflop aggressively; be cautious and often concede to large 3-bet/4-bet aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs 8d. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. Q8o is a relatively weak hand, but in a heads-up situation against a 3x open, it is generally within the defending range. However, Minni has been quite aggressive lately and my stack is significantly larger. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win 400), a call is standard to see a flop, although folding is also acceptable if I want to wait for better spots to exploit her aggression. I will choose to call and play post-flop.

On the flop of 8c-3s-Qc, I have flopped top two pair (Queens and Eights). This is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Since I am out of position as the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If Minni bets, I will likely just call to keep her range wide and trap, or potentially check-raise if I feel she will call with draws or weaker Queens. At this stage, checking is the most appropriate action.

Heads-up I opened 3x preflop with A♥T♣ (reasonable, keeps initiative). Flop Q♣ 8♣ 3♠: I currently have Ace-high with a single club (T♣) — no pair but a modest backdoor/runner flush possibility and a small blocker to some club combos. Against a big‑blind caller’s fairly wide calling range, a continuation bet will fold out lots of unpaired junk, deny equity to draws and weaker cards, and extract value from Kx/Jx and other hands that will call smaller bets. Pot is 600; I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (400) to apply pressure while still keeping bluffing room. If Flash calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check‑raises large I will largely concede this spot with only Ace‑high.

I have flopped top two pair (Queens and Eights) on a board of Qc-8c-3s. This is an extremely strong hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has been aggressive, and while calling her 400 bet might trap her into continuing a bluff on the turn, there is a flush draw present. I want to build the pot now to extract value from her 'Queen-X' hands and draws. Raising to 1200 allows me to take control of the hand and sets up a larger pot for later streets.

I opened to 300 preflop and c‑bet the Q♣ 8♣ 3♠ flop for 400 (2/3 pot). Flash check‑raised to 1,200, putting 800 more to call into a 2,200 pot. With A♥T♣ I have only Ace‑high and a single club — no real flush draw (would need runner‑runner) and roughly 6 clean outs to pair. Calling 800 requires ~26.7% equity; against a large check‑raise my opponent’s range is heavily weighted to Qx, sets or flopped club hands that dominate or nullify those outs, so my true equity is well below break‑even. A reraise as a bluff here is thin and unnecessarily commits chips with a very weak holding. Fold and preserve stack for better heads‑up spots.

I am on the button with 63o. This is a very weak hand, and while I have a large stack advantage, playing such a hand out of position post-flop (Minni is in the BB) isn't ideal. However, in a heads-up situation, 63o can occasionally be used as a bluff raise or a fold. Given the weak nature of the hand and the fact that I'm currently comfortably ahead in chips, I will simply fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button (small blind) with 5♣3♣ — a small suited connector with decent playability heads-up and I have position post‑flop. Pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 to call, but raising here to steal the BB and deny a cheap flop is standard. A minimum raise to 200 (2x BB) maximizes fold equity while keeping the pot controllable; if Flash folds I pick up the pot now, if he calls I’ll play a positional post‑flop pot with a hand that can make backdoor straights/flushes. If he 3‑bets large I’ll reassess (likely fold given the hand). Raise to 200 to apply pressure and leverage position.

I am in the big blind with 3h 5d. The button (Minni) has raised to 200, which means it is only 100 more for me to call into a pot of 300. While my hand is weak, the pot odds are 3:1, and Minni has been quite active. However, 35o is one of the weakest starting hands and plays very poorly out of position post-flop, especially against a player who has shown aggression in previous hands. Given that I have a significant stack lead (25950 vs 13750) and there are many hands left to play, I can afford to let go of these trash hands and wait for better spots to exploit her tendencies. Folding here preserves my chip lead and avoids unnecessary post-flop complications with a marginal hand.

I am on the button with Qh2c in a heads-up situation against Minni. Qh2c is a very weak hand, even for heads-up play from the small blind. While I have a significant chip lead (25,900 vs 13,950), there is no strategic reason to play this hand. Folding is the standard and safest play to avoid putting chips in with a marginal holding.

QcJc on the button (heads-up) is a strong, highly playable hand with great post‑flop equity and position. Flash in the big blind defends widely and often; opening takes the initiative and extracts value from worse hands while keeping SPR manageable with deep stacks. I’ll raise to the minimum opening (200 total = 2BB) to build the pot, apply pressure, and keep my range uncapped. If called I’ll use position to c‑bet or float depending on the texture; if 3‑bet I can call or 4‑bet selectively based on sizing and villain tendencies.

Minni has been raising frequently from the button. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call the additional 100 chips, 5c 7h is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given my current stack lead and the fact that we are only at hand 58, I prefer to fold and wait for a more playable hand rather than bleed chips post-flop with junk.

Holding K-10 offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation, I have a strong starting hand. I will put in a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure on Minni.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300; I'm in the big blind with T♥8♠ (T8o). In heads-up spots opening ranges are wide and T8o is well within a defendable BB range — it can make top pair, two pair, and occasional straights and has decent equity. The pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more, giving immediate pot odds of 200/(400+200) = 33%, which is easily satisfied versus a typical button raising range. 3-betting out of position with an unsuited, marginal hand is unnecessary and will often put me in awkward spots against a deep-stacked opponent. Folding is too tight. I'll call to realize equity and evaluate on the flop, controlling pot size OOP and looking to extract value when I connect or fold to heavy action when I miss.

Heads-up in the big blind with T8o on A♦–6♥–2♠. I completely missed the flop (only weak backdoor possibilities) and the preflop raiser’s range contains a lot of Aces and other hands that will either continue or comfortably call a c‑bet. Bluffing out of position with no Ace blocker and no strong draw is a poor use of stack depth — it will get called by worse hands and put me in tough spots on later streets. I’ll control the pot and keep my range wide by checking. If Flash bets I’ll fold to standard/large c‑bets (especially >~1/3–1/2 pot) but could call a very small probe to see a free turn; if he checks back I can take a free card and re‑evaluate on the turn.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the flop has come Ace-high and very dry (6h 2s Ac). This is an excellent board texture for a continuation bet, as I have a range advantage that includes more strong Aces and big pairs. Even though I only have King-high, a bet here puts a lot of pressure on Minni's non-Ace hands like pocket pairs or middle cards that may have connected with the 6 or 2 but cannot withstand much heat. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to try and take it down immediately.

I defended preflop with T♠8♥ which is fine heads‑up, but on the A♣6♥2♠ flop I missed entirely. Flash’s continuation bet (300 into the ~600 pot, a 50% c‑bet) requires ~25% equity to call (300 to win ~1200), and T8o has far less vs a BTN raising range that contains many Ax and hands that will continue. I have no pair, no clean draw (only weak backdoors), and I’m out of position against a deep stacked opponent who can apply pressure on later streets. Raising as a bluff is unattractive — I lack Ace blockers and likely won’t get folds often — so the +EV play is to fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

Heads-up preflop on the button (I’m the small blind) with Js2h. In heads-up play the opening range should be wide — J2o is playable and I have postflop position, so I want to take the initiative rather than just complete. Pot is 150 and the min-raise is to 200; I’ll raise to 300 (a standard ~3xBB total) to apply pressure, isolate the big blind, and build a pot when I can act last postflop. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, but if called use positional advantage to c-bet on dry boards (~1/3 pot) and proceed cautiously on coordinated/connected boards.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Four offsuit. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. Although the pot odds are 2:1 (calling 200 to win 400), Queen-Four offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive opponent. Given my significant stack lead, I see no reason to play a marginal hand and would rather wait for a better spot.

In Hand #61, I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Qs9d. This is a solid heads-up hand to open. Given the current stack depths (I have 25,950 and Minni has 13,900), I want to maintain pressure. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to seize the initiative pre-flop.

Facing a standard 3x open to 300 from the SB, I have KcTs in the big blind. Pot is 400; I must call 200 more (300 total) which gives me ~33% pot odds to see a flop. KTs is a very playable heads-up hand with good equity and postflop playability against a wide SB opening range (top-pair potential, broadway/straight possibilities). I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a small, low-fold-equity 3-bet to 500 — that line creates awkward postflop decisions versus an aggressive opponent and isn’t necessary with this hand and stack depth. Calling keeps my range wide, uses the pot/implied odds, and lets me evaluate the flop before committing more chips. I will call to 300 total and play the flop cautiously out of position.

I missed this flop with KcTs (board 5c 5h Js). I’m out of position vs the SB opener and he checked to me — which can be either weakness or a slowplay, but I don’t have a hand that wants to build a big pot OOP. I only have 6 realistic outs to improve to a pair by river (~24% to pair a K/T by river) and no realistic nut blocker to represent a strong hand. Betting as a bluff here is thin: many hands in his check-back range (Jx, small pairs, A-high) will either call or have showdown value, and if he raises I’m forced into an awkward fold or huge commitment with very little equity. I’ll control the pot and realize equity — check and reevaluate based on his turn action (call small bets, fold to large aggression, and pursue value if I pair/up).

I raised pre-flop with Qs9d and Minni called. The flop is 5c-5h-Js, which is quite dry and favors my pre-flop range as the aggressor, as I have more Jack-X and big pairs. Minni checked, which is expected. I will fire a continuation bet to represent a Jack or simply to take down the pot immediately. A half-pot bet of 300 is standard here.

Flash (SB) made a small c-bet (300 into a 900 pot) on 5♣-5♥-J♠. I hold K♣T♠ — no meaningful draws but 6 clean outs (three Ks and three Ts) to pair by the river (~24% to improve). Calling 300 into the 1200 pot gives ~20% required equity, so this is a marginal +EV call versus a wide SB continuation range. Raising OOP with a marginal hand is unnecessary and folding would surrender good pot odds. Plan: call 300 to realize equity and keep the pot manageable; if I hit a pair I will seek value, otherwise fold to significant turn aggression.

Check. I hold K♣T♠ on 5♣–5♥–J♠–4♥: no pair and no meaningful draw (only 6 clean outs to pair by the river, ≈13% from here). I already called a small flop c-bet to realize equity; out of position on the turn I don’t want to fire a bluff that will be called by many hands with showdown value (A‑high, small pairs, Jx) or get raised by a made hand. Flash’s line (preflop open, small flop c-bet, now passive on the turn) looks like either weakness or pot control with a pair — either way betting OOP is a poor risk/reward play. Checking controls the pot, preserves fold equity on the river (he may barrel with air) and gives me a cheap river to hit a pair. Plan: if I pair a K/T on the river I will seek thin value; if I miss and he bets a small/medium river sizing I’ll call if the price and his range justify it, but I’ll fold to large river aggression with no pair.

On the turn 4h, the board remains paired (5c-5h) and generally unconnected. My Qs9d hasn't improved, and I'm currently holding Queen-high. Minni has checked again. While I could check back to realize my showdown value or induce a bluff on the river, firing a second barrel might force Minni to fold weak pairs (like small pocket pairs or lower Ace-highs) that might have called the flop. However, given the dry texture and the fact that I have some showdown value against her air, checking back is a safe way to control the pot size and see a river. Actually, since Minni is relatively passive in these spots unless she has a piece, a second barrel of 600 (half pot) could effectively end the hand now if she's just floating with overcards.

Board: 5♣-5♥-J♠-4♥. I hold K♣T♠ — no pair and only 6 clean outs to hit a pair on the river (~6/47 ≈ 12.8% equity). Pot is 1800 and Flash bet 600; calling 600 to win 2400 requires ≈25% equity (600/(1800+600)), so my raw equity is well below the break-even threshold. Flash’s line (preflop open, small flop c-bet, now a turn bet) is consistent with many value hands (Jx, small-to-mid pairs), so a call is a long-term losing play. Raising as a bluff OOP with K-high lacks good blockers and is unnecessarily risky. Fold to this turn bet and preserve stack for better, higher-expected-value spots.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should be opening a wide range. Ad5d is a suited ace — strong enough to raise for position and postflop playability. I want fold equity and to avoid giving Flash a cheap look at the flop, so I’ll open to a standard 3xBB size (300 total). This size keeps the pot manageable with my 13,350 stack and preserves flexibility if Flash 3-bets (I can fold, call or shove depending on sizing/reads). Limping or just calling is too passive in this spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs 7s, a suited queen that plays reasonably well in a heads-up pot. Minni has raised to 300 from the small blind/button. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 400, providing 3:1 odds) and the playability of suited hands in position or as the defender in heads-up play, I will call to see a flop. Minni's stack is shorter than mine, and I want to see if I can connect with the board or outplay them post-flop.

I have flopped trips (three of a kind) with Queens. The board is very dry (Qd-Qc-2h), meaning there are few draws available for my opponent. Checking is the standard play here to allow the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) to continue with a bluff or a smaller pair. If I lead out, I likely fold out all of her air. I want to keep her range as wide as possible and potentially trap her into betting.

Heads-up as the preflop aggressor I should continuation-bet frequently on this dry, paired Q-Q-2 board. My Ad5d has little showdown value but blocks some of the villain's strongest AQ-type combos and has a tiny backdoor diamond equity. A small c-bet (~1/3 pot) maximizes fold equity versus Flash's wide calling range (Kx, Jx, small pairs, some missed suited connectors) while keeping the pot manageable if I'm called. Plan: bet 200 to take it down now; if I face a check-raise or significant aggression I’ll fold (my hand is too weak), if called I’ll re-evaluate the turn and proceed cautiously.

I have flopped trips with Queens on an extremely dry board (Q-Q-2). Minni has made a small continuation bet of 1/3 pot. If I raise now, I risk folding out all of her air and weaker hands. By calling, I keep her range wide, allowing her to continue bluffing on future streets or to value bet a pocket pair that I currently dominate. I will slow play this hand to maximize value.

I have trips with Queens on a very dry board. The King on the turn is a potential scare card that could either have helped Minni or might encourage her to continue bluffing if she thinks I'm weak. In heads-up play, as the first to act post-flop, checking is the best way to continue slow-playing this hand and induce a bet from her. I want to keep her range wide and let her commit more chips to the pot with either air or a weaker pair.

The K on the turn significantly reduces my equity — many hands that called my small flop c‑bet (Kx, KQ, Qx, sets, mid pairs) now beat my Ace-high. Flash’s check is ambiguous, but from the hand history he’s a sticky caller and will often continue with medium strength hands rather than fold to a turn bluff. Betting here would be a thin bluff with weak fold equity (I block very few bluffs) and a thin value bet against worse hands. With a marginal hand and a single-card improvement left, I should control the pot and preserve my stack. Check and see a river: if Flash fires a large bet I can fold; if he checks back I keep the pot small and can show down my Ace-high or take a stab on a safe river.

The board has completed as 2h-Qd-Qc-Ks-3h. I have trips with a seven kicker. The turn went check-check, which might indicate Minni has given up on a bluff or is checking back a medium-strength hand like a small pair or a King. By checking the river, I am once again trying to induce a bluff. If she has a King, she might value bet it, allowing me to raise. If she has nothing, she might try to steal the pot since the turn was checked through. Given there is only 1000 in the pot, leading out might just fold out her air, whereas checking gives her one last chance to bet. If she checks behind, I win a small pot. If she bets, I will likely raise for value.

I opened, c‑bet the paired Q/Q/2 flop and was called. That call on a paired flop is weighted toward Qx, Kx and medium pairs — many of which improve on the K turn (Kx becomes K+Q two‑pair; Qx becomes trips/full house). By the river (Q Q 2 K 3) my Ad5d only has the board pair with the best kicker (Q Q A K 5) and is behind most of Flash’s calling range. Flash’s passive check‑back on the turn and river makes thin bluffs unlikely and his sticky calling tendency means a river bluff will have very little fold equity; a value bet is also thin because almost every hand that calls will beat me. Pot control is the highest EV line: check and show down (fold to a large river bet).

I am on the button with A9o, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up game. I will raise to take control of the pot and build value. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here.

Facing a standard heads‑up open to 300 from the button. I have Tc6s — marginal but playable heads‑up. Calling the extra 200 (300 total) gives me ~33% pot odds (pot ~600 after my call) and reasonable equity vs a wide SB opening range. I’ll avoid 3‑betting OOP with an offsuit marginal hand against a deep, aggressive opponent; call to see a flop and play post‑flop selectively: continue with top pair/strong draws, use pot control when behind, and fold to heavy aggression on dry, high‑card boards.

Heads‑up vs a wide SB open. On 10♦‑8♠‑7♥ I have top pair (T♣) with a weak kicker (6♠). Against Flash’s opening range there are many worse tens, middle pairs (8s/7s) and loads of single‑card straight draws (9x, Jx, 6x) that I want to charge — checking gives free cards and lets draws see turn/river cheaply. I’ll bet for value and protection but keep the size modest since my kicker is weak and he’s deep/aggressive. Half‑pot (300) gets called by worse hands and denies equity to many draws; if he raises large I’ll assume a strong made hand and fold or re‑evaluate based on sizing/read. If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously OOP on the turn.

On this flop of 8s-Td-7h, I have Ace-high and an open-ended straight draw with my nine (needing a 6 or a Jack). Minni has led out with a 300 bet into a 600 pot. This is a coordinated board that likely connects well with her big blind defending range. However, with my nut flush potential (if the board were different, but here just high card) and an open-ended straight draw, I have significant equity. I will call the 300 to see a turn card, as I am getting 3:1 odds and have 8 outs to a straight plus two overcards that might be good if I hit an Ace.

I have T♣6♠ on 8♠-T♦-7♥-3♣ — top pair with a weak kicker. I c-bet the flop and was called; the 3♣ on the turn is a harmless card that doesn’t complete many of the villain’s hands. Flash called the flop, so his range contains lots of worse tens, middle pairs (8s/7s) and single-card gutshots (9x) — all hands I can get value from. I want to deny remaining equity and extract value while keeping the pot manageable OOP, so I’ll bet for value and protection. A half‑pot sized bet (600 into a 1200 pot) is large enough to charge draws and worse pairs/tens but small enough that I’m not over-committing with a weak kicker. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river; if he raises substantially or shoves I’ll heavily discount my hand and fold unless the sizing/reads indicate a bluff.

On the turn card 3c, the board texture remains very coordinated (8s-Td-7h-3c). Minni has led out again, this time for 600 into a 1200 pot. I still hold Ace-high and an open-ended straight draw (any 6 or Jack gives me a straight). My overcard Ace might also be live if she is betting with a lower pair or a draw. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call. Given my equity with the open-ended straight draw (8 outs) and the possibility that an Ace could be the best hand, I have enough pot equity to continue. I will call the 600 to see the river.

The river pairs the Ten giving me trips (Tc + Td + Ts). Flash has been calling my modest c-bet on the flop and half‑pot bet on the turn, so his continuing range is wide: worse tens, single‑pair 8s/7s, missed 9x/Jx draws and some small pairs. Hands that beat me (full houses/quads) are possible but relatively rare (pocket 8s/7s/3s or Th) and many of those would have played differently or would likely raise. Value-bet: I want to extract from worse tens and one-pair hands while avoiding over‑committing to a very unlikely full house. Betting 1500 into 2400 (~62% pot) achieves that — big enough to get calls from worse hands, small enough that a large raise would credibly indicate a full house/quads (in which case I’ll heavily discount my hand and fold to substantial shove/raise).

On a board of 8s-Td-7h-3c-Ts, I hold Ace-high (Ac 9d). Minni has led into me on every street (flop, turn, and river). This 'donk' betting line typically indicates a strong made hand or a strong draw that has now completed. The board is very coordinated; straights like J9 and 96 were possible from the flop, and any Ten now has trips on the river. My Ace-high is merely a bluff-catcher. Given Minni's persistent aggression across three streets on a texture that favors a big blind defending range, it is highly unlikely that Ace-high is the best hand. I am getting approximately 2.6:1 on a call, but my hand has no showdown value against her value range, and she has shown enough strength that a fold is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button with J♣5♥. Pot is 150 and I only need to add 50 to see a flop — very cheap with position and a wide heads-up opening range. Flash in the BB has shown aggression, so raising could get me 3-bet and put me in a tough spot with a marginal hand. I’ll call to use my positional advantage, see a flop cheaply, and then proceed based on the texture (c-bet small on dry boards, value/raise when I pair J or hit a strong draw, fold to heavy aggression on dangerous turns).

I am in the Big Blind with a weak hand (Tc 5s). Minni has limped from the Small Blind. In this situation, there is no reason to raise and bloat the pot with a mediocre hand that will likely be out of position throughout the rest of the hand (the SB acts last post-flop in heads-up play). Checking allows me to see a flop for free.

On a board of Kc-Th-7c, I have middle pair (tens) with a weak kicker (5s). The pot is small (200 chips) because Minni limped and I checked. Minni, being in the Small Blind, acts first on every street post-flop. Since Minni checked, I will check as well. While I have a pair, the board is fairly coordinated with straight and flush draws, and a King is over my pair. Checking back keeps the pot small and allows me to see how the turn develops without committing more chips with a marginal hand.

I called preflop with a very wide heads-up range. The flop K♣ T♥ 7♣ is relatively dry and checked by the big blind — a good spot to apply pressure. I have J♣5♥: no pair and only a weak backdoor club possibility, so I’m not betting for value but for fold equity and range denial. A half‑pot c‑bet (100 into a 200 pot) credibly represents a King/Ten or a strong club and will fold many unpaired hands, while keeping the pot controllable if called. Plan: fold to a significant check‑raise (very little showdown equity), call a single call and re‑evaluate the turn — barrel on favorable cards (J/5/club) or slow down on blanks.

On a board of Kc-Th-7c, I have second pair with a 10 and a weak 5 kicker. Minni limped pre-flop and has now bet 100 into a 200-chip pot. With 3:1 pot odds, folding second pair would be much too tight. I am currently behind any King, but I am ahead of most of her draws and any lower pairs. Calling is the best option to keep the pot small while protecting my equity and seeing how the turn develops.

I have second pair (Tens) with a weak kicker. The board is getting more coordinated with the 8s, potentially completing some straight draws like 9-6 or giving equity to J-9. Since I am out of position and have a hand with moderate showdown value but limited strength, checking is the best play to control the pot size and see how the opponent reacts. Betting would likely only get called by better hands and force out weaker ones that I currently beat.

Turn (8♠) is a blank for J♣5♥: my backdoor club draw is dead and I only have a 4‑out (any 9) straight draw (~8.7% to hit). I c‑bet small on the flop and was called, so Flash likely has at least a piece (K/T/7, a pocket pair or a draw). With very little equity and the turn not improving my range, I want pot control and to use position to see a river. Checking keeps the pot small, avoids committing chips with a weak draw, and lets me fold to strong aggression or barrel for value/bluff on a favorable river (9 or a running bluff card). Therefore: check.

I have rivered two pair (Tens and Fives) on a board of Kc-Th-7c-8s-5d. Minni limped pre-flop and bet the flop, but her check on the turn suggests she likely doesn't have a King. My two pair is a very strong hand in a limped pot, and I should bet for value. I'm targeting hands like a Ten, an Eight, or a Seven that might call a final bet. I will bet 300 into the 400 pot to maximize value while still being a size that can be called by second-best hands.

I have J♣5♥ and the river 5♦ gives me bottom pair. Villain check‑called my small flop c‑bet, both of us checked the turn, and now they bet a small 300 into a 700 pot. Calling 300 to win a 1000 pot requires ~30% equity. My hand beats bluffs and worse pair combos (22–44, some missed connectors) and has reasonable showdown value heads‑up. Raising is thin and will mostly get called by better (Kx/T x/7x) or fold only hands I already beat; folding surrenders too much for a small price. The call is cheap relative to my 13,900 stack, so I’ll call to realize equity and see their showdown range.

I am on the button with A2o. In a heads-up situation, an Ace is a strong starting hand. I will raise to take control of the pot and apply pressure on Minni. A 3x raise to 300 is standard and effective here.

QQ in the big blind vs a 300 open heads-up is a clear 3-bet spot. I want to take the initiative, build the pot with a hand that dominates the opener's range, and charge Ax, Kx, and medium pairs. A raise to 1000 total (~3.3x the open) is a balanced size — big enough to extract value and deny cheap equity to speculative hands, but not so large as to fold out many worse hands. Plan: if called I'll play aggressively on most flops and extract value; if Flash 4-bets big I'll re-evaluate based on sizing (likely call smaller, fold to extreme over-shoves). Calling/passive play here would be too weak, so I raise to 1000.

I started with an open raise to 300 with Ace-Deuce offsuit, which is a standard play on the button in heads-up. Minni responded with a 3-bet to 1000. While I have position post-flop, A2o is easily dominated by a typical 3-betting range (better Aces, pairs, and high Broadway cards). In previous hands, Minni has shown aggression and been willing to follow through on multiple streets. Calling 700 more with the weakest possible Ace kicker is likely to lead to difficult post-flop situations where I am either far behind or forced to fold. I will preserve my stack and find a better spot to engage.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with J♣5♥. Deep stacks and positional advantage postflop make this a hand worth playing aggressively rather than limping/calling. Flash in the BB will defend widely, so opening to ~3BB is standard to seize the initiative, get fold equity, and let me play position on most flops. I’ll raise to 300 total (consistent with my usual open sizing). Postflop I’ll continuation-bet favorable textures, control pot size with marginal boards, and fold to large 3-bets unless I improve.

I have KTs in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. KTs is a strong premium hand, especially in a heads-up situation, and definitely worth continuing. While a 3-bet is an option to take control, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see a flop with a hand that has good playability and realization of equity. Given the stack depths and position, calling here is a standard and solid play.

On this 6c 9h 7h board, I have King-high with overcards and a backdoor straight draw. This board texture is quite connected and generally favors the caller's range (mine), as I have more 8x, 5x, and two-pair combinations. I will check to the raiser to see how they react. If they bet, I can evaluate whether to call based on the bet size and my backdoor equity.

Heads-up after my standard button open; Flash called and checked the flop 6c 9h 7h. My Jc5h has almost no showdown value but does carry a gutshot to an 8 (4 outs) and a one-card heart backdoor. This is a textbook continuation-bet spot — my preflop raising range hits this board reasonably often and Flash will check with a lot of marginal hands. I want to seize the initiative and extract folds from unpaired Broadway hands and small pairs while keeping the pot controllable with a marginal holding. Betting ~50% pot gives solid fold equity without bloating the pot; if I get a big raise I’ll fold this hand as planned, but if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed only with improvement or clear equity.

On a board of 6c 9h 7h, I have overcards (King and Ten of spades) and a backdoor straight draw. This board is quite dynamic and hits a lot of a defending range. Minni's 300 bet into a 600 pot (half-pot) is a standard continuation bet. I have two overcards and potential for various runouts. Given the pot odds (calling 300 to win a total pot of 1200) and the fact that my King-high might still be the best hand or can easily improve to a winner, I will call to see the turn.

On this highly connected turn board of 6c-9h-7h-4d, I have King-high with a Ten-high straight draw (needing an 8). This board favors a range that contains many middle connectors. As I am out of position and have no made hand yet, checking is the most appropriate action. I want to see how Minni reacts and potentially see a free river or evaluate a bet based on the sizing.

I have J♣5♥ on 6♣ 9♥ 7♥ 4♦. My flop c‑bet succeeded but Flash just called, showing a wide but mostly made/pairing/call range. The turn (4♦) doesn’t improve me — my only clean outs are the four 8s for a straight (~8–9% on the river); the backdoor heart flush is effectively gone. Pot is 1,200 and I face no bet to act. Betting now would bloat a pot where I have very little equity and will be called by many hands that beat me; if raised I’d be forced to fold. I want to control the pot and realize my small equity, so checking is optimal. Plan: check and see the river; if an 8 hits I can value bet, otherwise avoid bloating the pot or bluffing into a caller who showed weakness by calling the flop.

The board is 6c-9h-7h-4d-Td. I have top pair with a King kicker (Tens). Minni raised pre-flop, bet half-pot on the flop, and then checked behind on the turn. The river Ten improved my hand to top pair. Since Minni checked the turn after being the pre-flop aggressor and flop bettor, it's likely they are either pot controlling with a marginal made hand (like an 8 or a pair of 7s/6s) or have given up with air. Betting now is a good value play to get called by middle pairs or a stubborn Ace-high. I will bet 800 into the 1200 pot to extract value.

Flash’s line (call preflop, call flop, check turn, then lead the river) is strongly weighted toward made hands or thin value (9x/7x/6x, tens, sets) and some missed-draw bluffs. Pot is 2,000 and I must call 800, so I need ~40% equity to justify a call. With J♣5♥ I have no pair and essentially no realistic showdown equity vs Flash’s calling/river-betting range — I only beat rare pure bluffs. Raising as a bluff is too polar and would risk unnecessary chips with no fold equity against many hands that beat me. Fold and preserve stack for better spots.

In this heads-up situation, I am on the button with Q5o. While not a premium hand, in a heads-up game, being on the button with any Queen is typically strong enough to open or call. The pot is small, and I only need to call 50 more to see a flop. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and take control of the hand, which has been my standard opening size.

I’m in the BB with 7♥4♠ facing a 300 open from the SB (pot 400, I must call 200). Calling requires 200 more (300 total) and gives ~33% break-even equity. In heads-up I defend fairly wide — 7‑4o is marginal but has reasonable equity versus a wide SB opening range. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to 3‑bet a weak hand (too bluffy deep-stacked and vulnerable to 4-bets). I’ll call to see a flop, plan to check and fold to meaningful aggression if I miss, and only continue aggressively if I pair or hit strong draws.

I have 7♥4♠ on A♦4♦6♠ — I’ve paired the board (second pair, weak kicker) and I’m out of position vs the SB who opened wide preflop. Flash’s range contains a lot of A‑x and many hands that dominate or have good equity vs my 4s, and stacks are deep (pot 600 vs ~13k effective). Betting out OOP with a marginal pair invites check‑raises and bloats a pot I don’t want to play big without a stronger hand. I’ll check to control the pot, allow Bluffs/weak c‑bets to fold or give me cheap showdown, and call reasonable-sized continuation bets to realize equity (while folding to large aggression that strongly represents an Ace/two pair). If the turn improves me (7 or a 4) I’ll become more aggressive for value; otherwise I keep pot small and re-evaluate on the turn.

I raised pre-flop with Qh5c and was called by Minni. The flop is Ad-4d-6s, which is not a favorable board for my hand as it is very Ace-heavy and contains two diamonds. Minni has checked to me. While a continuation bet is standard in many spots, this board hits a flatting range from the big blind quite well (Aces, draws like 57, 35, or diamonds). Since I have almost no equity here, checking back to see a free turn card is a prudent play to keep the pot small and avoid being check-raised.

I hold 7♥4♠ on A♦4♦6♠2♣ — a marginal made hand (pair of 4s) with a weak kicker. Flash opened wide preflop and has position but has checked flop and turn, showing weakness or pot-control. Out of position and deep-stacked, I don’t want to bloat the pot with a vulnerable second pair that loses to any A‑x, many pocket pairs, and possible sets. Checking keeps the pot small, realizes equity against missed hands, and lets me fold to strong river aggression. Plan: check to the river; call small river probes (roughly ≤1/3 pot) for pot odds, fold to large bets/raises, and become more aggressive only if I improve (7 or 4) or get a clear bluff-catching spot.

On the turn, the 2nd check from Minni suggests she is likely weak or has a marginal hand. The board (Ad-4d-6s-2c) is somewhat coordinated, but my 5 blocks some straight possibilities. After checking back the flop, I've kept the pot small. By betting now, I can represent a slow-played Ace or a middle pair that checked the flop for pot control. A bet of 400 into a 600 pot should be enough to fold out her air and weak draws.

I have 7♥4♠ — second pair (4s) with a weak kicker on A♦4♦6♠2♣. Villain (SB) opened wide preflop, checked flop and turn (showing pot-control or weakness) and now fires a small probe: 400 into a 1000 pot. That sizing gives me excellent pot odds — call 400 to win ~1800 (≈22% break-even). Against his wide checking range my 4s has decent showdown value vs missed hands and bluffs. Raising OOP with a vulnerable second pair is unnecessary and will bloat the pot against many better hands (A‑x, 6x, sets); folding here surrenders too much equity for a small price. I’ll call the 400, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate on the river (call small river bets for pot odds, fold to large aggression unless I improve).

I hold 7♥4♠ on A♦4♦6♠2♣2♥ — that’s two pair (4s and 2s). Flash opened wide preflop, checked the flop and then made a small turn stab (400) that I called. His river check is a pot‑control line that can represent medium A‑x, a missed hand, or a small pair. Crucially, many common hands in his raising range (A‑x, 6x, pocket 4s/6s, even some sets) beat my two pair, while worse hands (55–TT, Kx, Qx) will often just check back. Betting here would mostly get called by hands that already beat me and fold the hands I beat, so it’s thin or negative EV. I’ll check to take it to showdown and only call a reasonable river bet — fold to large raises — rather than bloating the pot out of position.

The board is Ad-4d-6s-2c-2h. I have Queen-high (Q5o), which has no showdown value. I raised pre-flop, checked the flop, and bet the turn for 400, which Minni called. Minni checking the river suggests she doesn't have a strong Ace or a monster, but she likely has a marginal pair (like a 4, 6, or a small pocket pair) or an Ace that she's playing cautiously. Since I have no showdown value, my only way to win the pot is to bluff. However, after she called the turn, she's shown interest in the hand. A river bet would need to be large to fold out a weak pair on this board, and given our history, she might be sticky. I will check and concede the pot to avoid losing more chips on a pure bluff with zero equity.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 5♥3♣ — a weak, unsuited hand but the button should open very wide. Pot is only 150 and it costs me 50 more to just call; I prefer to take the initiative and apply pressure from position. A standard 2x open to 200 (total) is the right play: it preserves fold equity, keeps my raising range wide, and lets me play the flop in position.
