If Flash 3-bets large I'll fold this hand; if he calls I'll proceed cautiously post-flop and use position to control the pot size.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit (K3o). Minni, the Button (SB), has raised to 200, which is a min-raise (making it 100 more for me to call into a pot of 300). K3o is a marginal defending hand, but given the pot odds (3:1) and the fact that we are playing heads-up, calling to see a flop is a standard play. Minni has been active and has a shorter stack than me, so I want to avoid bloated pots unless I hit the board well, but I will defend my blind here.

I am in the Big Blind with K3o (King of Clubs, 3 of Spades). The flop is Ac-4c-Qc, giving me the King-high flush draw (second nut flush draw) on a very coordinated board. Minni, the SB, raised pre-flop. In this scenario, I have significant equity with the flush draw, but as the Big Blind, I should play most of my range as a check to the pre-flop raiser. If Minni bets, I will certainly continue. If she checks behind, I may be able to see a free turn or potentially bluff later if the flush completes or if I turn a pair. For now, checking is the standard play on such a board.

Flop is Ac 4c Qc; I hold 5♥3♣ — a four-flush with 9 outs (~19% to hit on the turn, ~35% by the river). Heads-up as the preflop aggressor, this is a good semi-bluff spot: I have substantial equity and position and Flash checked, so I should use fold equity and charge worse hands/draws. A half-pot bet gives fold equity vs missed broadways/small pairs and keeps the pot controllable if called. If he raises big I can fold my weak showdown value; if called I can continue with decent equity and re-evaluate on the turn (value-bet if I hit, check/control if I miss unless opponent shows weakness). Bet ~½ pot to apply pressure.

I have the King of Clubs, which gives me the nut flush draw on this Ac-4c-Qc monotone board (since the Ace and Queen of clubs are already on the board). Minni has bet 200 into a 400 pot. With 3:1 pot odds and the best possible draw, calling is a very high-equity play. I will call to see the turn and see if I can complete my flush or if the board texture changes in my favor.

On this monotone board (Ac 4c Qc), I am holding the King of Clubs, which represents the nut flush draw since the Ace and Queen are already on the board. The Nine of Hearts on the turn is a blank that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Minni has checked behind on the turn, which often indicates a medium-strength hand (like a weak Ace or a Queen) that is afraid of the flush or is looking to control the pot size. By checking, I am keeping the pot small while maintaining my equity to hit the nut flush on the river. I will check and see if I can improve or potentially take the pot down if a club arrives or if I can represent a hand on certain river cards.

I have 5♥3♣ on Ac4♣Qc9♥ — a 4-card club with 9 outs (~9/46 ≈ 19.6% to hit on the river). I led the flop as a semi-bluff and got called; the turn is a blank and Flash checks. Betting the turn here would be thin: with only ~20% to improve I’d be building the pot vs many hands (A/Q/pairs) that will call and deny me fold equity. Better to control the pot and realize my equity for free — check the turn, see the river, and then either value-bet big if I make my flush or fold/counter-bluff on the river depending on the card and Flash’s action. Preserving stack and position advantage here is the higher EV line.

On the river, the board is Ac-4c-Qc-9h-Js. I have missed my nut flush draw and am left with King-high. The turn went check-check, which often indicates that Minni has a marginal made hand like a Queen or a weak Ace that she is happy to take to showdown. Since I have almost no showdown value but also block the King of clubs (a key card for her own potential bluffs or nut draws), and given the pot is relatively small (800 chips), I will check. If she bets, I will likely fold as it's hard to represent a strong hand here after checking the turn, and she likely has a pair that beats King-high.

I have 5♥3♣ on Ac4cQc9hJs — I missed my flush and have no showdown value. Flash checked the turn and river after calling my flop bet, which indicates weakness (single-pair hands, missed draws, or medium-strength holdings) rather than a value-heavy, slow-played monster. Betting gives me a chance to take down the 800-chip pot without a showdown. A half-pot river bluff (400) is the right balance: it credibly represents a club or a made hand (Jack/paired river) while keeping risk manageable if called. Break-even fold frequency for a 400 bet is ~33% — I expect to fold out enough Ax/Qx and small pairs on this runout to make this profitable. I avoid an overbet to not bloat the pot unnecessarily given my marginal equity if called.

On the river board of Ac-4c-Qc-9h-Js, I have missed my nut flush draw and am left with King-high. Minni bet 400 into a pot of 800 after the turn went check-check. While I hold the King of clubs, which is a significant blocker to the nut flush, my hand has zero showdown value. Minni's line (betting flop, checking turn, and betting river) often represents a marginal made hand like a Queen or a weak Ace that is looking for thin value or protecting against a missed draw. Since I cannot win at showdown and a bluff-raise here is risky given the small pot and the likelihood that she has at least a pair, the most disciplined action is to fold.

I am on the button with Q4o. While Q4o is a weak hand, in a heads-up situation against Minni, I should be playing a wide range from the button to maintain pressure. However, Minni has been quite active and has a shorter stack (143 BBs compared to my 255 BBs). Looking at recent hands, Q4o doesn't play very well post-flop and I have folded similar hands (Hand #49). Given the current stack leads and the relative weakness of the hand, folding is a safe, conservative play, but standard heads-up strategy often dictates raising or folding. Since Q4o is at the very bottom of an opening range, I will fold to avoid unnecessary marginal spots.

Heads-up on the button with A♦8♦ — a strong, positionally advantaged hand. I want initiative and fold equity against Flash’s wide big‑blind defending range, plus the nut‑flush potential makes this a hand I can play aggressively. With deep stacks and a small current pot (150) a standard 3xBB open is appropriate: it builds a pot I can play in position while not bloating it unnecessarily. Plan: c‑bet most flops for value and folds; call small 3‑bets to realize equity because of the suited Ace; fold to large, committed 3‑bets that price me poorly out of position. Raising to 300 balances value and protection.

I am in the big blind with 7d 4d. Minni has raised to 300 from the button/small blind. While 74s is not a strong hand, at these stack depths and in a heads-up situation, it's a reasonable hand to defend against a standard raise, especially given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot that will be 600). I will call and see a flop.

On a flop of 5d-Qh-4h, I have bottom pair (fours) with a back-door diamond flush draw. Minni raised pre-flop and is the aggressor. Checking to the raiser is the standard play here. If she bets, I have enough equity and a pair to potentially call one street, depending on the size.

I raised preflop to 300 and Flash called from the big blind. Flop is 5♦ Q♥ 4♥ and Flash checked. I hold A♦8♦ — the nut diamond flush draw plus an Ace-high. In position with a very strong draw and fold equity against a wide BB calling range, I should take the initiative and c-bet. Betting ~2/3 pot (400 into the 600 pot) accomplishes three things: it folds out many unpaired hands and weaker overcards, charges heart/backdoor draws, and builds the pot when I have the nut-flush outs (and an Ace to pair). If called I can continue on many favorable turns; if I face a large raise I will re-evaluate given my strong drawing equity. Therefore I will bet 400 to apply pressure and extract value.

I have bottom pair (fours) on a board of Qd-5d-4h. Minni has bet 400 into a 600 pot. While bottom pair is a weak holding, I have several ways to improve: another four for trips, a seven for two pair, or a diamond for a backdoor flush draw. In heads-up play, bottom pair is often worth at least one call to a single continuation bet, especially on a board with several draws (hearts, straight draws) where the opponent might be betting with air or a draw. I will call the 400 and evaluate on the turn.

On the turn, the King of Clubs is an overcard that likely favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (containing hands like AK, KQ, KJ). My bottom pair of fours is now quite vulnerable. Checking is the most appropriate move to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts. If she bets again, I will have to carefully consider whether to continue based on the bet sizing and my remaining equity.

Board is 5♦ Q♥ 4♥ K♣; I hold A♦8♦. After the turn the backdoor diamond flush is dead (I would have needed diamond on both turn and river), so I’m left with only Ace‑high — some showdown value heads‑up but no real draw. Flash called my flop c‑bet, then checked the turn; that line fits a wide calling range (Qx, small pairs, heart draws, some Kx) and also hands that prefer pot control. Betting the turn would be a thin bluff/value attempt that risks getting raised by hands that already beat me (Kx, Qx, sets) and will only fold the weakest part of his range. With position and only one card to come, I prefer pot control: check and see the river for free, then evaluate a call vs small river bets (and fold to large raises). Checking minimizes downside while preserving my ability to extract value when appropriate on the river.

On the river, the board is 5d-Qh-4h-Kc-7h and I have made two pair with 7d-4d. While two pair is a strong hand in heads-up play, the 7h completes a possible heart flush. Minni checked back on the turn, which suggests she might not have a powerhouse like a set or top-tier two pair, but she could be pot-controlling or have missed a draw. By checking, I allow Minni to bluff with her missed straight draws (like 68) or bet for value with a weaker one-pair hand that I can then call. Leading out might only get called by hands that beat me or strong one-pair hands, and it would put me in a difficult position if she raises.

Check. My diamond flush missed on the turn/river and I only have Ace‑high. Flash’s line — calling my flop c‑bet, then checking the turn and river — is consistent with lots of hands that beat me at showdown (Qx, small pairs, 5x/4x, or even a backdoor two‑heart combo that now makes a flush). A river bluff here has limited fold equity: the pot is 1,400 and I’d need opponents to fold a large fraction of his continuing range to make a bluff +EV; many of his flop‑calling hands will simply call a reasonable river bet, and a true flush will call/raise. With position and no value left, the lowest‑variance, highest long‑term EV line is to check and take showdown rather than bluff into a range that usually beats me.

I am on the button with J6o. In a heads-up situation, J6o is a marginal but playable hand to open from the small blind/button. Minni has shown a variety of post-flop play, sometimes being aggressive and sometimes passive. A standard raise here is better than just calling or folding to apply pressure and take control of the pot early.

Heads-up vs Flash (button) opened to 300. I have 5♠8♠ in the big blind — a suited connector with decent playability and good implied odds against a wide opening range. Calling 200 more (to 300 total) is an efficient defend: the pot odds are attractive and stacks are deep enough to realize equity post‑flop. A 3‑bet as a bluff is possible, but out of position with this hand I prefer to see a flop and play straightforwardly — proceed aggressively on strong flops (flush/straight/pair) and fold or control pot on dry, unfavorable boards.

I'm out of position with 5♠8♠ on A♥ Q♣ 2♦. Flash's opening range from the button contains a lot of A/Q/Kx and broadways that connect with this flop, so I haven't hit anything meaningful — only backdoor spade and very weak pair prospects. With no initiative and a dry, top‑card board, I prefer to control the pot and gather information rather than barreling OOP with air. Plan: check and see what Flash does; I will call small c‑bets (reasonable pot odds + backdoor equity) but fold to larger aggression. If Flash checks back I get a free turn to realize equity and can barrel or bluff later when the card profile favors my range (spade or low card).

On this flop (Ah-Qc-2d), the board is very heavy with high cards that likely connect with my opponent's range or at least scare off my Jack-high. I raised pre-flop with J6o and was called. After Minni checks the flop, a continuation bet is a standard play to represent the Ace or Queen and try to take down the pot immediately. However, against a player who has shown willingness to call, I should be cautious. Given I have no draw and virtually no equity if called, a small exploratory bet (1/2 pot) is appropriate to see where I stand.

Heads‑up on the button opened and I defended the big blind with 5♠8♠. Flop A♥ Q♣ 2♦ — I checked and Flash c‑bet small (300 into a 900 pot, ~1/3 pot). I have no made hand and, importantly, no realistic flush draw (there are no spades on the flop and only two cards remain), just six clean outs to pair a 5 or an 8 and some weak backdoor straight paths. However the bet is small, giving ~20% break‑even equity (300 to win ~1500). Versus a reasonably wide button c‑betting range my hand’s equity is in the ballpark of that threshold, and stacks are deep enough to realize implied odds on favourable turn cards. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me see a cheap turn to realize equity or fold to significant turn aggression, and preserves the option to bluff‑catch or peel a low card that helps my range. Plan: call this small c‑bet, re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to big raises or barring a favourable card, proceed cautiously out of position.

Out of position with 5♠8♠ on A♥ Q♣ 2♦ 3♣. I missed the turn — there are no spades on board and only six clean outs (three 5s + three 8s) to pair on the river, roughly 13% equity. Flash opened from the button and c‑bet small on the flop, so his range is wide but contains a lot of A/Q and broadway hands that currently beat me. With stacks deep and very little showdown value, I want to control the pot and avoid barreling OOP with air. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets me see a river for free if Flash checks back, and preserves the option to fold to significant turn aggression or call a very small bet to realize equity. Therefore: check.

The turn is the 3c, which is largely a brick for my J6o. I double-barrel here because my pre-flop and flop betting line strongly represents an Ace or a high Queen. Minni checked for the third time (including the pre-flop check-option). If Minni had a strong Ace or better, she likely would have check-raised the flop or led out. By betting again, I am putting pressure on her marginal hands like middle pairs (small Queens, Jacks, or Tens) or weak draws that may have called the flop. A bet of 600 (half pot) is consistent with my previous sizing and maintains the narrative that I have a strong hand.

I checked the turn as planned and now face a 600 bet into a 1800 pot. Calling 600 requires ~600/(1800+600) ≈ 25% equity. With 5♠8♠ on A♥ Q♣ 2♦ 3♣ I only have ~6 clean outs (three 5s, three 8s) — roughly ~12–13% to pair on the river — and some of those outs are weakened if Flash holds a 5 or 8. Flash’s line (small flop c‑bet then a larger turn bet) maps to a fairly strong continuing range (Ax, Qx, two‑pair/set candidates or strong broadways), so my equity and implied odds don’t justify calling. A bluff‑raise here would be out of position with poor fold equity against many hands and unnecessarily high risk. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Heads-up on the button (SB) I should be opening very wide. 2♣5♣ is marginal but suited and playable postflop, and raising here uses my positional advantage while applying pressure to the big blind. A standard min/open to 200 (2.0x BB) is a small, low-variance size that often wins the pot preflop and still lets me fold to a large 3-bet without committing too many chips. If called I can play the flop in position and realize good equity with flush/straight backdoor potential; if Flash 3-bets I can give up this hand against a likely stronger range and preserve my stack. Raise to 200.

I have Ace-King offsuit (AKo), a premium hand in any poker context, and especially strong in a heads-up game. My opponent (Minni) has opened to 200 from the small blind/button. Given the strength of my hand and the need to build a pot while ahead, I will 3-bet. A standard 3-bet size here would be around 3x to 4x the open raise. I'll raise to 700 to put pressure on and gain the initiative.

I opened wide to 200 in heads-up, but Flash 3-bet to 700. The pot is 900 and I must call 500 (pot would be 1400), so I need ~36% equity to justify a call. 2♣5♣ is too weak vs a typical 3-bet range (many pairs, broadways, suited Aces) and is often dominated; its backdoor potential and positional advantage don’t make up the gap. A light 4‑bet is possible in heads‑up play, but 2‑5 suited is a poor candidate for a 4‑bet bluff here given the stacks and sizing. Preserving a healthy stack and folding marginal spots is the higher EV play. I fold.

I am holding King-Ace of spades on the button, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. My goal is to build the pot for value while maintaining control of the betting. Based on previous hands, a standard opening raise of 3x (300 chips) is consistent with my playstyle and allows me to see how Minni reacts.

I’m in the big blind with 4♦8♦ facing a button raise to 300 (pot 400, 200 to call). 4♦8♦ is a marginal but suited, connected holding with good implied odds heads-up. Calling is cheap here — I only need ~33% equity (200/(400+200)) to break even — and the stacks are deep enough to realize value on diamond/straight flops. I avoid a 3‑bet with this weak holding because I’d be out of position postflop and don’t want to bloat the pot with a dominated hand. Plan: call, see the flop, continue aggressively on favorable boards (diamonds, low connected boards) and fold to heavy action on dry high boards.

Flop: K♣ 6♦ 3♠ with 4♦8♦. I have no pair and only a backdoor diamond (need two diamonds) plus a few pair-outs — this is a marginal hand. Flash opened preflop so their range contains lots of Kx, pairs and broadways that connect well with this board. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a weak holding, so I’ll control the pot size and see what they do. Check and call small continuation bets for pot odds/implied odds; fold to heavy pressure or large raise. If Flash checks back I get a free turn to improve or to take the pot on a safe turn card.

I have top pair with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a dry board of Kc-6d-3s. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has checked, which is expected as the out-of-position defender. I want to bet for value and to protect my hand against potential draws or lone overcards. A standard continuation bet of about half the pot (300 chips) is appropriate here to extract value and maintain initiative.

Flop: K♣ 6♦ 3♠. Flash c-bets 300 into a 900 pot. Calling costs 300 for ~25% pot odds (300/(900+300)). My 4♦8♦ has very little showdown value but reasonable cheap equity: ~6 outs to pair by the river (~24% to hit) plus a small (~4%) runner‑runner diamond backdoor, so my combined chance to improve is around the mid‑20s — roughly inline with the pot odds. Stacks are deep enough to realize implied odds if I hit. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand (raising is too ambitious; folding surrenders too much equity). Plan: call the 300 to see the turn, fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss, and proceed aggressively only if I improve or pick up significant additional equity (diamond turn or pair).

Turn (K♣ 6♦ 3♠ 5♠): my 4♦8♦ still has no pair and the backdoor diamond is gone. I only have 3suits with two pair/straight outs: 3 fours + 3 eights = 6 pair outs, plus four 2s and four 7s = 8 straight outs, ~14 outs total (~30% to improve on the river). I’m out of position and want to control the pot — I called the flop with pot-odds and implied odds, but I don’t want to bloat this pot with a marginal hand now. I’ll check to see what Flash does. If Flash bets small (roughly ≤ ~500) the pot odds make a call reasonable (B/(1200+B) ≤ my equity ≈30%), so I’ll call and see the river; if Flash bets bigger I will fold to avoid committing with only draw/one-pair chances. If Flash checks back I get a free river to try and hit or bluff-catch.

I currently hold top pair with the best kicker (Ace) and have picked up the nut flush draw on the turn (5s). The board is relatively dry, and Minni has checked to me twice, indicating weakness or a marginal made hand. I want to continue extracting value from weaker Kings, pocket pairs, or straight draws that Minni might be holding. Betting 800 into a 1200 pot (2/3 pot) is a strong value bet that builds the pot for a potential big payoff on the river while offering a poor price for any draws.

Board K♣ 6♦ 3♠ 5♠, I hold 4♦8♦. Flash opened/CB’d and now barrels a sizable 800 into a 2000 pot. To call I need ~28.6% equity (800/(2000+800)). I have 14 raw outs to improve by the river (3 fours + 3 eights + 4 twos + 4 sevens = 14 → 14/46 ≈ 30.4%), but that raw number is misleading: many of those outs (pairing a 4 or 8) will still lose if Flash holds a K or another made hand. Against a K‑x/value heavy line my only clean outs are the 2s and 7s (8/46 ≈ 17.4%), well below the pot odds. Raising from my weak, unpaired, out‑of‑position holding is too ambitious and committing; calling is borderline and likely negative EV given reverse implied odds (if I hit a low pair I can still be second‑best). Flash’s line looks value‑oriented/polarized. Stick to plan: don’t bloat the pot with a marginal hand — fold and wait for a better spot with position or real equity.

Heads-up on the button with AdJs is a strong, positionally advantaged hand — open-raising is standard. I want to take the initiative, build a pot with a hand that plays well postflop in position, and apply pressure to the big blind's wide defending range. A 3xBB open (to 300 total) is a balanced size: it's above the minimum, extracts value from worse Axs/Kx/broadways and gains fold equity against marginal hands, while leaving plenty of stack behind to maneuver if Flash 3-bets. (Raising to 300 total means I’ve posted 50 already, so I add 250.)

I am in the Big Blind with Jd Tc, a solid broadway hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. My hand is definitely strong enough to defend, and given the deep stacks and heads-up dynamic, JTo is well within the defending range. I will call to see a flop and realize my equity.

I have flopped top pair with a Jack kicker on a relatively disconnected board, though there is a spade flush draw. Being out of position as the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their reaction. My hand is strong enough to call a continuation bet, but checking allows me to control the pot size and observe Minni's betting pattern.

I opened to 300 preflop with A♦J♠ and Flashes called from the BB — heads‑up he has a very wide defending range. Flop T♦ 6♠ 3♠ is fairly dry and he checked, so as the preflop aggressor in position this is a textbook continuation‑bet spot. My hand has two overcards and a spade blocker (reduces some strong spade combos), so I have fold equity against Kx/Qx/missed connectors and can realize when I pair an A or J. I want a size that folds out a big portion of his missed range but leaves room to maneuver on the turn — ~350 into a 600 pot (~55–60% pot) accomplishes that. Plan: bet to take it down now; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (bet A/J for value, slow down on blanks); if he check‑raises big I’ll give weighted folds unless I pair on the turn.

I have flopped top pair with a Jack kicker. This is a strong hand in a heads-up pot, but not necessarily a monster. Minni's 350 bet into 600 is a standard continuation bet. If I raise here, I risk folding out her bluffs while only being called by hands that have me beat (like AT, KT, or sets) or strong draws. By calling, I keep her range wide, allow her to continue betting with air on the turn, and keep the pot manageable. I will call and see how the turn develops.

On the turn, the 5 of spades completes the flush draw. Since I am out of position with top pair, the most prudent play is to check. This allows me to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts to the flush completing. Leading out here would likely only be called by better hands (flushes, sets, or better Tens) and would force out her bluffs. By checking, I give her the opportunity to continue betting with a wider range, which I can then re-evaluate based on her bet size.

Board is T♦-6♠-3♠-5♠ and I hold A♦J♠. I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the flop; Flash flat‑called. Turn brings a third spade — important, but note: to have a made flush on the turn the villain must hold two spades (both hole cards spades), which is a relatively small subset of his calling range. His check is consistent with single‑spade hands, one‑pair hands (T or small pairs), weak broadways, and some draws. I want to (1) deny a free spade river that will give any single‑spade hand a flush, (2) charge pairs/Tx and worse Ax/Kx combos, and (3) extract value while still being able to fold to very strong turn/river lines. A sizable bet (~2/3–3/4 pot) accomplishes that. I’ll bet 900 into the 1300 pot: large enough to charge draws and get value from calls, but small enough to keep maneuvering if called. If Flash calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river (barrel blanks for value; be cautious if a spade comes); if he check‑raises massively on the turn I’ll give credit to a turned nut‑flush or set and fold unless I improve or the sizing is clearly light.

On the turn, the 5 of spades completes the spade flush. I have top pair (Tens) with a Jack kicker. While the board has become significantly more dangerous with the flush completing, top pair is still a relatively strong hand in a heads-up pot. Minni's bet of 900 into 1300 is large (about 70% of the pot), indicating either a strong hand or a polarized bluff. Folding here would be too exploitable against an aggressive opponent, but raising would only invite a call or reraise from hands that beat me (flushes, sets, or better Tens). I will call the turn bet to see the river and re-evaluate based on the final board card and Minni's action.

On the river, the board has paired with a Six, giving me Tens and Sixes. However, the spade flush completed on the turn, which is a significant part of Minni's aggressive range given her 70% pot bet on that street. By checking, I am controlling the pot and allowing Minni to check back with marginal hands or attempt a bluff with her missed draws. If she bets, I will have to evaluate based on the size, but leading out myself risks running into a flush or trips while likely folding out hands I currently beat.

Flash checked the river. I have Ad Js — just ace-high; no pair. He called my flop c‑bet and turn barrel, so his range is weighted to made hands (T‑x, pocket pairs, turned/rivered 6s, or a two‑spade flush from the turn) and stubborn broadways. The river 6♦ actually helps his range (any 6 becomes trips) and materially reduces my bluff equity. Betting here would be a very thin value bet at best and is likely to get called by better hands or fold out too infrequently; it also risks a check‑raise from hands that now crush me. With pot = 3100 and both stacks deep, the prudent, +EV line is to preserve chips and take my showdown — check back and show down the ace-high.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with King-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand for heads-up play. My opponent, Minni, is in the Big Blind and has a relatively short stack compared to mine (about 107 big blinds, but I have nearly 300). I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot with a superior range.

I’m in the big blind with Ah6h facing a Button open to 300. The pot is 400 and I need to add 200 (300 total committed including my BB). Calling is the best default: A6s has good playability and flush/ace potential vs a wide button opening range, and the pot odds (≈25% break-even) make a call profitable vs a typical wide opener. With 107bb effective there’s room to maneuver postflop, but I’m OOP — so a 3-bet bluff to 500 is unattractive because my kicker is weak and a small 3-bet is unlikely to fold out enough hands while committing me to a marginal spot. I’ll call to realize equity, play the flop cautiously (bet/raise when I have top pair or strong draws, check/fold when severely out of position and facing heavy aggression on dry boards), and avoid bloating the pot with a dominated ace.

Heads-up on a dry, paired flop (8d 8h 4s). I have Ah6h — only Ace-high with a tiny runner-runner heart backdoor, no pair and I’m out of position. Betting OOP with a marginal hand here just bloats the pot and gets me raised or called by better hands (8x, 4x, overpairs, pockets) that dominate me. Villain checked preflop raiser range includes many misses, but checking controls the pot, lets me realize equity cheaply, and keeps the option to call small turns or fold to heavy aggression. Long-term / stack-preservation reasoning: avoid committing more with a marginal holding OOP and extract value only when I improve or get a clear read. I check.

On a paired flop of 8-8-4, I have King-high. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage. This board is quite dry, and it's unlikely Minni connected strongly, though she can have some 8s in her big blind defending range. A continuation bet here often takes the pot down immediately and denies equity to hands like small pocket pairs or various overcard combinations. I will bet 300 into the 600 pot to maintain pressure.

Call. I checked and now face a half‑pot c‑bet (300 into 900) with A♥6♥ on 8♦8♥4♠. Calling 300 to win 1200 requires ≈25% equity; against a wide button c‑bet range I have reasonable showdown equity (I block many A‑combos, beat a lot of Kx/Qx and pure bluffs, and have backdoor hearts). Raising OOP with just Ace‑high would bloat the pot and commit me with a weak hand, folding surrenders attractive pot odds. Plan: call to realize equity, then fold to big turn raises/shoves if I don’t improve; continue or apply pressure only if I pair the Ace or pick up a meaningful draw.

I have A♥6♥ on 8♦ 8♥ 4♠ 9♦ and I currently only have Ace-high (no pair, no draw). Flash is the preflop raiser and barreled the flop (large bet) and the turn — his range is heavy in eights, some 4x, overpairs and a mix of weaker Ax/Kx/turn converts and occasional bluffs. There is no bet outstanding (To Call = 0) and the pot is already meaningful (1,200). Betting or raising here would be a thin bluff with very limited fold equity against his range and would risk committing chips with a very weak holding. Pot control and showdown realization is the superior line: check and see a river card. If he checks back we get to showdown cheaply; if he leads the river we can evaluate bet size and likely fold to large river pressure unless we improve. Stack sizes (I: 10,200; Villain: 28,600) also argue against turning this into a big bluff. Therefore I check.

On a board of 8-8-4-9, I have King-high with a Jack kicker. I was the pre-flop and flop aggressor. Minni called a half-pot bet on the flop and has checked again on the turn. While I still have overcards and some showdown value, the 9 might have connected with some of her flop-calling range (like 9x or straight draws). Checking back here allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity. If the river is a King or Jack, I likely have the best hand, and if it's a blank, I may still win against missed draws or weaker overcards without risking a check-raise.

I improved to top pair on the river (A♣? I hold A♥6♥ vs board 8♦ 8♥ 4♠ 9♦ A♦). Villain opened preflop, c-bet the paired flop and then checked the turn — that line is wide: it contains some strong hands (8x trips, 9x/4x, two‑diamond flushes, big pairs) but also many medium/weak hands (Kx/Qx/Jx/Tx, missed broadways, floats). A small value bet is the highest EV line: it extracts from worse hands that will call (Kx/Qx/TX etc), keeps the pot manageable, and preserves a clean fold if villain raises big. I’ll lead for about one-third of the pot to get called by weaker hands while folding to significant river aggression; checking gives up value and lets bluffs and marginal hands see a free showdown.

On the river, the Ace is a very strong card for Minni's range, as she likely would have called a flop continuation bet with many Ax holdings. After both players checked the turn, Minni's 400-chip lead into a 1200-chip pot (total pot now 1600) is a small 'blocking' or value bet. While the odds to call are favorable (4:1), my King-high loses to any pair, including those Minni might have held from the flop (like 4x or small pocket pairs) or hit on the turn (9x). Since I have very little showdown value and cannot reliably beat any of her calling range, I will fold.

Heads-up on the button (I am the small blind). Q♠2♣ is weak but in HU I should open a very wide range to exploit position and steal the big blind. A standard 2x raise to 200 applies pressure while keeping the pot manageable given my ~113 BB stack. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet (Q2o is often dominated), but if called I'll play postflop in position — c-bet on favorable textures and proceed cautiously on Q-high boards where I'm likely behind.
In a heads-up situation, Queen-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a minimum raise from the Button. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to make the call and see a flop.

I have top pair with a solid kicker (Queen) on a relatively connected and semi-wet flop (two hearts, straight possibilities). In heads-up play, the Big Blind typically checks to the pre-flop raiser on the flop to see their continuation bet. I will check here with the intention of calling or potentially raising depending on Minni's bet size, as my hand is currently very strong against her wide button-opening range.

Heads-up I opened a very wide range preflop, so a continuation bet is standard here. The flop (5♥ 9♦ 7♥) is fairly dry and favors the preflop raiser’s range — many of Flash’s calling hands are missed. I hold Q♠2♣: one overcard to the board but no hearts or meaningful draws. Betting gives me fold equity to take down the pot now while keeping the pot manageable against a deeper stack. I’ll size to ~60% pot (250) to apply pressure on weak pairs and floats but still allow easy folding if Flash check-raises big. Plan: c-bet 250; fold to a sizable check-raise, but if called re-evaluate on the turn (continue only with improvement or if turn is another clean card for my range).

On a board of 5h 9d 7h, I have top pair with a Queen kicker. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play, especially against a standard continuation bet. Minni bet 250 into a 400 pot. While the board is connected with straight and flush draws, raising right now might shut out her bluffs or weaker hands. I will call to maintain pot control and allow her to continue betting with a wider range on the turn, while also protecting myself against being pushed off the best hand if she is semi-bluffing.

On the turn 2d, which is a complete brick, I still hold top pair with a strong kicker (Queen). Since I am out of position as the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows Minni to continue her bluffing range or bet with her draws (like hearts or straight possibilities). I am well-ahead of her air and middle-pair hands, and I intend to call if she bets to keep her range wide.

The turn paired my 2 (I hold Q♠2♣ and the board is 5♥ 9♦ 7♥ 2♦) — I’ve improved to a weak pair with position and showdown value. Flash called my flop c-bet, so his range includes heart two-card draws, floating Ax/Kx hands and some made pairs (9x/7x/5x). A small/medium bet now accomplishes three things: extracts value from missed overcards and two-heart draws that called the flop, charges the draws, and keeps the pot controlled against hands that currently beat me. I’ll size to ~1/3 pot to get called by worse hands but avoid bloating the pot when behind; if I run into a substantial check-raise I’ll fold per my preflop plan. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the river.

On a board of 5h-9d-7h-2d, I hold top pair with a Queen kicker. Minni's turn bet is very small (300 into a 900 pot, 25% pot size). This sizing often indicates a drawing hand, a weak made hand looking for a cheap showdown, or a persistent bluff. Given that the 2d is a total brick, my hand remains high in my range. I will call to keep her range wide and allow her to continue betting on the river with bluffs or missed draws, while also protecting myself against bigger hands.

I have top pair with a Queen kicker on a board where most draws (hearts, 68) have missed. The river 4s is mostly a brick, though it completes a very unlikely straight (A2345 or 23456). In heads-up, top pair is a very strong hand. I will check to induce a bluff from Minni's missed draws, as betting might only get called by hands that beat me or marginally worse 9s, while checking keeps her entire range in.

I have Q♠2♣ and the river (5♥ 9♦ 7♥ 2♦ 4♠) gives me a pair of deuces with a Q kicker. Flash checked after calling my 250 flop c‑bet and 300 turn bet — a line consistent with floats, missed overcards or weak one‑pair hands. Heads‑up this is an exploitable value spot: a modest river bet will get called by many worse hands (A/K/Q highs, Jx, single‑pair floats) while folding out pure air. I size to ~0.45 pot (700 into 1500) — large enough to extract value but small enough to keep thin callers in. If Flash jams or makes a large raise I’ll fold this weak second pair; if he calls I’ll go to showdown.

On a final board of 5h-9d-7h-2d-4s, I hold top pair with a Queen kicker. Most of the primary draws on the flop, specifically the heart flush and the 86 straight, have missed (though 86 was already there). Minni's bet sizing has been somewhat inconsistent, betting over half pot on the flop, then sizing down to a third on the turn, and roughly half pot on the river. In a heads-up match, Q9 is a very strong hand to reach the river with, and I only need to be right about 24% of the time to justify the call. Given the number of missed heart draws and other semi-bluffs she could have followed through with, I cannot fold top pair here.
I have QTs on the button, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Standard play is to raise to gain control and build the pot. I will raise to 300 chips.

Facing a standard 3x button open to 300 while I'm in the big blind with A♣5♦. Pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call (calling would make my total contribution 300). Heads‑up the button's opening range is very wide, and A5o blocks a lot of Ace combinations while retaining good playability (top‑pair, wheel potential). Calling 200 into the 400 pot gives me ~33% break‑even equity; against a wide HU range A5o has roughly ~40–45% equity, so a flat call is +EV. 

I considered a 3‑bet bluff, but I'm out of position and stacks are deep (I ~98bb, villain ~297bb) — 3‑betting OOP with A5o will bloat the pot and put me in tough spots postflop against an aggressive opponent. Flatting keeps the pot manageable, allows me to realize my equity and play postflop (look for A/5 or favorable low boards, fold to heavy pressure on dry Ace boards where I miss). 

Decision: call to 300 total and play the flop carefully out of position.

Check. I have A♣5♦ on K♦–Jc–8♦: only Ace‑high with a weak backdoor diamond possibility (need two diamonds on turn+river — ≈4% to complete) and only ~12% to pair an Ace by river. Villain was the preflop raiser (wide button range) so their continuing range contains lots of Kx/Jx and broadways that currently beat me. I’m out of position and stacks are deep (I ~96–97bb, villain ~297bb), so leading with air will bloat the pot and get called or raised too often. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize equity, and keeps options open. Plan: check-call small C‑bets (cheap equity), fold to large aggression, and if villain checks back take a free card to the turn and re-evaluate (barrel only if I pick up clear equity or a credible bluffing line presents itself).

On this flop of K-J-8, I have an open-ended straight draw (any 9 or Ace gives me a straight). This is a strong semi-bluffing candidate. Since Minni checked, I will place a continuation bet to represent a King or a strong Jack while building the pot for the times I hit my draw. A bet of 400 (two-thirds pot) is appropriate to apply pressure.

I check-called the flop. I’m out of position with A♣5♦ on K♦–Jc–8♦ — currently only Ace‑high but I have a weak runner‑runner diamond possibility and I block some Ace combinations. Flash opened wide from the button preflop and is c‑betting this board with a range that contains a lot of Kx/Jx/top‑pair hands but also a fair share of air and missed broadway hands. Facing a 400 bet, my call cost gives me ~22% break‑even equity (400 to win ~1800), and versus a wide HU c‑bet range A5o typically has at least that (plus I keep the pot manageable OOP). Raising OOP with essentially no pair would bloat the pot and put me in very tough spots against a deep, aggressive opponent, while folding here gives up too much equity for the price. Plan: call to see a turn — fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve (ace, 5, or runner‑runner diamond) or pick up a clear bluffing line later.

Turn analysis: I hold A♣5♦ on 8♦–J♣–K♦–4♣. I have no pair and my backdoor diamond draw is dead now (only one street left), so my hand has essentially zero showdown value. Flash was the preflop raiser and c‑bet the flop; him checking the turn shows either weakness or pot control with a one‑pair hand. Betting here as a bluff is thin — most hands that missed (broadways, junk) will call a small probe and Kx/Jx/Ax will often continue, and a bet risks getting raised into a very big pot OOP with deep stacks. With the pot only ~14bb and my stack ~92bb, preserving stack and seeing the river for a cheap card is the highest EV line. Plan: check and evaluate the river — if I pair A or 5 I can extract value; if the opponent bets I’ll fold to large sizing and consider calling very small bluffs depending on sizing and river texture.

On the turn 4c, the board remains relatively unchanged. I still hold an open-ended straight draw (any 9 or Ace completes the straight). Since Minni checked again after calling my flop continuation bet, she likely has a marginal made hand like a Jack or an 8, or perhaps a King with a weak kicker. While a second barrel could push her off middle or bottom pair, checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially hit my straight on the river without risking a check-raise that would force me to fold my draw. I will check.

I have A♣5♦ and missed the river — the board K♦–Jc–8♦–4c–2c leaves me with only Ace‑high. I checked flop and turn; Flash (the preflop raiser) c‑bet the flop and then checked the turn, which looks like either pot control with a one‑pair hand (Kx/Jx) or weakness. Betting as a bluff here has very limited fold equity: the pot is 1,400, so a half‑pot bluff would need Villain to fold >33% — but his checked turn range contains many hands that will call a river probe (Kx, Jx, broadways, some Tx/Qx). I also block some A‑combos, but not enough to make a bluff +EV. With negligible value and thin fold equity, the low‑variance, +EV line is to check back and take showdown rather than risk chips on a likely called bluff.

On the river 2c, the board remains very similar to the flop. I have Queen-high, having missed my open-ended straight draw. Minni has checked for the third time (flop check-call, turn check, river check). While a bluff could potentially push her off a weak 8 or a marginal Jack, she has shown a tendency to be sticky in several previous hands. Since I checked the turn, my range looks somewhat capped, and a river bet might not be credible enough to force a fold from a middle-pair hand. Given I have no showdown value but a significant chip lead, I will check back and concede the pot rather than risking more chips on a low-frequency bluff.

2♣7♠ is the weakest starting hand — dominated by almost any reasonable defending range. I’ve already posted the small blind (50) and would have to call another 50 to see a flop. Although the call is cheap, the hand has very poor playability OOP/versus likely defense and 3‑betting from an aggressive big blind with a much larger stack. Raising to 200 to try to steal is too risky and easily exploited. Preserve chips and wait for a much better spot — fold and concede the small blind.

I am on the button with 6h 7h, a strong suited connector that plays very well in position. Given my significant stack advantage over Minni, I want to open for a standard raise to build a pot and maintain pressure while being in position for the remainder of the hand.

I’m the big blind (10500) facing a 3x open to 300 from the SB/button. 9♥6♠ is marginal but has reasonable equity heads-up versus a wide opening range and the pot odds are attractive (200 to call into a ~400 pot, break-even ~33%). Folding is too nitty; 3-betting light is feasible but unnecessarily escalates OOP with a hand that’s hard to play deep. I’ll defend by calling 200 more (300 total committed) and evaluate the flop — continue with top pair/two pair/strong draws, otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to heavy, committed aggression on dry boards.

Flop: 8♥ Q♥ 3♥, my hand 9♥6♠. I have a very strong drawing hand — a four‑heart flush draw plus backdoor pair outs (9s/6s). Rough equity: ~35% to hit the flush by the turn/river and ≈51% to improve to at least a pair or a flush by river. Flash opened wide preflop and checked the flop, showing some weakness. Betting as a semi‑bluff is correct here: it gives me fold equity against many of his non‑heart hands and builds the pot when I have ~50% equity to improve. I’ll make a standard half‑pot c‑bet (300 into 600). If he raises small/medium I’ll call with good equity; I’ll re‑evaluate or fold to any extreme overbet/shove depending on sizing.

I have flopped a flush with 6h 7h on an 8h Qh 3h board. Minni has led out for 300 into a 600 pot. This is a very strong position for me, but since the board is monotone and I have a low flush, I need to be careful of higher flushes while also maximizing value. Raising now helps protect against a fourth heart and gets value from hands like a single heart, a pair, or two pair. Given the pot size and Minni's lead, a raise to 900 (3x the bet) is a standard value play that keeps their range wide enough to call.

Flop: 8♥ Q♥ 3♥, my hand 9♥6♠. I bet 300 as a semi‑bluff and Flash raised to 900 (pot 1800, 600 to call). I have a four‑card heart draw plus pair outs — roughly ~35% to make the flush by river and ~50%+ to improve to at least a pair or flush. The raise is a medium sizing; pot odds to call are about 25% (600 to win ≈2400), so a call is +EV. Raising OOP with just a draw would bloat the pot and be unnecessarily aggressive against a player who can have made flushes or strong Qx hands. Folding is too tight given my equity. I’ll call the 600 more (900 total committed) and re‑evaluate on the turn — proceed aggressively if I hit or fold to overwhelming action on blanks.

Turn: board 8♥ Q♥ 3♥ 4♥ — I’ve made a 9‑high flush with 9♥6♠. Flash’s line (open pre, big raise on my flop c‑bet) polarizes him toward both hearts and strong Qx/strong holdings. I’m behind to any higher heart (A/K/T/J) but ahead of a lot of his range (weaker hearts, Qx, pairs, combo draws). Betting for value and to keep the pot from getting out of control if I’m dominated is the right play. A modest, half‑pot sized bet both extracts from worse hands and is small enough to call for many inferior hands; if he comes over the top very large I’ll fold to avoid getting stacked by a higher heart. Pot = 2400, I’ll bet half‑pot (1200).

The turn has brought a fourth heart, making my 7-high flush extremely vulnerable. Any single heart in Minni's hand higher than the seven (the 9, 10, Jack, King, or Ace of hearts) currently beats me. Minni has led for 1,200 into a 3,600 pot, which is a 1/3 pot bet. This sizing could indicate a range of hands, including a higher flush, a set of eights or queens, or potentially a smaller heart like the five. Given the pot odds and the fact that we are heads-up, folding a flush to such a small bet is too tight, but I must remain extremely cautious on the river. I will call to see the final card and re-evaluate.

River pairs the board (4♦) and turns my turn-made 9♥ flush into a vulnerable holding. Flash’s line (open, big flop raise, then a call on my turn bet) is polarized toward either strong hearts or strong made hands that now can convert to full houses/trips with the paired 4 on the river (pocket 3s, any 4, Q4, QQ, etc.). Betting here mainly gets value from a small number of worse flushes and will get called (or check‑raised) by many hands that beat me. With pot control important (pot = 4,800, my stack 8,200) and a large portion of his range that now beats me, the prudent line is to check back, realize my showdown value, and avoid bloating the pot or getting committed to streets where I’m often behind. If he bets I can fold to big aggression or call small bluffs.

On a board with four hearts, my 7-high flush is extremely weak. Minni led into the turn after the fourth heart appeared, which strongly suggests they have at least one heart in their hand. Most of the hearts in their range (Ace, King, Jack, Ten, Nine) beat my seven. While the river paired the board, making full houses a possibility, Minni's check on the river indicates they may be in pot-control mode or have given up with a weaker heart/bluff. Betting here provides no value because I will only be called by better flushes or full houses, and worse hands will fold. Checking back allows me to reach showdown with my marginal equity.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) A♥8♠ is a strong open in this spot — a top-of-range ace with reasonable postflop playability. I want to seize initiative and take advantage of position, so I’ll open to a standard 3x BB. That gives fold equity versus the big blind, keeps weaker aces and broadways in my opening range, and leaves plenty of stack depth for postflop play. Note I’ve already posted 50 (pot is 150), so raising to 300 means I put in 300 total preflop (250 more). If Flash 3-bets big I’ll reassess (likely fold vs a large 3-bet); if he calls I’ll play aggressively with position.

Holding Ten-Four offsuit in the Big Blind, I am facing a 3x open raise from the button (Minni). This hand is weak, lacks connectivity, and doesn't play well out of position. Even with favorable pot odds, the long-term expected value of calling with such a hand is negative. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight of diamonds, which is a very strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. Standard procedure is to raise for value and to seize control of the pot. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind).

Facing a 300 open from the button while I'm in the big blind with Jc9c. Heads-up J9s is a strong, playable hand with good postflop equity and nut-flush potential, but I'm out of position, so I don't want to bloat the pot unnecessarily with a marginal 3-bet. Effective stacks are deep (~130 BB), so seeing a flop is profitable when I have implied odds and playability. I will flat-call the extra 200 (300 total) to realize equity, keep worse hands and bluffs in my opponent's range, and evaluate on the flop. Postflop plan: proceed aggressively when I pair or make strong club draws; call small continuation bets with draws/top-pair type hands; fold to large, polarized pressure when I miss completely.

Flop: Qs Ah Ts. I hold Jc9c — no pair but a double-gutshot: any K or 8 completes a straight (8 clean outs), and pairing J or 9 gives me second-pair possibilities (6 more, but often still behind). No flush is possible. I’m out of position and this is a drawing hand, so I want pot control and to realize equity rather than bloating the pot by barreling as a bluff. Checking keeps worse hands and bluffs in Flash’s range and allows me to call a reasonable c-bet to see the turn. Plan: check now; call small-to-medium continuation bets (roughly up to ~300 chips here ≈1/2 pot) to chase the ~31% equity to river, fold to large, polarized pressure that represents strong made hands, and if Flash checks back take a free turn to barrel or value-bet only if I improve (K/8/J/9) or get a favorable runout.

I have top pair with an Ace on a very coordinated board (Qs-Ah-Ts). While my kicker (8) is relatively weak, I still have the best hand most of the time in a heads-up situation. I will bet for value and to protect my hand against various draws that Minni could be holding, such as straight draws or back-door spade draws. A bet of 300 into a 600 pot (half-pot) is a standard sizing to extract value from weaker pairs (Qx, Tx) or draws while keeping the pot manageable if I am out-kicked.

I’m OOP with Jc9c on Qs Ah Ts. Flash bets 300 into a 900 pot (small c‑bet). My hand has 8 clean outs to a Broadway/9‑8 straight (Ks or 8s) and additional backdoor pair equity; by the river the straight-only chance is ~31–32%, and including pair equity my overall showdown equity vs a broad c‑betting range is even higher. Facing 300 into 900 I need ~25% equity to break even (300/(900+300)=25%), so a flat call is +EV. With ~128BB effective stacks there are strong implied odds if I complete my straight, and calling keeps worse hands and bluffs in Flash’s range. I’m not going to inflate the pot out of position with a bluff 3‑bet here; instead I’ll call, realize equity, and fold to large, polarized pressure if I completely miss on later streets. If I improve (K/8/J/9) I’ll proceed aggressively; if Flash checks back I’ll take a free turn to continue on wet cards.

I call. I have Jc9c on Qs Ah Ts 2s — a double-gutshot (Ks or 8s = 8 clean outs) plus pair/upside (J or 9 = 6 more outs). Facing a small ~300 turn bet into the ~1200 pot gives me excellent pot odds: I only need ~20% equity (300/(1200+300) ≈ 20%), and my chance to improve to a pair or the straight by the river is roughly 14/46 ≈ 30.4%, so calling is +EV. I’m out of position with deep stacks, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a raise as a semi‑bluff — calling realizes equity, keeps worse hands and bluffs in Flash’s range, and preserves implied odds if I hit. Plan: call the 300 now; if I hit the river I’ll extract value, if I miss I’ll fold to large polarized pressure but can call small/medium river bets depending on pot odds and reads.

The board has become very dangerous with three spades now present, completing potential flushes. While I have top pair with my Ace, my kicker is weak and I do not have a spade in my hand to act as a redraw or a blocker. Minni's check-call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board (like a Queen, Ten, or a draw). By checking back on the turn, I control the size of the pot, avoid a potential check-raise, and keep the pot manageable so I can try to reach a cheap showdown or evaluate the river.

I improved to second pair (9s) with a weak Jack kicker on the river (Qs Ah Ts 2s 9d). Flash’s line — open preflop, small flop c‑bet, then check turn — is consistent with Ax/Qx/Tx, single‑spade flushes, and some turn/river floats; many of those hands still beat my pair. Betting here will mostly fold out pure air and get called by the hands that beat me (A/Q/T and spade flushes), so a value bet is unlikely to be +EV. I’m out of position and the pot is relatively small, so the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value line is to check and see a showdown. If Flash now leads into me I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing (fold to a large polarizing shove, call small/medium probes). Action: check to show down.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a very coordinated board. The turn card completed a potential flush (three spades), and the river 9d doesn't change much but adds more straight possibilities (J9, etc.). Minni has check-called the flop and checked both the turn and river. While I likely have the best hand against her pairs of Queens or Tens, betting for value is risky because the board is so connected. If I bet, she's unlikely to call with worse hands, but she might check-raise with a flush, straight, or a better Ace. Checking back allows me to take the pot at showdown without further risk.

Heads-up on the button with J♦9♥ — a playable, well-connected hand. I have position and deep stacks, so I want initiative and to avoid giving the BB cheap flops. Standard heads-up strategy is to open fairly wide; I’ll raise to 3x the big blind (300 total). If Flash calls I’ll use position to c-bet and apply pressure; if he 3-bets heavily I’ll largely fold and re-evaluate given my hand’s equity.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc6s. Minni, on the button, has raised to 3x (300). While it is heads-up and ranges are wider, J6o is a very weak hand to defend out of position against a 3x open. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win 400), and the fact that I am out of position for the rest of the hand, folding is the most disciplined play to maintain my chip lead.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy dictates an open-raise to build the pot and exert pressure. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent sizing throughout the match.

Heads-up with AQo in the big blind vs a button open — this is a strong hand vs a likely wide opening range. Calling 200 is cheap, but I prefer to 3-bet to take the initiative, isolate, and get folds from worse aces/broadways while building value. I’ll raise to 1000 total (a ~3.3x 3-bet): large enough to apply pressure and earn fold equity, but small enough to keep postflop stacks playable (I still have deep stacks). If Flash 4-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to an enormous 4-bet jam, call a reasonable 4-bet depending on sizing and read).

In heads-up play, Ace-Jack offsuit is a premium hand. After my initial raise to 300, Minni 3-bet to 1,000. While I could 4-bet for value, calling allows me to play a strong hand in position and keeps Minni's range wide, including potential bluffs. My goal is to see a flop and use my positional advantage to realize equity.

Flop is 2c Qh 2s — I hold Q♦ A♥: top pair with the best kicker after 3-betting preflop. Flash called my 3-bet, so his continuing range contains lots of Qx, some Ax, broadways and medium pocket pairs, plus a small number of single-deuce combos that make trips (the main nut concern). This is a very good hand vs his calling range but vulnerable to any deuce. I want to extract thin value from worse queens/Ax and charge medium-strength pocket pairs while keeping the pot controllable against a possible trip. I’ll lead for about half the pot to get value and deny a free runout; I’ll fold to an obvious large check-raise (which almost always represents a deuce/trips), and if he simply calls I’ll reevaluate on the turn.

I have Ace-high (Ace-Jack offsuit) on a dry, paired board (2c-Qh-2s). Minni 3-bet pre-flop and is now leading with a continuation bet of 1,000 into a 2,000 pot (offering me 3:1 pot odds). While a Queen is certainly in her range, so are hands like AK, smaller pocket pairs, and total bluffs. Given my position and the strength of Ace-high in heads-up play, folding here is too conservative. Raising would likely only be called by hands that beat me. Calling allows me to keep her bluffing range in and see how she reacts on the turn.

Heads-up with Q♦A♥ on 2♣ Q♥ 2♠ 6♠. I 3‑bet preflop and led the flop for value; Flash called both streets, which weights his range to a lot of Qx, some Ax, broadways and medium pairs — very few 2x combos. The 6♠ on the turn changes little (gives a backdoor spade but doesn’t beat my hand). I want to extract value from worse queens/aces and charge any medium pairs while denying free rivers to overcards, so I’ll bet a concrete, value-heavy size instead of checking and giving him a free card. Bet half the pot (2000 into 4000). Plan if action: — If he check‑raises to a large/pot‑sized sizing or jams, that almost always represents a deuce/trips and I’ll fold. — If he makes a small/medium raise I’ll call and re‑evaluate on the river. — If he calls or folds, proceed accordingly on the river.

On a board of 2c-Qh-2s-6s, Minni has 3-bet pre-flop and led both the flop and the turn. My Ace-high (As Jh) is currently losing to any pocket pair (33 through AA) and any Queen in her 3-betting range (AQ, KQ, QJ). The board is extremely dry, meaning there are very few natural semi-bluffs for her to have, other than perhaps AK or spade draws. I am getting 3:1 pot odds, but I likely only have 6 outs (Aces and Jacks) if she has a hand like KQ or a smaller pocket pair, and even fewer if she has AQ or AA/JJ. Given the risk of a third barrel on the river and Minni's history of value betting strongly, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with pocket deuces (2♦2♣). With 145bb effective and the pot only 150, calling 50 to ‘set-mine’ gives poor immediate pot odds (≈4:1) vs ~12% to hit a set, so I prefer to take the initiative. I want to apply pressure, pick up the blinds when missed, and get value/isolate when I flop a set. A standard 3x BB open (raise to 300 total) is a clean size here — big enough to generate fold equity but still small relative to our deep stack. If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on size (call small 3-bets to set-mine, fold to large ones). Raising to 300 keeps options open postflop and fights for initiative.

I am holding Ten-Seven of diamonds in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. T7s is a solid defending hand in a heads-up situation, offering both straight and flush potential. Given the pot odds (I need to call 200 to win a total pot of 400, meaning I need about 33% equity), a call is the standard and correct play here to see a flop and realize my hand's potential without bloating the pot out of position.

On the flop of 6s-5h-8s, I have an open-ended straight draw with my Ten-Seven. The board is quite connected and contains a flush draw (spades). As the Big Blind, checking is the standard play here to see how the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) reacts. If Minni bets, I have plenty of equity to continue. Checking allows me to realize my equity or potentially check-raise if the situation warrants it, though a check-call is generally safer with a draw out of position to manage the pot size.

I took the initiative preflop with a button raise and the flop is 6s-5h-8s — fairly dry except for spade draws. I hold pocket deuces, an underpair with some showdown value but vulnerable to overcards. In heads‑up play I want to continue to apply pressure while keeping the pot controlled given our deep stacks. A small c-bet (~1/3 pot = 200 into 600) will fold out unpaired overcards, charge spade draws, and let me realize equity cheaply if called. If Flash folds I take the pot; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously (avoid bloating the pot with just an underpair and fold to large check‑raises unless I improve).

On the flop of 6s-5h-8s, I have an open-ended straight draw with my Ten-Seven (needing a 4 or a 9). Minni has led with a small bet of 200 into a 600-chip pot (1/3 pot size). This offers me excellent pot odds to continue. Given the high equity of an open-ended straight draw and the relatively small bet, calling is the most profitable and standard play here. This allows me to realize my equity and see the turn card without over-committing to the pot while out of position.

On the turn of 6s-5h-8s-3c, I still hold an open-ended straight draw (needing a 9 or a 4). The 3c is essentially a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Being out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows me to see if Minni continues betting. If she checks behind, I get a free card to hit my straight on the river. If she bets, I will evaluate the price offered to see the final card based on my pot odds and implied odds.

Check. I opened preflop and made a small flop c‑bet that was called; the turn (3c) is a brick for my hand — I still only have an underpair (22) with limited equity. Betting the turn would fold out some pure overcards but will be called too often by spade draws and any pair, and with deep stacks I don’t want to build a big pot out of position against those holdings. In position I prefer pot control: check to see the river, preserve my showdown value, and keep the option to call smaller river bets or bluff/raise selectively if the action and runout justify it.

On the river board of 6s-5h-8s-3c-Kd, I have missed my open-ended straight draw and currently hold Ten-high. In Hand #84, Minni checked back on the turn after a small flop bet, which suggests she likely has a marginal made hand or a missed draw she's content to take to a cheap showdown. Since I have no showdown value, my only way to win the pot is to bluff. However, the King is a card that often hits the pre-flop aggressor's range, and since I am out of position, leading into the pot after a missed draw is risky against a player who has shown some stickiness. I will check and see if she checks behind, acknowledging that I likely lose this pot, but avoiding throwing more chips into a situation where a bluff might be called by any pair or even Ace-high.

Check. I have an underpair (22) on K‑8‑6‑3 board and my line (raise pre, small c‑bet called on flop, both checked turn) suggests Flash’s calling range contains missed spade draws, overcards and small pairs. The river K is a scary card but Flash checked it — betting here would fold out some worse hands but get called by most hands that beat me and unnecessarily bloats the pot with a marginal holding. Pot control and showdown are optimal: check to see his action and only call reasonable river bets (fold to very large river shoves unless pot odds justify).

In this hand, I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Queen-Deuce offsuit (Q2o). While I have position for the rest of the hand, Q2o is a very weak starting hand. Minni has shown significant aggression in previous hands, and I don't want to invest chips in a marginal hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially against a player who frequently bets and raises. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 5♥3♣ — a very weak, non-connected offsuit hand. In heads‑up play the button/SB should open a very wide range to steal the BB and seize initiative. Raising to the minimum (200 total) gives good fold equity, keeps pot sizes manageable with a poor hand, and lets me play position postflop if called. If Flash 3‑bets I’ll respect the aggression and fold this hand rather than bloating the pot with almost no showdown value. If called I’ll proceed cautiously postflop, c‑bet when appropriate and fold to strong resistance. Raise to 200 to apply pressure and exploit the heads‑up dynamics.

In the big blind, I'm facing a min-raise from Minni on the button. I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (100 to call for a total pot of 400). Although 6-5 offsuit is a weak hand, it is sufficient to defend against a small raise in a heads-up situation to see a flop. I will call the 100 more.

I have bottom pair with an open-ended straight draw (sixes and fives with a 7, 6, 10 board, needing a 4, 8, or 9 for improvement or a 5 or 6 for a stronger hand). This is a strong enough drawing hand to check and see how the opponent acts. I will check to the raiser to maintain pot control while I evaluate the strength of their continuation bet.

I raised preflop with a very wide heads‑up range and Flash just called. Flop 7♥ 6♣ T♠ misses my 5♥3♣ — I only have a gutshot to a 4 (4 outs) and a very small backdoor heart route. As the preflop aggressor in heads‑up play I should c‑bet frequently to fold out unpaired overcards and seize the initiative. The pot is 400, so I’ll make a small half‑pot continuation bet (200) to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal hand. If I’m check‑raised or face heavy action on the flop/turn I’ll fold unless I pick up significant equity.

I have bottom pair (sixes) and an open-ended straight draw on a 7-6-10 board. Minni's 200-chip bet into a 400-chip pot gives me 3:1 pot odds. With 8 outs for a straight and additional outs to improve to two pair or trips (roughly 13 total outs), I have more than enough equity to call. Calling is preferred over raising here to keep the pot manageable while playing out of position and to allow Minni to continue bluffing with weaker hands.

I have bottom pair (sixes) and an open-ended straight draw (needing a 4 or 8) on a board that just paired tens. Since I am first to act on the turn, I will check to see how Minni reacts to the paired board. This allows me to control the pot size while I decide whether to continue based on her bet size and my drawing odds.

The turn pairs the Ten and I’ve missed completely with 5♥3♣. I only have a 4‑card gutshot (4 outs ≈ 4/46 ≈ 8.7%) and no realistic backdoor flush. Flash called my flop c‑bet, showing a tendency to call down light; his check on the turn looks like pot‑control with a pair (T, 7, 6) or a slow‑played stronger hand. Betting here would require a reasonably high fold frequency to be +EV versus his calling range, and if he calls I’m a large favorite to lose chips given my tiny equity. With deep stacks and heads‑up dynamics I should control the pot and realize my equity cheaply — check back and see the river. If he now bets the river I can fold most non‑made hands; if the river brings a 4 I’ll pursue value/bluff lines.

On the river, the board is 7-6-T-T-2. I have a pair of sixes. The turn went check-check after Minni's flop continuation bet, which often indicates she has a marginal made hand or a missed draw she's looking to take to showdown. My pair of sixes has some showdown value, but it is unlikely to get called by a worse hand if I bet. Checking allows me to see a showdown for free or potentially catch a bluff if she decides to bet her missed draws. I will check.

I hold 5♥3♣ and missed the board 7♥ 6♣ T♠ T♦ 2♠. I was the preflop/ flop aggressor (opened to 200, c‑bet 200) and Flash called the flop then checked the turn and the river. That line looks like pot‑control with a made hand (a Ten, or a small pair) or a tendency to check back marginal hands rather than folding frequently. Given Flash’s calling tendencies in earlier hands, my river bluff would need a fairly high fold frequency to be +EV (e.g. a 400 chip bluff needs >~33% folds; a 200 chip bluff needs >~20%). I don’t trust he will fold enough here, and I don’t want to bloat the pot with essentially no showdown value. I’ll check back, realize showdown equity if he has missed, and avoid unnecessary risk. If he bets I’ll fold most non‑made hands.

I am on the button with 6h 3s. This is a weak starting hand. While I'm in position and the pot is small, Minni has been relatively aggressive in previous hands, and 63o is well outside a standard opening range even in heads-up play. Folding is the most prudent long-term strategy here to preserve my stack lead.

J♠T♦ is a very strong heads‑up hand — wide value/semibluff candidate. I’m on the button (I posted SB) so I act first preflop but will have positional advantage postflop. With deep stacks (~147 BB) I want to seize initiative, build the pot and get value from worse hands while folding out some pure air. Flash is an active player who calls and raises a lot, so a standard 3x BB open is appropriate: large enough to apply pressure and consistent with table sizing but not overcommitting against a potential 3‑bet. If he 3‑bets big I can reevaluate/fold; if he calls I’ll play the flop in position. Raise to 300 total.

In this heads-up scenario, I am in the Big Blind with 8s6s and facing a standard 3x raise to 300 from Minni. Although 86o is not a particularly strong hand, I have a deep stack and the pot odds are 2:1 to see a flop. However, given that Minni is the shorter stack and has been playing aggressively, I need to be cautious about playing weak offsuit connectors out of position. Comparing the risk of calling versus the potential of the hand, folding is a disciplined play here to wait for better spots, as 86o is towards the bottom of the defending range in this configuration.

Holding K6o on the button in a heads-up situation, I'll put in a standard raise to apply pressure. Minni has been active and has a stack of about 148bbs, so K6o is definitely strong enough to raise from the small blind/button to try and win the blinds or play a pot with initiative.

Facing a 3bb open to 300 from the button while I’m in the big blind, Q♦3♦ (Q3o) is a very weak, frequently-dominated hand. I’m out of position post‑flop against a wide but aggressive opening range that includes a lot of Qx/Ax/Kx, so calling 200 to see a flop will put me in many difficult spots with poor equity. 3‑betting as a bluff is unnecessarily spewy here (I don’t have the right blockers and Flash shows aggression postflop), and the min‑raise to 500 would risk too much with a marginal holding. Preserve chips for better, more playable spots — fold and accept the 100bb posted.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 6♦7♠ — a marginal but playable, connected hand. In heads-up play you should open very wide from the button to take the initiative and use position. Pot is 150 and a call costs only 50, but raising gives fold equity and lets me play postflop with position and a disguised hand. I’ll open to a standard ~3xBB sizing (to 300 total). That commits me to 300 (I’ve posted 50, so I’ll add 250); the pot becomes 400. If Flash folds I win the pot; if he calls the pot will be ~600 and I’ll have position to play postflop. A min-raise is efficient, but 3x balances fold equity vs keeping stacked depth reasonable against an opponent who calls fairly wide. Therefore: raise to 300.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack of Hearts and Eight of Diamonds. The pot is currently 400 and it costs me 200 to call a 3x raise from Minni (Button/Small Blind). Jack-Eight offsuit is a marginal defense hand in a heads-up situation, but given the pot odds (2:1) and the fact that we are playing deep-stacked (Minni has 145BB, I have 251BB), calling to see a flop is a standard play here to defend the big blind.

I have top pair with an Eight on a very dry board of 4-2-8. In a heads-up pot where I defended the big blind, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play. I will look to check-call most reasonable bet sizes to see how the hand develops, as my hand is strong enough to continue but not necessarily strong enough to lead out into the aggressor.

Heads-up on the button with 6♦7♠ vs a checked BB on a very dry flop 4♥-2♦-8♣. I currently have no pair, only a one-card gutshot to a 5 (4 outs) and a backdoor diamond — ~16% to make by river. As the preflop aggressor I should c-bet frequently on dry boards to pick up pots and pressure missed overcards in his calling range. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll use a small sizing (~1/3 pot) to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot controllable if called. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn (continue only if I improve or get a good spot, fold to significant raise on a blank).

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a very dry board of 8-4-2. Minni's small continuation bet of 200 into 600 is standard and could represent a wide range of hands, including total air, draws, or value. Since my hand is strong but vulnerable to overcards on the turn and river, calling is the best strategy here. Raising might fold out their bluffs, whereas calling allows Minni to continue betting with weaker hands on later streets and keeps the pot manageable.

On the turn, the board is 4h-2d-8c-7c. I have top pair with an Eight and a Jack kicker. Since I have been check-calling and the board remains relatively safe (with only potential straight draws appearing for hands like 5-6), I will continue with my strategy of checking to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows Minni to continue bluffing or betting with a wider range of hands, while I keep the pot controlled with a medium-strength hand.

I turned second pair (7s) with a weak kicker on a relatively dry board 4♥-2♦-8♣-7♣. Flash called my small flop c-bet, so his range includes top pair 8x, small/medium pairs, floats/overcards, and some backdoor club combos. Heads-up and in position I want to extract value from worse pairs and floats while charging any drawing combos, but I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand that can be dominated by 8x or sets. A small-to-medium continuation bet keeps the initiative, gets value, and denies a free river card to many overcards. I’ll bet ~40% pot (400). If he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river; if he check-raises large I’ll be inclined to fold this vulnerable second pair.

On a turn board of 4h-2d-8c-7c, I still hold top pair with my Eight and a Jack kicker. Minni's bet of 400 into a 1000 pot is a relatively standard size that could represent a wide range, including lower pairs, draws, or air. My hand is too strong to fold at this point but raising might only force out bluffs and get called by hands that beat me (like sets, two pairs, or a straight). Calling keeps the pot controlled and allows me to see the river and potentially catch a bluff or win at showdown.

The Ace on the river is a significant scare card as it frequently connects with the pre-flop raiser's range. My pair of Eights, which was top pair on the flop, is now essentially a marginal showdown hand. Checking is the most prudent move here, as it allows me to reach showdown for free if Minni also checks, or to evaluate her bet size and determine if she's likely value-betting an Ace or bluffing with a missed draw.

I have 7♠6♦ — second pair (7s) with a weak 6 kicker. The river A♣ is a dangerous overcard and also completes potential club flushes for any opponent holding two clubs. Flash called my small flop and turn bets, so his range contains top pair (8x), middle/weak pairs (4x,2x), some overcards and combo draws; his river check is ambiguous (pot control with an Ace, weak showdown, or trap with a made flush). Betting here risks a check‑raise or being forced off a marginal hand by stronger pairs/flushes, while a thin value bet will only get called by a narrow subset of worse hands. With decent showdown equity vs his non‑made hands and a high cost to being raised, the lowest‑variance +EV line is to check and take it to showdown rather than bloat the pot or fold to pressure. Therefore: check.

I am on the button (Small Blind) in a heads-up situation with K5o. With a stack of 26,050 and Minni having 13,800, I have a significant chip lead. K5o is a strong enough hand to raise from the button in a heads-up game to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot early. Raising is generally better than just completing the blind here as it allows me to take the pot down immediately if Minni folds or sets up a continuation bet on many flops.
