Facing a button open to 300 from SB and I'm in the big blind with T9o. Stacks are deep (~138bb effective) and Flash has shown a wide/aggressive opening profile. T9o is a strong defending hand heads-up — good equity and postflop playability — so I prefer to call and see a flop rather than 3-bet out of position with a hand that lacks strong blockers. Calling keeps the pot manageable, gives good implied odds, and lets me evaluate the flop (raise/lead with strong connects or draws, fold to heavy pressure with air). I will call to 300 total.

Flop is K♠ K♣ 4♠ — I have T♥9♦ (no pair, no meaningful backdoor). Facing a button open preflop from an aggressive opponent, their range contains many Kx, big pairs and broadways that beat me outright. This is a very dry, paired board with almost zero equity for T9o and poor blockers for a credible bluff. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot with essentially no equity and little fold equity versus a wide/calling opponent. Best line is pot control: check and let the villain prove it. If Flash bets, I should fold the vast majority of the time; if they check back I can reevaluate on the turn with free cards.

I have flopped trips with Kh 5d on a 4s Ks Kc board. With only one King remaining in the deck, it is highly likely I have the best hand. Minni checked to me, and while I could check back to induce a bluff on the turn, I prefer to start building the pot and get value from hands like pocket pairs, a 4, or spade flush draws. A bet of 300 (half pot) is a standard value bet that keeps her range wide and makes it easy for her to call with marginal hands.

I checked OOP on the flop and now face a 300 bet into a 900 pot. I have T♥9♦ on K♠ K♣ 4♠ — no pair, no spade/flush draw, no realistic straight outs. Calling 300 requires ~20% equity to be +EV; versus Flash’s wide but c-betting SB range on this dry, paired board (lots of Kx, Ax, pocket pairs and some spade draws) my realized equity is well under that. A bluff-raise is possible in very specific blocker-driven spots, but T9o has no meaningful blockers to Kx or spade combos and I’d be risking a large portion of my deep stack with low fold equity. Prefer to preserve chips, avoid playing big pots with near-zero equity, and wait for a better spot. Fold.

I’m on the button (SB) heads-up with 8♣5♥ — a marginal hand but in heads-up play my opening range should be wide. A min-raise to 200 (2x the BB, total committed = 200) applies pressure to the big blind, often stealing the pot outright while keeping the pot manageable if called. If Flash folds I pick up the 150 chip pot; if called I keep positional advantage and can c-bet dry boards or play cautiously vs strong action. I’ll fold to a large 3-bet, but otherwise continue postflop in position.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 2s. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has made a min-raise to 200. Although the pot odds are 3:1 (100 to call into a 300 pot), 82o is one of the weakest starting hands in poker and plays poorly out of position. Given my significant stack lead, I see no reason to defend with such a marginal hand.

I am on the button with a pocket pair (2c 2d). In a heads-up situation, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand. I will put in a standard 3x open raise to 300 to take control of the pot and see how the opponent reacts.

Heads-up in the big blind with pocket 5s vs a button open to 300. Flash is an aggressive opener and stacks are deep (I have ~136bb), so 55 has good implied odds for set-mining. I’m out of position postflop, so a small 3-bet (min to 500) gives little extra fold equity and a larger 3-bet unnecessarily bloats a pot I don’t want to play out of position with a small pair. Calling keeps the pot controlled, preserves my implied odds to extract big value when I hit a set, and lets me fold to heavy action when I miss. Plan: call 200 more to see the flop, proceed cautiously on boards with overcards, and look to extract value if I flop a set or strong disguised hand.

I called preflop as a set-mining call with 55. I missed my set — flop is K-4-4, so there’s an overcard (K) that beats my pair and many of the preflop raiser’s value combos (Kx, bigger pocket pairs). I’m out of position and stacks are deep, so I want to control the pot rather than bloat it with a vulnerable one-pair hand. Check to keep the pot small, induce action and see how Flash responds. Plan: call a small-to-medium c-bet to evaluate the turn, fold to heavy aggression, and only build the pot if I improve (or get clear readable value) on later streets.

On a paired board with a King (4h Kc 4c), my pocket deuces (2c 2d) currently hold some showdown value but are very vulnerable to overcards. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and the board is relatively dry, I will place a continuation bet of half the pot (300). This bet aims to fold out better hands that missed (like Ace-high or small suited connectors) and protect my marginal hand's equity. If Minni calls, I will likely check back on many turns unless I improve.

I have pocket 5s on K♣ 4♥ 4♣ — I missed the set and currently hold a vulnerable underpair to the King, although my 5s are ahead of a lot of unpaired and bluff hands and are ahead of any non‑K pocket pairs below 55. Flash is an aggressive, wide opener and the flop bet is small (~1/3 pot), so his range contains many Kx, some pairs, and plenty of bluffs. Out of position with a marginal one‑pair hand, my priority is pot control and to keep bluffs in; a call is the lowest-variance line. Calling 300 gets decent immediate pot odds (300 to win ~1,200 ≈ 25%) and preserves the chance to hit a very strong turn (two 5s give me a full house) while allowing me to fold to heavy turn aggression if necessary. I will re-evaluate on the turn and only build the pot if I improve or receive a clear read/value, so I call the 300.

Check. I have pocket 5s on 4♥ K♣ 4♣ 7♦ — I missed my set and remain an underpair to any Kx, any 4x and many higher pocket pairs. There are only two outs (the remaining fives) to make a full house on the river (~4.3%), so I don't want to bloat the pot out of position. Flash is an aggressive, wide opener who will continue many missed hands; checking keeps the pot under control, induces bluffs I can call, and preserves the option to fold to heavy turn aggression. Plan: check here, call small-to-medium continuation bets to keep showdown equity and fold to very large/committing bets; if Flash checks back we re-evaluate on the river.

On the turn (7d), the board remains paired (4h Kc 4c). My pocket deuces are still a marginal hand with some showdown value, but they are unlikely to improve and are vulnerable to many cards. Minni has checked twice. While a second barrel might fold out some hands, it is safer to check back here to control the size of the pot and try to reach a cheap showdown. If Minni has a King or a 4, they are likely not folding, and if they have a draw or a lower pocket pair, they might fold, but checking behind keeps the pot manageable for my weak-to-marginal hand.

I check. Both checked the turn and the river 2♠ changes nothing — my pocket 5s remain a vulnerable underpair to any Kx, any pocket pair >55 and to 4x. Betting here has very thin value (most hands that call beat me) and mainly folds out the exact bluffs I want to catch, while also exposing me to a check‑raise from an aggressive opener. With only 1,200 in the pot and deep stacks, the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line is to keep the pot small and take showdown. If Flash now bets a small amount I can call to catch bluffs; I’ll fold to a large overbet/raise.

I spiked a miracle deuce on the river to complete a full house (deuces full of fours). The board is 4-K-4-7-2. My hand beats all Kings, all missed draws, and even three-of-a-kind fours (which would be 4-4-4-K-7). The only hands that beat me are higher full houses like K-4, 7-4, or quad fours, which are unlikely given Minni's passive check-calling line throughout the hand. Since Minni checked for the third time, I will place a value bet of 800 (about 67% of the pot) to extract value from a King or any other mid-pair she might be holding.

Call 800. Summary: I have 5♠5♥ on Kc 4h 4c 7d 2s — a vulnerable underpair that loses to Kx, any 4x, and higher pocket pairs but beats a lot of air and missed draws. Flash was the preflop raiser, c‑bet small on the flop and checked the turn — a line consistent with thin Kx and plenty of missed/drawy hands (and with his aggressive tendencies he will bluff or barrel rivers with missed draws). Pot math: calling 800 into the current pot (final pot ≈ 3600) requires ≈22% equity to be break‑even (800/3600 ≈ 22%). Given Flash’s line and aggression, it’s reasonable he’s bluffing or thin‑value betting often enough to clear that threshold. Calling is low risk (only ~6% of my stack) and the correct low‑variance line to catch bluffs; raising as a bluff OOP is too risky and folding surrenders too much equity. I will call the 800 and fold to any significant raise.

Heads-up on the button with 5♠T♥ and deep stacks (~122bb effective). In heads-up play I should open a wide range to seize initiative and apply pressure — T5s is a perfectly reasonable raising hand (playable in position, has backdoor flush/straight potential and blocks some tens). I’ll use a standard ~3bb open to deny the BB good odds to see a flop for free and to mix with the table’s common sizing. Raise to 300 (total committed) keeps fold equity and gives me position postflop. If Flash 3-bets to a large size I’ll fold this marginal hand; versus a smaller 3-bet I can consider flats and realize equity. If called, I’ll continuation-bet favorable textures and use position to control the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of fours. Minni, who is on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 300. Pocket fours is a solid hand to defend with heads-up, but since I am out of position and Minni has a relatively short stack compared to mine (12,000 vs 27,600), I want to see a flop and set-mine. Calling the additional 200 to see a flop is profitable here. While a three-bet is an option to put pressure on Minni, I prefer calling to keep the pot size manageable out of position with a low pocket pair.

On the flop of Kd-9h-3c, my pocket fours are very weak as there are two overcards. I am out of position against Minni, who raised pre-flop. I will check to see how Minni acts. If Minni bets, I will likely fold unless the bet size is extremely small, as it's difficult to continue with only a small pair on this board texture against a range that includes many Kings, Nines, and higher pocket pairs.

Flop K♦ 9♥ 3♣ is very dry and favors the preflop raiser’s range. I have T♥5♠ — no pair, but a few showdown outs (three Tens, three Fives) and a backdoor spade. Heads-up and in position I should leverage fold equity rather than give Flash a free turn. A half‑pot c-bet applies pressure to a wide checking/calling range and keeps the pot manageable if called. Bet 300 into 600 to take it down now; plan to fold to a large check‑raise, and if called proceed cautiously — check the turn and only continue with a clear improvement (a Ten/Five or strong draw) or a well-timed bluff on a highly favorable turn card.

The flop contains two overcards to my pocket fours (King and Nine). Minni's continuation bet is standard, and while she could be bluffing, my hand has very little equity to improve and will be difficult to play on future streets out of position. I'll fold and look for a more favorable situation.

I am on the Button in a heads-up situation with Queen-Seven offsuit. This hand is strong enough to open for a raise to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take the pot down immediately or play in position. I will follow my established sizing and raise to 300.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with K♦5♣ — a marginal but playable heads-up hand. The pot is 400 and I need to call 200 (total 300), so I only need ~33% equity to break even. Versus a wide button opening range K5o has acceptable equity and good showdown value; I can also realize backdoor improvements. I’ll call rather than 3-bet: a small reraise would bloat a pot I’ll play out of position and is unlikely to fold out many better hands, while a large 3-bet is thin for value. With deep stacks I prefer to see a flop and use postflop playability.

I have top pair (K♦5♣) on K♥ T♥ 7♠ but a very weak kicker and I’m out of position. Flash opened wide from the button preflop, so his range contains lots of worse kings, tens, and bluffs — and he barrels frequently. Leading out OOP bloats the pot and can get me raised off a marginal hand; checking lets me control pot size and induce bluffs/c-bets I can call. Plan: check and call small-to-medium bets (≈≤ half-pot) to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to a large, polarized raise unless I pick up a read that he’s bluffing. Checking minimizes downside while keeping worse hands and bluffs in his range.

I have bottom pair on a coordinated board of King-Ten-Seven with two hearts. While I have some showdown value, betting here doesn't necessarily get better hands to fold or worse hands to call (other than draws). Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and see a turn card for free, keeping the pot small with a marginal made hand.

I have top pair (K5) with a very weak kicker and I'm out of position. Pot is 600 and Flash checked both flop and turn — against his wide button opening range that pattern is more often weakness or a slow‑play, but I shouldn't bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand. Checking keeps worse hands and bluffs in, controls the pot size, and lets me evaluate the river; I can call small-to-medium bets (~≤½ pot) on the river and fold to large, polarized jams unless I improve or pick up a clear read. With these stacks and the dry board, the prudent line is to check back the turn and re-evaluate on the river.

On the turn, the 3 of spades is a relatively blank card. I have third pair (sevens), which has some showdown value. Minni has checked for the second time, which could indicate weakness or a trap with a marginal hand like a Ten. Betting here doesn't achieve much; better hands like a Ten or a King are unlikely to fold, and I'd prefer to see a river for free to keep the pot small. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and reach showdown more easily.

I have top pair (K♦5♣) but a very weak kicker and we’re out of position. Flash checked both flop and turn, which is more often a sign of weakness or a slow‑play — but given his wide opening range and the small pot (600) I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. A small river bet would only get value from a narrow subset of worse hands and will often fold out worse holdings while inducing raises from better kings (KQ/KA) that beat me. Pot‑control and showdown value are preferable here: check and take this to showdown rather than risk being raised off my hand.

I have third pair (Sevens) on a board with two overcards (King and Ten). Minni has checked on every street (Flop, Turn, and River), which suggests she likely has a weak hand, a missed draw, or a marginal hand she's trying to take to showdown cheaply. While I could bet for value, there aren't many worse hands that call me, and a King or Ten will certainly call or even raise. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and win the pot if my pair of Sevens is the best hand, which is quite likely given the action.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with 3♣9♦ — a marginal hand but in heads-up play I should open a very wide range to seize initiative and use position postflop. Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to complete; standard strategy is to raise rather than limp to avoid playing a defined range out of position and to pick up the blind. I’ll make a standard 2x BB open to 200 total: small, economical, preserves stack (I have 12,850) and gives good fold equity. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet (hand has poor equity vs a strong 3-bet); if called, c-bet on many dry flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated boards, realizing equity when appropriate.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h6h, which is a solid suited connector in a heads-up situation. Minni has made a minimum raise to 200, meaning it only costs me 100 more to call and see a flop with a total pot of 300. Given the excellent pot odds (3:1) and the playability of my hand, a call is standard here.

On a flop of 5c-7d-7s, I have an open-ended straight draw with 8h6h (needing a 4 or 9). Checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard play here to see their continuation bet sizing and decide whether to call or raise based on the price offered.

Heads-up I opened preflop and Flash (BB) simply called, so his range is wide. Flop is 5c 7d 7s — paired, low and relatively dry. My 3c9d has no pair and little equity, so I have two main goals: (1) try to take the pot down now with a continuation bet vs missed hands/overcards, and (2) keep the sizing small so I don’t commit huge chips when I’m likely behind to any made pair or set. Flash checked, which is consistent with many weak/wide holdings. I’ll make a small c-bet (~35% of the pot) to fold out hands with a single overcard and to charge any draw. Plan if faced with a substantial raise (large check-raise or shove): fold (I have poor showdown equity). If called, proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate based on the turn card and opponent’s tendencies.

I have an open-ended straight draw (8, 6 on a 5-7-7 board). Minni has made a small continuation bet of 150 into a pot of 400. The pot odds are very favorable (150 to call for a total pot of 700, roughly 21.4%), while my chances of hitting the straight by the river are approximately 32%. Even considering the paired board, which slightly increases the risk of Minni having a full house or a 7, the small sizing makes this a clear call to see the turn and try to realize my equity.

On the turn, the board is 5c-7d-7s-3d. I still have an open-ended straight draw with my 8h6h. The pot is currently 700 chips, and it is my turn to act. Checking is the most appropriate move here as I am out of position and want to see if Minni continues her aggression. If she bets a reasonable amount, I will have the odds to call and try to hit my straight on the river. If I check and she checks back, I get a free card to realize my equity.

Check. The turn 3 gives me the bottom pair (3♣3♦) on a 5♣-7♦-7♠-3♦ board, but Flash called my small flop c-bet which polarizes his range toward many made hands (7x, 5x, pocket pairs) and some floats. My pair is almost always behind the hands that would call the flop, so betting here would have very little fold equity and only bloats a pot (700) where I'm likely behind and can be denied on a river. Checking controls the pot size, keeps weaker hands and bluffs in his range (which I can call on favorable river situations), and lets me fold to aggression if he shows strength. Plan: check now; if Flash bets a small/medium amount on the river I’ll call selectively vs bluffs but fold to large river raises (I have poor stack-to-pot equity to continue against heavy action).

On the river, the board is 5c-7d-7s-3d-6d. I have made a pair of sixes, giving me a final hand of two pair, sevens and sixes, with an eight kicker. Minni showed some weakness by checking the turn after a small flop bet. My pair of sixes now beats all of her unimproved high cards (like Ace-high or King-high) and any pair of fives she might have been betting. However, it is not strong enough to value bet, as she is unlikely to call with a worse hand but will certainly call or raise with a seven or a higher pocket pair. Therefore, checking to reach showdown is the most logical move.

I have only bottom pair (3♣ with 5♣-7♦-7♠-3♦-6♦). Flash called my small flop bet and both of us checked the turn, so his calling range contains many hands that beat me (7x, 5x, pocket pairs, 6x) and some floats. The river 6♦ improves several of those hands and also completes backdoor diamond possibilities. Betting here has very little value — I’m unlikely to get called by worse and will often be called or raised by better. Checking controls the pot (700), takes the showdown for minimal risk, and lets me call small river bluffs but fold to heavy aggression. I’ll check and go to showdown.

I am on the button with 8-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. While I should be opening a wide range of hands from the button in a heads-up scenario, 82o is generally considered a fold. Given my significant stack advantage, I see no reason to play such a weak hand out of position post-flop if called, or to invest chips in a low-equity spot.

Heads-up on the button with J♣5♦ (marginal hand but in position). Pot is 150 and it’s cheap to either call (put total 100) or raise. Against a single big blind who plays aggressively, I want to seize initiative and apply pressure — opening a wide range from the button is standard. A 3x BB open to 300 looks credible on this table (we’ve been opening to 300 frequently) and gives good fold equity while not overcommitting the stack. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold; postflop I’ll play aggressively when I connect (top pair, backdoors) and pot-control/float when I miss. Raise to 300 to take the lead and use position.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. Minni opened to 300 from the Button. Ace-high hands, even with a weak kicker, are standard defending hands in the Big Blind against a button open, especially given the current stack depths. I will call the additional 200 to see a flop and play the hand post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. The flop of 6h 4c 9d is quite dry. I have Ace-high and a backdoor straight draw. While I haven't improved yet, Ace-high still has some showdown value against Minni's button opening range. I will check to see how Minni reacts on this board.

I opened preflop to 300 and Flash just called; pot is 600. Flop 6♥-4♣-9♦ and I hold J♣5♦ — no pair, no realistic straight or flush draw. Heads‑up this is a textbook spot to continuation‑bet to fold out many of the BB’s missed hands and take the initiative. I’ll make a half‑pot c‑bet (300): large enough to apply pressure and fold out unpaired overcards or weak hands, but small enough not to overcommit the stack if called. Plan: if Flash folds we take the pot; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously (give up to a sizable raise, only continue barrels if I improve or pick up a clear read); if he raises significantly on the flop I’ll fold this hand. Bet 300 to seize initiative and deny free cards on a dry board.

On this dry 9-6-4 rainbow board, my Ace-high (Ac 3s) still holds reasonable equity against a wide button opening range. Minni's 300 bet into a 600 pot (half-pot) is a standard continuation bet. I have two overcards to the middle of the board and some backdoor potential. I will call one street to see if I can improve on the turn or if the action checks through, as folding Ace-high immediately on such a disconnected board would be too exploitable.

On the turn Jack of Diamonds, the board is now 9-6-4-J rainbow. I have Ace-high with Ace of Clubs and Trey of Spades. Minni has checked behind on the turn after I checked. This check from Minni often indicates a hand with some showdown value that doesn't want to bet into a potential check-raise, or a complete air hand that is giving up. My Ace-high still has some showdown value and I will check to see the river and potentially reach showdown cheaply or see if I can catch a pair.

I improved to top pair on the turn (J♣ with Jd on board) but with a weak 5 kicker. Flash called my flop c-bet, then checked the turn — his range is wide (9x, 6x, 4x, pocket pairs, floats). This is a clear value/protection spot: bet to extract from worse one-pair hands and charge any backdoor draws while denying free cards. I’ll size ~2/3 pot to get called by medium pairs and weaker jacks but still avoid overcommitting if I face heavy resistance. If he raises large I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold; if he calls I’ll decide on river based on the card and his response.

On the turn Jack of Diamonds, the board is now 9-6-4-J. I have Ace-high (Ac 3s). Minni has bet 800 into a 1200 pot. This bet size (2/3 pot) following a flop continuation bet is quite polarizing. While my Ace-high was worth a call on the flop, the Jack is a good card for a button opening range and my hand has not improved. I only have Ace-high and no real draws. Calling 800 more with just Ace-high on this board texture against a persistent bettor is likely a losing play in the long run. I will fold.

I am on the button with Q4o. In a heads-up situation, Q4o is a marginal but playable hand from the small blind. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on Minni, who has a smaller stack. This is a standard opening range move to steal the blinds or take control of the pot early.

Flash (button/SB) opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with Q♦8♥ — a marginal but playable heads‑up hand. To call I need to put in 200 more (300 total) which is cheap relative to the pot (good pot odds) and I should defend a wide range out of the big blind heads‑up. Q8o is not strong enough to 3‑bet for value out of position (min‑raise to 500 is too small to gain fold equity and would commit me to tricky postflop spots), nor is it worth folding given the price. Plan: call to see a flop, proceed based on board texture (bet/raise with top pair or strong draws, fold to heavy aggression when missed).

I defended Q♦8♥ preflop and on Td 8♠ 9♥ I have second pair (eights) with a decent Q kicker. The flop is very connected and draw‑heavy (many J/7 combos and broadway cards have lots of equity), and I’m out of position. Betting here is thin — it gets called by many worse hands and raised by stronger hands/draws, so I’d rather control the pot and realize equity. Plan: check to keep the pot manageable, call reasonable continuation bets to see a turn, and re‑evaluate if Flash shows heavy aggression (fold to extreme check‑raises/overbets, call smaller bets for value/protection).

I have Queen-high on a board of T-8-9 rainbow. This is a very connected board that likely hits many of Minni's calling ranges (7-6, J-T, 8-7, 9-7, Q-J, etc.). While I have two overcards and an inside straight draw (Jack), the board is too wet to continuation-bet with just Queen-high and no real equity against her calling range. I will check behind to see a free turn and potentially realize equity if a Jack or an overcard comes, or simply to avoid bloating the pot when she is likely to check-raise or call with many pairs and draws.

I have Q♦8♥ — second pair (eights) on T♦‑8♠‑9♥‑A♥. Flash was the preflop aggressor but checked flop and now checked the turn as well, which looks like either a missed hand or a disguised strong hand. The turn Ace is a scary card that gives many of his preflop raising combos (A‑x, T‑x, 9‑x) the advantage, and I’m out of position. Betting here is thin: it will get called by better hands and by worse eights while exposing me to costly check‑raises or turn/river bluffs that are difficult to play OOP. Best play is pot control — check and go to river to reevaluate. Plan: check now, call small river bets for thin value or fold to large aggression; if he suddenly shows strength (big bet/check‑raise) I’ll give it up.

The turn card is an Ace of hearts, which is an excellent card for the pre-flop aggressor's range. However, the board (T-8-9-A) is still very coordinated and likely interacts well with Minni's pre-flop calling range from the big blind (hands like 7-8, 8-9, J-T, or various gutshots). Since I checked the flop, my range looks a bit more capped. Minni has checked to me twice. While I could attempt a bluff here representing the Ace, Minni has shown a tendency to be sticky with pairs. With only Queen-high and very little equity if called, checking back allows me to see a free river and potentially win at showdown if the hand checks through or if I improve.

Board: Td 8s 9h Ah Ac — I hold Q♦8♥ for second pair (eights) with a decent kicker. Flash was the preflop raiser but checked flop/turn/river, which can be either a weak hand or a slow‑play. The river pairing the Ace is the big problem: any A in his range is now trips (or better if he had a pocket pair that pairs the board) and TT/99 become full houses. Betting here for thin value is unattractive — I’ll get called by many better hands and possibly face a check‑raise I must fold to, while the only worse hands that might call a small bet are speculative (Kx/Qx) and don’t make this +EV enough to risk a raise. Pot is small (600) relative to stacks, so preserve chips and take the showdown. Action: check and show down; if he fires a sizable bet I fold.

On a board of T-8-9-A-A, my Queen-high has almost no showdown value, as even Minni's missed draws (like Jack-high) beat me. However, the board is highly coordinated and interacts well with Minni's big blind calling range. While the double Ace on the river is a good card to bluff on, my decision to check both the flop and the turn makes representing an Ace less credible. Minni has shown stickiness in previous hands, and given the small pot, it is unlikely she will fold any pair or even a Jack-high if she suspects a missed draw from me. I will check behind and concede the pot rather than risk a bluff in a low-probability situation.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with Jc7c — a suited J with decent backdoor equity and good playability in position. In heads-up play I should be opening a wide range to take the initiative and use positional advantage postflop. Flash in the big blind has shown a wide and often aggressive defending range, so a raise here will generate fold equity and put him to tough decisions while keeping the pot a manageable size. I’ll raise to the table minimum open (200 total) — it’s a standard, balanced sizing: large enough to apply pressure but not so large that a call from a wide defending range forces me into bloated pots with a marginal holding. Plan: if he folds we take the blinds; if he calls we play postflop in position — c-bet favorable flops, float or check-back when appropriate, and re-evaluate against 3-bets (fold to big 3-bets, consider calling very small 3-bets given backdoor club/straight potential).

I am in the Big Blind with T3o. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind (a 2x raise). The pot is 300 and I need to call 100 more. This hand is quite weak, but in heads-up play, the pot odds are attractive (3:1) to see a flop. However, T3o doesn't play well post-flop and Minni has been aggressive. Given the stacks and the relative weakness of the hand, I'll fold this time and wait for a better spot to defend my blind.

I am on the button with J7o, which is a marginal hand but generally playable in a heads-up situation. My stack is significantly larger than Minni's (26,350 vs 13,500). Raising to take control of the pot and put pressure on the opponent is a standard aggressive strategy here. A standard 3x open is appropriate.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call, so I need ~33% equity to break even — reasonable heads-up. T7o is a marginal hand but has playable equity against a wide opening range and can hit tens or useful draws; I'm out of position postflop, so I don't want to 3-bet light with a dominated hand. I'll defend the blind and see a flop cheaply; plan to proceed only with favorable textures (top pair, two pair, good draws) and fold to heavy aggression on dry A/K-type boards.

I defended the blind preflop with T♣7♦ (T7o) heads-up — marginal but reasonable. The flop J♣-6♥-4♠ is very dry and I’ve completely missed: no pair, no meaningful straight or flush draw (only a remote runner-runner straight). I’m out of position and the hand has very little showdown value, so I don’t want to bloat the pot or float OOP. I’ll check to control the pot and give up to any substantial bet — only consider calling very small probes or raising as a polarized bluff against a specific wide/sticky opponent. Default line is check and fold to real aggression here.

I have flopped top pair with a weak kicker (Jack of Diamonds with a Seven of Clubs) on a relatively dry board of 6h-4s-Jc. Minni has checked to me. I should bet here for value and to protect my hand from overcards or straight draws. A standard continuation bet of about half the pot is appropriate.

Facing a 300 bet into a 900 pot (I must call 300 → ~25% break-even). I have T♦7♥ on J♣-6♥-4♠: no pair, only a small backdoor heart and 6 clean outs to make a pair (≈24% to pair by river) — plus a tiny backdoor flush chance, but that’s unreliable. Even if I hit a pair I’m often behind the kinds of hands Flash will c-bet with (Jx, big broadways, pairs/overcards). I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal, often-dominated hand. A call is borderline and will likely face further aggression; a raise is a thin bluff without good blockers. Preserve stack and fold here.

I’m on the button (SB) with 2♠4♦ — a very weak, non-connected off‑suit hand with poor playability. Although it’s only 50 to call, this hand is frequently dominated and will create difficult post‑flop decisions versus a deep, often‑defending big blind. Open‑raising with 2‑4o is too loose and calling to see a flop is likely negative EV long‑term. Preserve chips and fold this poor spot.

I am on the button with Ten of Diamonds and Deuce of Diamonds. While T2s is a relatively weak hand, in a heads-up situation, suited hands have some potential. I have a significant chip lead over Minni (26,700 vs 13,150). Based on previous hands, I have established a pattern of raising to 300 from the button to apply pressure. I will continue this strategy to try and pick up the pot pre-flop or see a flop with initiative.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a 3x open — 7♠8♥ is a playable, connected hand that plays well vs a wide opening range. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200, so my break-even equity is ~33%. Versus Flash’s frequent 3x open my hand has enough equity to continue. I don’t want to 3-bet light and build a bloated pot out of position with a marginal hand, so I’ll just defend by calling and see a flop where I can fold to extreme aggression or extract value when I hit. Call to 300 total.

Heads‑up vs an aggressive 3x opener. I defended preflop with 7♠8♥, but the flop K♠ J♥ 4♦ completely misses my hand. I have no pair, no realistic flush draw and only tiny runner‑runner straight outs — essentially negligible equity versus a c‑betting range that contains many Ks, Js and broadways. I don’t want to voluntarily build a pot out of position with air by leading or check‑raising as a bluff. Best line is to check and either take a free showdown if he checks, or fold to a standard continuation bet. I’ll check and fold to any substantial bet (maybe call a very small probe, but otherwise preserve stack and wait for a better spot).

On a board of J-4-K with one diamond, I have a back-door flush draw and some potential to represent a strong range as the pre-flop raiser. Minni has checked to me. While I only have ten-high, this is a good board texture to fire a continuation bet to test her strength and try to take the pot down immediately. A small bet of half the pot (300) is consistent with my previous aggressive play and puts her in a tough spot if she hasn't connected with the jack or king.

I defended preflop with 7♠8♥ vs a wide button open, which is fine heads‑up. The flop K♠ J♥ 4♦ completely misses me: no pair, no meaningful draw (no flush draw and only a runner‑runner straight possibility). Flash bets 300 into the pot (pot now 900); calling 300 requires ~25% equity. Against his c‑betting range (lots of Kx/Jx and broadways in position) my equity is well below that threshold. Raising as a bluff out of position with no blockers is costly and unnecessary. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with A♣Q♣ — a top-tier, positionally powerful hand. I want to take the initiative preflop, build the pot with a hand that plays very well postflop in position, and deny Flash easy odds to see a flop for free. A standard 3x BB sizing (raise to 300 total) achieves that: it’s big enough to extract value and fold out weaker hands, but not so large as to commit an outsized portion of my 12,900 stack in case of a 3-bet. If Flash 3-bets I can reassess with position and stack depth; if he calls, I’ll have position and a strong range advantage. Action: raise to 300.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 2c, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Minni has raised to 3x the big blind from the Button. Although it's heads-up, this hand is not worth a defend even with a large stack advantage. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 8d 6s. While this is a weak hand, it is playable in a heads-up situation from the small blind against a shorter-stacked opponent. Minni has approximately 130BB while I have 270BB. A standard open-raise to 300 has been my consistent sizing and helps maintain pressure and pick up the blinds, or at least see a flop with initiative.

I’m in the big blind with 6♦3♠ facing a button open to 300 (3bb). Calling 200 requires ~33% equity to be profitable; 6-3 offsuit is severely dominated by a typical open-raising range and plays poorly out of position. Flash has shown frequent aggression and willingness to barrel, so calling or min-raising will put me in difficult postflop spots with a weak hand and deep stacks. Preserve stack for better spots — fold preflop.

Heads-up on the button with T♥3♦. Pot is 150 and I need to put 50 more (my total commit will be 100). I will call. Rationale: T3o is too weak to open-raise thinly — a min-raise to 200 invites 3-bets or builds a pot I don't want to play big without real equity. Calling is cheap (50 to win ~200, good pot odds) and preserves positional advantage to fold to heavy aggression on bad flops or to extract value / apply pressure on favorable boards. If Flash 3-bets strongly I’ll fold; otherwise I’ll evaluate on the flop and play for pot control.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven suited, a very strong hand in a heads-up context. Minni has limped from the Small Blind, which often indicates a weaker or more speculative range. I want to take the initiative and build the pot with my equity advantage. A raise to 400 (an increase of 300 over the current blind) is a standard isolation size to put pressure on the limper and gain control of the hand.

Facing a raise to 400 after I completed the small blind. I hold T♥3♦ — a weak, offsuit hand with limited equity. Calling the additional 300 (total commit 400) is cheap relative to the pot and my stack, and preserves my positional advantage. A 3-bet here would be thin and likely to get me into difficult spots vs Flash’s deeper stack; folding surrenders too much of the button. Plan: call to see a flop, fold to a sizable 4-bet, and play postflop for pot control — only continue aggressively if I pair the Ten/3 or get a favorable board to float/pressure.

On this flop of 8s-Ts-Jd, I have an overcard (Ace) and a nut flush draw (7s-As on a two-spade board). The board is quite coordinated, which means it likely hits a limping-calling range well (hands like Q9, T9, J9, etc.). However, with my equity from the nut flush draw and the back-door straight potential, I have a very strong semi-bluffing candidate. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and have the range advantage as the Big Blind raiser, I will lead out with a continuation bet to build the pot and potentially take it down immediately or realize my equity on later streets.

Flop: 8♠ T♠ J♦ — I have top pair (T♥) with a very weak kicker (3♦). Flash’s bet is small (400 into a 1200 pot), which in heads-up often represents a wide c-bet range of missed overcards, draws and thin value hands. Calling 400 to win ~1600 gives ~20% break-even equity — my TP easily has >20% against a c-bet range and I keep the advantage of position. plan: call to control the pot and see the turn; fold to a large turn overbet/raise that polarizes to very strong hands, but if I improve (second Ten, trips, or a favorable card and a check from villain) I will extract value. If turn bricks and villain checks, I can probe for value/fold equity.

The turn card, the Jack of Spades, is an incredible card for me as it completes my nut flush. While the board is now paired, which introduces the possibility of a full house (J8, J10, 88, or TT), the likelihood of Minni holding exactly those hands in a limped pot compared to the rest of her calling range is relatively low. I must bet for value to extract chips from hands like trips (any Jack), straights (Q9), or even a lower flush if she has one. A bet of 1100 into a 1600 pot (approx 70% pot) is a strong value sizing that builds the pot while still being likely to get called by her sticky range.

Board on the turn: 8♠ T♠ J♦ J♠. My hand T♥3♦ is now two pair (Jacks and Tens). Pot is 2700 and Flash bet 1100 (to call 1100). Assessment: my two-pair is a reasonably strong hand heads-up but vulnerable to trips (any J), higher two-pair made from pocket QQ+/KK/AA, and any made spade flush (there are three spades on board). Flash has been aggressive preflop and on the flop; his turn sizing is modest (~40% pot), which can include both value and bluffs/semi-bluffs. Pot odds on a call are ~28–30% break-even — my two-pair has good equity vs a wide continuing range, while raising here would polarize my range and often get called by hands that beat me (trips/flush/QQ+). Plan: call the 1100 to control the pot, realize equity and re-evaluate on the river. If the river brings a scary card (a spade or a J) and Flash bets big, I will strongly consider folding; if the river is relatively blank and Flash checks, I will look to extract thin value.

The river 5h is a complete blank that does not change the strength of my hand. I have the nut flush (Ace-high flush) on a paired board. While a full house is theoretically possible (J8, JT, 88, TT), Minni's line of limping pre-flop and calling three streets suggests she is more likely to have trips (a Jack), a straight (Q9), or a lower flush. I have been the aggressor the entire hand, and I must continue betting for value. A bet of 2600 into the 3800 pot is a strong value sizing designed to get called by any Jack or straight in her range, maximizing my profit while still being a reasonable amount for her to call.

Board: 8♠ T♠ J♦ J♠ 5♥. I hold T♥3♦ — two pair (Jacks and Tens). Pot is 6400 and I must call 2600. Calling requires ~29% equity. Against Flash’s wide heads-up, aggressive line (raise preflop, c-bet flop, bet turn, bet river), my two-pair has substantial showdown value versus a lot of bluffs/semi-bluffs and thin value hands. The hands that beat me are mainly: any J (trips/full house), pocket Tens (full house), or a spade flush. Those combos exist but are a minority of a typical aggressive continuation/river-betting range heads-up. Raising would dangerously polarize my range (getting called by the very hands that beat me or folding out worse hands and losing EV) and folding loses positive EV given my pot odds and hand strength. Therefore I will call the 2600 to realize equity and see showdown rather than inflate the pot further.

I am holding 5h 2c on the button. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While I have a significant chip lead over Minni, there is no reason to enter the pot with such a poor hand, especially since Minni has shown a willingness to defend the blinds and call raises in recent hands. Folding is the most prudent play here.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with J♥2♠ — a marginal hand but perfectly playable in position. Pot is 150 and I face 50 to call. Versus one opponent I want to take the initiative and use my positional advantage, so I’ll open a standard-sized raise to apply pressure while risking very little of my 84.5bb stack. A raise to 300 (total) gives fold equity, keeps the pot manageable if Flash 3-bets, and lets me play postflop in position against a wide range. If he 3-bets big I can fold; if he calls I can evaluate the flop and continue with c-bets or pot control as appropriate.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8-7 offsuit. Minni, who is the shorter stack, raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Given the 2:1 pot odds and the nature of heads-up play, 87o is a standard hand to defend by calling. I will see a flop and look for connectivity or pair potential.

On this flop of Qh-5h-Td, I have 8s-7c, which is a total miss. There are two high cards and a heart draw possible. Minni raised pre-flop and is now checking to me. While I could try to bluff here, Minni's stack is relatively short (8200) compared to the blinds and recent history suggests they can be sticky. I have zero equity or backdoor draws. I will check back and see if I can catch a lucky turn or just give up on the hand.

I raised preflop and Flash just flat-called, so his calling range is wide (pairs, Qx/Tx, broadways, some draws). On Qh-5h-Td I have Jh2s: no pair, a single heart (only a runner-runner backdoor flush) and limited showdown value. As the preflop aggressor I should take the initiative and apply pressure, but keep the pot controllable with a standard half-pot continuation bet. Betting 300 (~1/2 pot) folds out lots of missed hands and gets value from worse Tens/Queens and draws that will call; it also preserves stack depth if he calls. Plan if called: proceed cautiously on the turn (check/ pot-control unless I improve). If he check-raises big on the flop I will fold — this hand has poor equity versus an aggressive check-raise. Bet half-pot to leverage position and fold equity while minimizing risk.

I have 8-high with no draws on a board containing two overcards (Queen and Ten). Minni bet 300 into a 600 pot after I checked. Since I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw, I have zero equity in this hand. Continuing would be a waste of chips, especially since Minni's short stack makes them more likely to be committed to the pot. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have been dealt pocket Queens, which is a premium hand. Given I am on the button in a heads-up situation, I want to raise for value and build a pot while I have a significant equity advantage. A standard opening raise of 300 chips is consistent with my previous play and puts pressure on the big blind.

Heads-up vs a standard 3x open to 300 from the button — Flash is opening a wide range here. I have K♦4♠: not strong enough to 3-bet for value out of position, but it has reasonable equity vs a wide opening range. The pot is 400 and I must call 200; calling 200 to win ~600 (pot after my call) gives ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity). K4o has better than that vs a wide opener, so a call is profitable and preserves stack depth. Plan: see a flop cheaply, proceed if I hit a king or a good draw (bet for value or continue), fold to heavy aggression on dry boards where I miss. Avoid bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand by 3-betting here. Action is to call and go to the flop.

I have top pair (K) with a weak kicker on a fairly dry K–J–3 board heads-up. Flash opened wide preflop, so his range includes a lot of worse Kx, Jx and broadways that will call a reasonable bet. Betting ~50% pot (300 into 600) extracts value from worse pairs, charges any backdoor outs, and denies free cards; checking hands off hands lets the button take initiative and realize equity cheaply. Plan: bet for value now; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue for value/protection if I improve or the turn is safe, but proceed cautiously to large, polarized raises.

I have pocket Queens, a very strong overpair pre-flop. On a flop of K-J-3, there is one overcard (King). Minni's lead of 300 into a 600 pot is small. While the King is in her range, my hand is still very strong and ahead of many hands she might lead with (Jacks, draws, or air). I will call to see how the turn develops and evaluate if she continues showing strength. Raising here might bloat the pot unnecessarily if she actually has the King.

I have trip kings (Kd plus Kh and Kc on board) on the turn — a very strong holding heads-up. Flash opened wide preflop and called my flop bet, so his calling range contains a lot of worse Kx, Jx and broadways that will pay to see another card. Betting the turn extracts value from those worse hands and charges any backdoor draws before the river (and avoids giving a free card that could pair J or 3 to make a full house for some holdings). The pot is 1,200; I size to ~2/3 pot to maximize value while keeping worse hands in the pot. Plan: bet 800 now; if called I’ll re-evaluate the river and size for thin value. If Flash check‑raises very large (polarized), I will assume a full house and proceed cautiously (fold or re-evaluate based on sizing), but against normal calls/raises I keep building for value.

I have pocket Queens on a board of 3-K-J-K. While the second King on the turn is a scary card, it also reduces the likelihood that Minni has a King in her hand (only two remaining). Minni led on the flop and is now leading for 800 into a 1200 pot. My hand is still very strong and beats all Jacks, smaller pocket pairs, and straight draws (like QT or T9). Given Minni's aggressive history, raising here would likely only be called by a King or better, whereas calling allows her to continue bluffing on the river or allows me to reach a relatively cheap showdown if she has a Jack. I will call the 800.

I have Kd on 3c Kh Jd Kc 5d — trips. Flash opened wide preflop and called both my flop and turn bets, so his calling range contains a lot of worse Kx, Jx and missed draws. The river is a blank that only turns pocket JJ/33/55 into full houses — a small portion of his range. Checking risks giving up thin value from many worse hands; betting extracts value. Pot is 2800; I size for thin value and to keep worse hands calling: bet 2000 (~70% pot). If Flash check‑raises very large (polarized) I’ll re‑evaluate — such sizing usually represents a full house and I would proceed cautiously (fold to an all‑in, call small/medium raises).

I have pocket Queens on a board of 3-K-J-K-5. This gives me two pair, Kings and Queens. Minni has led out on every street since the flop. Her range includes Kings (which beat me), Jacks (which I beat), and missed straight draws like QT or T9 (which I also beat). Given Minni's history of aggression and the fact that the second King on the turn makes it mathematically less likely she holds one, my hand is a very strong bluff catcher. I am getting roughly 2.4:1 odds on a call (2000 to win a total pot of 6800). Given her tendency to bet wide, I believe my Queens are good here more than 30% of the time. Raising would only be called by a King or better, so calling is the most profitable action.

6♣2♥ is basically junk. We’re on the button (small blind) and can see a flop for a tiny additional 50 chips. Folding would be too nitty in heads‑up; raising to 200 is a pure bluff here and Flash (BB) has a deep stack and defends wide, so a raise will get called or 3‑bet often and force us to play a bloated pot out of position with a terrible hand. Calling completes cheaply (pot odds ~3:1) and preserves position postflop — plan to continue only with a real pair, strong draw, or a favorable board texture and otherwise fold to heavy aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with KJo, a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Minni limped from the Small Blind, which often indicates a wider, weaker range. I will raise to 400 to take the initiative and build a pot with my equity advantage.

Heads‑up on the button with 6♣2♥ — a very weak hand. Flash (BB) is deep and has shown a tendency to defend and raise wide, so a shove/bluff‑raise will often get called or 3‑bet and leave me out of position with garbage. Pot is 500 and I must call 300 more (400 total committed) — that gives me break‑even equity of 300/800 = 37.5%. Calling is the best low‑risk, high‑flexibility play: I preserve position and can fold to heavy aggression on unimproved boards, but still continue when I hit a pair or a strong draw or when the texture allows profitable bluff attempts. I will call to see the flop, plan to fold to significant continuation aggression unless I improve (pair/two‑pair/straight draw), and avoid bloating the pot with this junk hand.

I have King-high with two overcards and some backdoor straight potential. As the pre-flop aggressor, I hold the range advantage on this Queen-high board. I will place a standard continuation bet of 400 (half the pot) to maintain initiative and put pressure on Minni's range, which consists of many hands that missed this flop.

Call 400. I flopped a pair of sixes (6♣) — I’ve improved from preflop and that hand has real showdown value heads‑up, but it’s not strong enough to polarize the pot by raising. Flash (BB) has been wide and aggressive preflop and on the flop his 400 into the current pot represents a fairly wide range (bluffs, worse pairs, some Qx/8x that beat me). Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves position, and gives me correct pot‑odds (400/(1200+400)=25% break‑even) — against his wide range I have sufficient equity to continue. Plan: evaluate the turn — fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve or the board becomes worse; if I improve to two‑pair/trips or he shows weakness on the turn, I’ll extract value.

The turn card is the Ace of Hearts, which is an excellent card for my range as the pre-flop raiser and flop bettor. I have more Aces in my range (AJ, AK, AQ, AT) than Minni, who limped and called a raise. Although I currently only have King-high, the board texture is favorable for a double barrel. A bet here keeps the pressure on her mid-range hands like 8x, 6x, or small pocket pairs that might have called the flop. Given the pot size is 1600, a bet of 1000 (roughly 62% pot) should be effective in fold equity while allowing me to represent the Ace convincingly.

I’ve got middle pair (6♣) on Q♣–8♠–6♦–A♥. Flash is very aggressive and opened/CB'd a wide range earlier, so his turn bet of 1,000 can contain a lot of bluffs/worse pairs as well as Ax/Qx that beat me. Pot is 2,600 and calling 1,000 commits me to a 3,600 final pot (break‑even equity ≈ 27.8%). Heads‑up my pair of sixes should have at least that equity against a wide betting range, so folding would be too nitpicky. Raising here is thin value (an Ace or Queen will call; I’ll only fold out some bluffs) and just bloats the pot with a vulnerable hand. I’ll call 1,000 to keep the pot manageable, use position on the river to evaluate, fold to heavy river aggression if I don’t improve, and extract value or raise only if the river gives me a clear two‑pair/trips or he shows weakness.

I am holding King-high on a board of 6-8-Q-A-4. I have been the aggressor throughout the hand, raising pre-flop and betting both the flop and the turn. The Ace on the turn is a significant scare card that heavily favors my range as the pre-flop raiser. Although Minni called my turn bet, her range is capped at marginal hands like a Queen, an 8, or a weak Ace, as she likely would have raised earlier with a monster. A river bet completes the story of a strong Ace (like AK or AJ) and puts maximum pressure on her middle-pair hands. Based on previous hands (like Hand #10), Minni has shown the ability to fold to river aggression. I will bet 2200 into the 3600 pot to maximize fold equity against her likely holdings of Qx or 8x.

Call 2200. The pot is 5800 and calling 2200 commits me to a final pot of 8000, so my break‑even equity is ~27.5% (2200/8000). Heads‑up Flash has been very wide and aggressive this hand (flop & turn barrels and another small/medium river raise), so his continuing range contains a lot of bluffs/missed hands and weaker pairs as well as Ax/Qx/8x value hands. My pair of sixes (admittedly with a weak kicker) has reasonable showdown equity vs a wide barreling range and the river bet is a modest ~38% pot-sized probe — good pot odds to call. Raising is thin and will either fold out some bluffs but get called/raised by the hands that beat me (and I have poor kicker/blockers), and folding is too tight given the price. I’ll call to see showdown.

I have T9s on the button in a heads-up situation against Minni. T9s is a very strong starting hand in heads-up play, offering great straight and flush potential. Given the stack depths and my position, I want to play a pot and apply pressure. Raising is the standard play here to take control of the hand.

Heads-up in the big blind facing a button open to 300. I hold 9♦6♥ — a marginal offsuit hand but not worthless heads-up, and stacks are deep (≈161bb). Calling costs 200 into a 400 pot (pot after my call 600), so I’m getting ~33% pot odds; against a wide button opening range 9-6o has roughly borderline-to-acceptable equity and some postflop playability (pair + straight/backdoor possibilities). I do not want to 3-bet — this hand lacks fold equity and playability OOP, and a min-raise would bloat a big pot out of position. Folding is too tight and forfeits good implied odds. Plan: call to see the flop, play cautiously postflop — proceed only when I pair or hit strong draws, otherwise fold to heavy aggression.

Flop Kc-4s-5h, I hold 9♦6♥ — missed the flop with no real draw. I defended preflop correctly given heads-up implied odds, but out of position on a K-high board I’m behind most of the raiser’s continuing range (Kx, overpairs, strong Ax). Priority is pot control with deep stacks. Check to keep the pot small and avoid bloating a pot with air. Plan: fold to any meaningful c-bet, only consider calling very small bets (≈≤200 ≈1/3 pot) to realize equity, and never lead or check-raise here since I lack fold equity and showdown value.

In Hand #111, I have Ten-high on a King-high board after raising pre-flop. The board of 4s-Kc-5h is relatively dry and generally favors the pre-flop aggressor, as I have more Kings in my range. Since Minni checked to me, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet to apply pressure and pick up the pot immediately. My hand also has a small amount of backdoor equity with the Ten of spades. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to represent strength.

I face a c-bet of 300 into the current pot (Pot=900, To Call=300). Break-even equity on a call is 300/(900+300)=25%. I hold 9♦6♥ on Kc-4s-5h: no pair, no meaningful straight or flush draw, so my hand equity vs a button’s continuation range is well below the 25% threshold. I’m out of position and stacks are deep, so I don’t want to bloat a pot with air or chase marginal equities. Per my pre-flop/flop plan (check and fold to meaningful bets), this is a clear fold — preserve chips and wait for a better spot or a cheaper float opportunity.

Heads-up on the button with pocket Tens — a very strong hand in position. Calling 50 (completing the blind) is an option, but I want to take the initiative, isolate the BB and build a pot in position. 3xBB to 300 is my standard heads-up open here (min-raise 200 is too small and gives the BB good odds); 300 extracts value from worse hands (broadways, weaker pairs, suited connectors) while still keeping worse hands in. If Flash 3-bets a normal size I will typically call (TT plays well deep); I’ll re-evaluate/fold to an unusually large 4-bet depending on sizing. Raise to 300 total.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Four offsuit. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. While this is a heads-up situation where ranges are wider, J4o is a very weak hand to play out of position. Given that Minni has been active and willing to call down bets in recent hands, I prefer to wait for a stronger hand rather than defending with such a marginal holding.

I am on the button with T4o, which is a weak hand. However, in heads-up play, the button should open frequently. I have a significant stack lead over Minni (23,950 vs 15,900). Looking at recent hands, Minni has been calling many pre-flop raises but folding to continuation bets (e.g., Hand #111, Hand #103, Hand #101). Given the current pot odds and the need to maintain pressure, I will make a standard raise to 300 to see if I can take the pot pre-flop or set up a post-flop play.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the button. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 (total 300 from my stack). I have 4♦7♣ — unsuited, disconnected and a marginal holding, but heads‑up the button’s open range is very wide. Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives me ~25% pot‑odds; 4‑7o has roughly that equity (and ~32% to pair the flop), so a flat call is the lowest‑variance, +EV decision here. I don’t want to bloat a deep, out‑of‑position pot with a weak hand by 3‑betting. Plan: call to see the flop, play for pot control — check and fold to strong, committed aggression on dry boards; continue only with a pair, two pair/trips, or strong draws, and look for cheap turn/river realization or selective bluffing opportunities.

I have 4♦7♣ on A♣ K♥ 7♥ — bottom pair with a terrible kicker and I’m out of position vs the preflop raiser. This is a dry, high-card flop that hits the raiser’s range (lots of A’s, K’s and broadways) so many continuation bets will be strong. I don’t want to bloat a pot OOP with a marginal holding, so I’ll check and control the pot. Plan: let Flash show aggression — call small c-bets to realize equity (look for a 7 or backdoor improvements) and fold to heavy, committed raises or pot-sized bets. If he checks back I’ll reevaluate the turn; if I improve I’ll extract value, otherwise keep pot small.

The board is ACE-KING-SEVEN, which is heavily favored toward me as the pre-flop raiser. I have a significant range advantage here because I would raise all strong Ace and King combinations pre-flop. Minni checked, showing weakness. Even though I only have Ten-high, this is a prime opportunity for a continuation bet. Minni has recently demonstrated a pattern of folding to post-flop aggression on boards like this (Hands #111 and #103). I will bet 300 to pressure her off her weaker holdings and take down the pot immediately.

I have bottom pair (7♣) with a terrible kicker, out of position vs the button who opened pre‑flop. Flash’s bet is small (300 into a 900 pot) — that sizing is consistent with a wide c‑bet and gives me ~25% pot odds to call. Raising OOP with a weak pair is too thin and folding now would be too nitty given the odds and my hand can improve. Plan: call to keep the pot manageable, realize equity and see the turn; fold to heavy turn/river aggression unless I improve to two pair/trips or pick up a clear read to continue.

I hold 4♦7♣ — bottom pair with a terrible kicker on a A♣ K♥ 7♥ 2♣ board. I’m out of position versus an opponent who opens and c-bets very wide, so his continuing range contains a lot of A’s, K’s and broadways that dominate my hand. The turn is a blank for my equity and a bet OOP would often get called by better hands (and fold out hands I could bluff-catch on the river). I want to keep the pot small, realize equity, and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal holding. Plan: check for pot control and to induce bluffs; call small turn/river bets if the price is right, but fold to large/polarized aggression unless I improve to two-pair or trips or pick up a strong read.

I am continuing the narrative of having a strong hand on a board that heavily favors my pre-flop raising range (Ace-King high). Although Minni called my flop continuation bet, she has shown a pattern in previous hands (like Hand #98 and Hand #71) of folding to a second barrel on the turn when she doesn't have a premium holding. The turn card (Deuce of Clubs) is a brick that shouldn't have improved her range if she was calling with a King, a Seven, or a draw. By betting 800 into a 1200 pot, I am applying significant pressure to her likely middle-pair or marginal hands, which aligns with my goal of maximizing my stack through controlled aggression on favorable boards.

I hold 7♣4♦ — bottom pair with a terrible kicker, out of position on A♣ K♥ 7♥ 2♣. Pot is 2,000 and Flash bet 800, so calling 800 would win ~2,800 (≈28.6% break‑even). My direct outs to improve to two‑pair/trips are small (6 outs → ≈13% to improve), but this hand also has showdown value vs missed c‑bet/bluff hands and Flash has shown a wide, aggressive opening/c‑betting pattern. Raising OOP with a weak pair is thin and will often be dominated; folding here is too nitpicky given the price and villain’s range. I’ll call to keep the pot manageable and realize equity. Plan for the river: if I pair a 7 or 4 (two‑pair/trips) I’ll extract value; if I miss and face large, polarized river aggression I’ll fold, but I can call small/medium river bets or check back versus a checked river.

I have 7♣4♦ — bottom pair with a very bad kicker on 7♥ A♣ K♥ 2♣ Q♥. Flash was the preflop opener and barreled flop and turn, so his continuing range is heavy in Ax/Kx and broadways; his check on the river looks like weakness or pot‑control with a made top pair rather than a willingness to get stacks in. Betting here for thin value is unattractive — worse hands that would call (some Jx/T9 etc.) are marginal and many of his check‑back hands actually beat me (Qx, Kx, Ax). A bet will mostly fold out pure air and get called by hands that beat me, so it’s often negative EV. I’ll check back to realize showdown equity, keep the pot small, and avoid being bluffed‑away or check‑raised into ugly spots. If he were to raise now (unlikely after checking), I’d fold to large aggression since my kicker is terrible.

I have Ten-high with no showdown value. The board is very coordinated (Ace, King, Queen, and a possible flush). While I have the Ten of Hearts as a blocker to both the straight and the flush, Minni has called two streets of aggression on a very high-card board, suggesting she has at least a King or an Ace. Given Minni's history of calling down in previous hands (such as Hand #110), a third-barrel bluff here is high-risk and unlikely to succeed against her range. I will check and concede the pot to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with T♦7♦. In heads-up play this is a playable, suited tens hand and I should be opening a wide range from the button to seize initiative and exploit position post-flop. Calling 50 is cheap, but raising to a standard 3x BB (300 total) maximizes fold equity, keeps my opening sizing consistent with my previous raises, and gives me the lead in most heads-up pots. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll treat this as a fold-heavy spot with this marginal holding; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops or control pot size. Overall a 300 open is the highest EV line here.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine offsuit. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. K9o is a strong enough hand to defend my blind against a standard 3x open in a heads-up scenario. I will call the additional 200 to see the flop, as my hand has decent high-card value and playability.

I have flopped top pair with a King and a nine kicker. The board is relatively safe, though there is a spade draw present. I am out of position. Standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and see how they react. If Minni bets, I will most likely call or raise depending on the sizing, as top pair is a very strong hand in heads-up play.

Heads-up I was the preflop aggressor and this flop (2h Ks 4s) is very dry. I missed with T♦7♦ (no pair, no spade/diamond draw) but my opening range contains Kx and other strong hands, so a continuation bet here will often take the pot or fold out unpaired hands. I’ll use a small c-bet (~1/3 pot = 200) to preserve fold equity while keeping the pot controllable with this marginal holding. If Flash folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn and proceed cautiously. If he check-raises big I’ll fold this hand.

I have flopped top pair with King-Nine offsuit on a board of K-4-2 with two spades. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Minni has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a 600 pot (offering 4:1 odds). There is no reason to fold, and while raising is an option to protect against the spade draw, calling allows Minni to continue bluffing with weaker hands or air. Given the board texture and the small bet size, I will call to keep the pot manageable and see the turn, maintaining the strength of my hand in my calling range.

I have top pair with a King on a fairly dry board, although a flush draw is present. I am out of position against Minni. After calling her small continuation bet on the flop, the Jack on the turn doesn't change the board state significantly. I will continue with a check to control the pot size and allow Minni to potentially bluff or bet with a wider range of hands that I currently beat. Top pair with a nine kicker is a strong holding in heads-up play, but it functions well as a check-call hand to navigate the rest of the streets.

I'm on the button with T♦7♦ — I missed the flop and the turn (2h K♠ 4♠ J♦) gives me no pair and no realistic draw. Important point: the flop had no diamonds, so even with two diamonds in my hand I cannot make a flush by the river here. Flash called my flop c-bet, so his calling range is weighted toward Kx, pocket pairs, Jx, and other hands that beat me; his turn check looks like pot-control or a passive call. Betting the turn would be a thin bluff with very limited fold equity and would unnecessarily inflate a pot I’m a huge dog in; checking controls the pot and lets me see a free river to possibly realize showdown or bluff on a better card. Action: check.

On the river, the board is 2h-Ks-4s-Jd-8s, giving me top pair with a nine kicker. The turn went check-check after Minni's small continuation bet on the flop was called. Her check on the turn often indicates a marginal hand like a Jack, a small pocket pair, or a draw. While the river 8s completes a potential spade flush, my King is still very likely the best hand given the passive turn action. I will place a value bet of 500 (half the pot) to extract chips from a Jack, an Eight, or a mid-pocket pair that might check back if I check, but will likely call a standard-sized bet.

Heads-up on the button with T♦7♦. I opened, c-bet the dry flop and was called. Turn was checked by both of us. River (2♥ K♠ 4♠ J♦ 8♠) brings a third spade and Flash now bets 500 into a 1000 pot (pot is 1500 with his bet). Calling 500 to win 1500 gives me 3:1 pot odds (≈25% equity needed). I have no pair, no spade, and no realistic river outs — essentially 0% equity against a value-heavy range. Flash’s line (call flop, check turn, half-pot river bet) is exactly what you’d expect with Kx, Jx, pocket pairs and completed spade flushes — hands that beat me. I also don’t have useful blockers to make a credible river bluff (I hold two diamonds, not spades or a king/J), so raising as a bluff has very thin fold equity and is a high-variance play I should avoid here. With essentially zero showdown equity and only marginal chance he’s bluffing, the correct, lowest-risk +EV play is to fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 3h 4s, a very weak hand. In a heads-up situation, while I have position, this hand has very little equity and poor playability post-flop. Given the stack sizes and the hand's weakness, folding is the most disciplined and standard play to avoid unnecessary losses.

Heads-up on the button with T8o is a very playable, low-to-medium strength hand — in heads-up you should be opening a wide range. Pot is 150 and to-call is only 50; I want to take the initiative and use position postflop. A standard 2x BB open (raise to 200 total) applies pressure to the BB, keeps my range wide, and lets me continue on many favorable flops. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this holding; if he calls I’ll c-bet on dry/advantageous textures and proceed carefully on dangerous boards. Given stack depth (≈169 BB) and opponent tendencies, raise to 200 is the +EV play.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs7s, a solid suited gapper. Minni, who is on the Button/Small Blind, has opened for 2x (200 total). I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. Given the suitedness and the Queen high, this is a standard defend against a small open from the button. I will call to see a flop.

On a board of 9d 9s 4s, I have a Queen-high flush draw and two overcards to the 4. Since I am out of position as the Big Blind against the pre-flop raiser, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. If Minni bets, I have plenty of equity to continue, either by calling or semi-bluffing with a raise, but for now, checking is the best way to start the action.

I was the preflop aggressor but on this flop (9d 9s 4s) my Th8c has no pair and no meaningful draw — essentially air. This is a paired board where a c‑bet will often be called by small pairs, 9x, and many of the hands that called preflop, and I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with virtually zero equity. With deep stacks I prefer pot control and to keep options open: check to see what Flash does. If he gives me a small probe I can consider a fold or a very selective call based on sizing; if he checks back I get a free turn to realize my equity and can attempt a delayed bluff on favorable cards. Therefore I’ll check and avoid committing chips with air.

On the turn 7h, I have improved to a pair of sevens to go along with my Queen-high flush draw on a paired board of nines. Since the flop was checked through, it is unlikely Minni has a nine or a strong overpair, though she could be slow-playing. By checking again, I allow her to potentially bluff with her air or bet with marginal hands that I now beat. Given my significant equity with the flush draw and the pair of sevens, I am comfortable seeing a river for free or calling a reasonably sized bet.

Check. I have T8o on 9d 9s 4s 7h — no pair and no realistic draw. Flash checked both flop and turn, which indicates weakness (or a very rare trap). Betting the turn here would be a thin, high-variance bluff with very little equity and the risk of a large raise on deep stacks. I prefer pot control and to see a free river: that preserves chips, lets me realize my tiny river equity, and keeps the option to make a targeted delayed bluff on a favorable river card (or fold to aggression). If Flash leads the river I’ll fold most sizes; if he checks again I can attempt a small steal on a blank or value/bluff appropriately if I pair.

On a board of 9d-9s-4s-7h-Qd, I have ended up with two pair (Queens and Sevens). The flop and turn both checked through, suggesting Minni is unlikely to have a nine or a strong overpair. The Queen on the river gives me top pair to go with my pair of sevens. Since the action has been passive, I will lead out with a value bet to get called by hands like mid-pocket pairs (88, 66, 55) or an Ace-high that might think I am bluffing a missed spade draw.

I was the preflop aggressor but purposely checked flop and turn to control the pot with T8o. On the river Flash leads for 300 into a 700 pot after two checks — calling 300 to win 1000 requires ~30% equity. I have no pair and essentially no showdown equity against a realistic betting range (Qx, 4x trips, 7x, some 9x/full house combos and thin value hands); I only beat pure bluffs. A raise would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to fold enough of his value hands and exposes me to a shove. Given the line, sizing and my lack of equity, folding is the lowest-variance, +EV choice here.

I am on the button with 5d 6d, which is a solid speculative hand in heads-up play. Given my stack lead and the fact that Minni has shown some tendency to fold to pre-flop aggression or play cautiously post-flop, I will make a standard opening raise to 300 to take the initiative.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the button heads-up, Qc5s is marginal but defendable from the big blind. Calling 200 more (total commit 300) gives ~3:1 pot odds and reasonable equity vs a wide BTN opening range; raising would bloat the pot with a weak hand and folding is too nitty heads-up. Plan: see the flop and fold to large, non-giveaway continuation bets on very dry boards if I miss; continue (call or raise) when I pair the queen/5, pick up strong draws, or improve to two pair/sets. Overall defend and play the flop for position/reads rather than 3-betting here.

I have top pair (Qc) with a very weak 5 kicker on a fairly dry 2h-4s-Qh flop. I defended preflop expecting to see this board and I'm out of position versus a wide, aggressive BTN opener. Betting OOP with a marginal top pair would bloat the pot and often get called by better Qx or Ax hands that dominate me; checking preserves pot control and induces the frequent c-bets this opponent plays. Plan: check to invite a value-bluff mix from Flash. If he bets a small/medium c-bet I will call and re-evaluate on the turn (extract value from worse Qx, call down vs bluffs, fold to very large raises). If he checks back I'll take the initiative on many non-heart turn blanks. Checking maximizes information and protects my stack with this one-pair, weak-kicker holding.

I opened pre-flop with 5d 6d and have picked up a gutshot straight draw on the 2h-4s-Qh flop. Minni checked to me, which is expected. I will fire a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to take advantage of my positional lead and represent the Queen. This often wins the pot immediately against hands that missed this board entirely.

I have top pair (Qc) with a very weak 5 kicker out of position against a wide BTN opening/continuation range. Flash’s 300 into ~600 (≈50% pot) is a standard c-bet — I beat lots of his bluffs/overcards and can get value from worse Qx, but I’m behind vs QQ/QQx/sets and don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal kicker. A flat call keeps the pot manageable, gets a free card to re-evaluate, and denies him easy fold equity with small turn/river bluffs; raising risks getting 3-bet or called by better Qx and losing more. Plan: call the 300, see the turn, and fold to large action if I’m clearly crushed or proceed to extract value against worse hands and floats.

Check. I have top pair (Qc) with a very weak 5-kicker and I’m out of position versus a wide, aggressive BTN opener. The A♦ on the turn helps a lot of Flash’s range (lots of A‑x and some two‑pair/sets) and betting OOP here would bloat the pot and get called by many better Qx/Ax hands while pricing out his bluffs. Checking controls the pot, keeps worse Qx and bluffs in, and lets me react to his turn action. Plan: check and evaluate — call a small/medium bet (I beat many bluffs and worse Qx) but fold to large overbets or check‑raises that strongly represent A‑x/sets. If he checks back I can consider a small river stab on safe runouts but otherwise will proceed cautiously to protect my stack.

On the turn, an Ace appears, which is a great card for my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. Although I only have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3) and no pair, Minni's check-call on the flop suggests a marginal hand like a low pair or a draw. By betting the Ace, I exert maximum pressure on any middle pairs (4x, 2x) or weak Queens she might be holding. I will bet 800 into the 1200 pot to represent the Ace and try to take the pot down now.

I have top pair (Qc) with a very weak 5-kicker and I’m out of position. I checked the turn to control the pot and planned to call small/medium turn bets — this 800 into the ~1200 pot (pot now 2000) gives ~28.6% pot odds, which makes a call reasonable versus Flash’s wide, aggressive button range.
