The A♦ does improve many of his value combos (Ax, A2/A4 two-pairs, sets) and some Qx with better kickers still beat me, but Flash barrels a lot of thin value and bluffs too. Raising OOP with this kicker would bloat the pot and get called by better hands; folding here is too nitpicky given the pot odds and opponent tendencies. I’ll call 800 to see the river and re-evaluate — fold to an extreme river overbet unless I improve, call small/medium river bets more often (and value-bet small if he checks a safe river).

Check. I have top pair (Qc) with a very weak 5-kicker and I'm out of position. Flash's line (open pre, c-bet flop, bet the A on turn) contains many Ax, Qx with better kickers and hands with a deuce that just improved on the river (A2, 2x, or even 22 → trips/full house/quads). The river 2d materially increases the number of hands that beat me and reduces the thin value I can realistically extract. A small river bluff-catcher bet would fold out some pure air but will also get called or raised by a lot of better hands; raising or bloating the pot OOP with this marginal kicker is unnecessary risk. Checking controls the pot and takes the showdown; if Flash now bets small/medium I can call given pot odds and his wide, aggressive tendencies, and I can fold to a large polarized overbet. For stack preservation and best EV with this holding, I check to showdown.

The board has double-paired with a Deuce on the river, and both the Ace and Queen are present. My 5-high has no showdown value. However, the Ace on the turn was a very good scare card for me to bet, and Minni has checked all three streets. This line from Minni (check-call, check-call, check) often indicates a capped range like a weak Queen or a middle pair that is now in a tough spot. Since I've represented strength throughout the hand, checking back would mean losing the pot at showdown. A river bet puts immense pressure on those marginal hands, especially since the Ace and the second Deuce make it even harder for her to call. I'll bet 1600 into the 2800 pot to try and force a fold.

Flash has shown a consistent, aggressive value-building line (open, c-bet flop, barrel the A on the turn). I hold top pair (Qc) with a very weak 5-kicker and am out of position; the river 2 pairs the board and increases the number of combos that now beat me (2x, A2→full house, some sets), but Flash also barrels thin and bluffs frequently. The river bet is 1600 into a 4400 pot, so I need ~26.7% equity to make a profitable call — this size is a small/medium barrel relative to the pot and fits my pre-stated plan to call those. Raising OOP with this weak kicker is unnecessary and folding here gives up too much immediate equity. I will call 1600 to reach showdown and fold only to a large raise or clear show of strength.

Heads-up with Qs Qd is a premium hand — raise for value and to isolate the big blind while preserving position postflop. I’ll make a standard 3xBB sized open to 300 total (I’ve already posted 50 SB, so this puts meaningful pressure and gets worse hands to call). If Flash 3-bets I’ll reassess by size (call most reasonable 3-bets to see a flop deep; 4-bet/shove only vs very large/obvious bluffs). Postflop I’ll c-bet for value on dry boards and proceed cautiously on coordinated A/K-high textures that heavily interact with his range.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey suited (Kd 3d). Minni has opened to 300 from the small blind/button. In a heads-up situation, K3s is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard raise, especially since it has flush potential and some high-card value. While being out of position for the rest of the hand is not ideal, folding would be too tight, and 3-betting might inflate the pot unnecessarily with a marginal hand. I will call to see the flop.

On this dry flop of Q-6-3, I have bottom pair with a King kicker and a backdoor diamond flush draw. Since I am out of position in the Big Blind, checking is the standard play to see how the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) proceeds. My hand is strong enough to check-call a standard continuation bet, but not strong enough to lead out myself.

I flopped a set of queens (Qs Qd on Qc 6d 3s) heads-up versus Flash, who defended a wide BB range preflop. The board is very dry with no real draws, so this is a pure value-extraction spot. I want calls from worse hands (weaker Qx, KQ/AQ, and underpairs) and to keep weaker holdings and bluffs in the pot, so I'll make a small-to-medium c-bet — about 1/3 pot. That sizing (200 into 600) maximizes EV by inducing calls and occasional raises from worse. If he calls I'll continue extracting on later streets unless a scary card appears; if he raises I'll call most reasonable raises and re-evaluate on the turn since my set is very strong.

I have bottom pair with a King kicker on a relatively dry board. Minni's small continuation bet (1/3rd of the pot) is very standard for a pre-flop aggressor. With bottom pair and a backdoor diamond draw, I have plenty of equity to continue. Folding would be much too tight against this small bet, and raising would likely only isolate me against better hands like a Queen or a higher pair. I will call to see the turn.

The turn brings a second 3 (3h), giving me trips (three of a kind) with a King kicker. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play, especially on a board that is relatively disconnected. I checked the flop and called a small bet. Now that I have improved significantly, I will check again to allow Minni to continue bluffing or betting with a Queen. If she checks behind, I can go for value on the river; if she bets, I will likely just call to keep her range wide and avoid folding out her bluffs, or consider a raise depending on the sizing.

I have Queens full of Threes (Qs Qd with Qc 6d 3s 3h = QQQ33) — essentially the near-nut full house. Flash called my small flop c-bet (wide defending range) and checked the turn; most of his calling range (single Q, 6x, underpairs, some boats, occasional bluffs) is now well-beaten. Only realistic hand that crushes me is pocket 33 (quads), which is very rare and would likely produce a strong line if he has it. This is a pure value spot: I want to charge worse hands and build the pot, so I’ll bet pot to extract value from Qx, 6x, two-pair/sets and sticky calls. If he check-raises big I’ll re-evaluate (small/medium check-raise = call; a huge overbet shove strongly polarized toward quads and I’ll reassess then).

The turn card is excellent for my hand, giving me trips (three of a kind) with a King kicker. Minni's pot-sized bet indicates either a strong hand (like a Queen) or a polarized bluff. By just calling, I maintain the deception of my hand strength and allow her to continue betting on the river with her bluffs or value hands like Qx. Raising now might scare off her bluffs and potentially isolate me against the few hands that beat me (66 or QQ), whereas calling keeps her entire range in play.

On the river, the board is 6d-Qc-3s-3h-Ac and I have trips (3-3-3) with a King kicker. My hand is extremely strong in a heads-up pot. Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and bet significant sizing on the turn (pot-sized). This indicates she likely has a strong Queen, a boat (though 66 or QQ are rare), or is on a total bluff. The Ace on the river is an interesting card; it might scare her if she has a Queen, but it's also a card she could potentially represent or have hit with a hand like Ax. Since I am out of position, checking is the best play to see if she continues her aggression. If she has a Queen, she might check back for showdown value, but if I bet, she might fold her bluffs. By checking, I allow her to fire a third bullet with a bluff or value bet with an Ace/Queen. If she bets, I will definitely call and potentially raise depending on her sizing.

I have the near-nut full house (Qs Qd on Qc 6d 3s 3h — QQQ33). The only realistic hands that beat me are pocket Aces (AAA33) or pocket Threes (quads), both of which are rare given Flash’s line (called my flop and turn rather than 3‑betting/raising preflop or firing/raising harder earlier). Flash’s calls are consistent with worse hands — single Qx, 6x, underpairs and sticky A‑x — so this is a pure value spot. Pot is 3,000; I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (2,000) to maximize calls from those worse hands while not folding out too many of them. If he check‑raises big it polarizes to the rare combos that beat me (or a bluff) and I’ll reassess then, but betting now extracts the most EV. Action: value bet 2,000 into the 3,000 pot.

On the river, the board is 6d-Qc-3s-3h-Ac and I have trips (3s) with a King kicker. Minni has played this hand aggressively, leading out with a small bet on the flop, a pot-sized bet on the turn, and now a 2/3 pot bet on the river. My hand is very strong in a heads-up context, beating all bluffs, all Queens, and all Aces. However, the turn bet was very polarized, and the Ace on the river is a card that could have improved her (if she had Ax or AQ) or it could be a card she is using to bluff. While a raise might get value from a stubborn Queen or an Ace, it also risks a reraise from a full house (66, QQ, or AA) and would likely force her to fold her bluffs. By calling, I ensure that I win the pot against her entire betting range, including her bluffs and her value bets with marginal hands.
I am on the button with KTh, a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Standard opening strategy is to raise to 3x the big blind to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a 3x open to 300 from the button. I have Q♣2♥ — a weak offsuit holding but the price to see a flop is small: pot is 400 and I need 200 more (≈33% break-even equity). The button’s opening range here is wide, so Q2o has sufficient raw equity to continue. 3-betting as a bluff is unattractive — Q2o lacks good blockers and will be difficult to play OOP deep-stacked if called. Plan: call to see a flop, fold to heavy multi-street aggression unless I improve (top pair, two pair, a decent draw, or a favorable board texture), and use position/stack depth later to extract value when appropriate. Therefore I’ll defend the big blind and call the 200 additional chips (300 total committed).

Check. I have Q♣2♥ — bottom pair (a small pair) on T‑3‑2. Heads‑up out of position this is worth protecting but not worth building a big pot. Flash opened wide preflop and will c‑bet a high frequency, so checking lets him barrel bluffs and keeps the pot controllable. Plan: check‑call reasonable flop bets (approx ≤1/2 pot) to see a turn, fold to large raises or pot‑sized aggression, and re‑evaluate on the turn (bet thin for value vs worse tens or fold to heavy pressure unless I improve). This line realizes equity while minimizing the risk of getting raised off the hand OOP.

I have flopped top pair with a King kicker on a relatively dry board (3s-2c-Th). This is a strong hand in a heads-up pot. Since Minni checked, I will make a standard continuation bet of half the pot to extract value from weaker pairs, draws, or high-card hands that might call.

I call. I have Qc2h — bottom pair (pair of twos) on a T‑3‑2 rainbow. Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bet a small half‑pot (300 into the ~600 pot), so his range is likely wide and includes many bluffs/weak overcards. Facing 300 to win 1200 after my call gives me ~25% break‑even equity, which my hand has versus a high c‑bet frequency. With deep stacks and out of position, check‑calling a half‑pot c‑bet is the lowest variance, +EV line: it realizes equity without bloating the pot with a marginal hand. I’ll fold to large, pot‑sized raises or heavy multi‑street pressure unless I improve. If the turn pairs the board or brings a Queen/trips, I’ll transition to value‑betting/raising; if Flash checks the turn I may take a small stab for value. Calling here commits 600 total chips (300 preflop + 300 more now) and keeps the pot controlled for the next street.

Heads‑up, I have Q♣2♥—a small but made hand (pair of deuces) on T‑3‑2‑9 rainbow. Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop small, then checked/checked‑line on the turn shows weakness or pot control. This is a good spot to take the initiative with a small probe to fold out pure overcards and deny a free river. I’ll bet about a third of the pot so I can take it down now while keeping the pot manageable if called. If I face a pot‑sized or larger raise I’ll fold (he likely has trips/overpair); if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and proceed cautiously.

On the turn, the Nine of Diamonds is added to the board (3s-2c-Th-9d). I still have top pair (Tens) with a King kicker. Minni has led out for 400 into a 1200 pot after I continuation-bet the flop. This 'donk lead' on the turn often represents a marginal hand like a weak Ten, a draw (like QJ or J8), or perhaps a pair that just improved (like a 9). It's unlikely she has a powerhouse given the pre-flop action and flop check-call. Folding is out of the question as my hand is too strong. Raising might bloat the pot unnecessarily if she is trapping with something like a set or Two Pair (T9), or it might force out her bluffs/draws. Calling allows me to control the pot size while keeping her range wide and seeing how the river develops. I will call the 400.

I have Q♣2♥ — bottom pair (pair of twos) on T‑3‑2‑9‑6. Pot is 2,000 and villain (Flash) opened wide preflop, c‑bet the flop and then called my small turn probe. That line shows a lot of hands with real showdown value (Ts,9x,3x, some 2x, and strong floats) and therefore a relatively low fold frequency on the river. The 6♣ is a blank for my hand and doesn’t appreciably increase my equity. Betting here would need significant fold equity to be +EV; given he willingly called the turn, his calling range on the river is likely high and I’m behind a large share of it. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand or face a raise. Best line is to check and realize my showdown equity rather than risk additional chips on a thin value bet or a bluff that won’t fold enough of his calling range.

On the river 6c, the board is 3s-2c-Th-9d-6c. I have top pair with a King kicker. After Minni led for a small amount on the turn and then checked the river, it strongly suggests she has a marginal hand like a weaker Ten or a middle pair (88, 77, or a 9) that she is now looking to take to showdown cheaply. While there's a small risk of a slow-played set or T9, top pair is usually the best hand here. I will bet 1000 for value, as a Ten or a stubborn mid-pair is likely to call a half-pot bet in a heads-up situation.

Flash bet 1,000 into a 2,000 pot (3,000 total) — I must call 1,000, so I’m getting 25% pot odds. I hold Q♣2♥ (pair of deuces). Villain opened wide preflop, c‑bet the flop and then called my small turn probe; that line is weighted strongly toward real showdown hands (T‑x, 9‑x, 3‑x, some 2‑x and pocket pairs) and not a high-frequency river bluffing range. Against that value‑heavy range my deuce rarely has >25% equity. Raising as a bluff here is too risky and thin given his willingness to call the turn. I’ll preserve chips and fold this thin river spot rather than call into a range that likely dominates me.

I have 2♣7♦ — essentially the worst starting hand. Pot is 150, I’ve already posted 50; calling costs 50 more (100 total) and keeps the pot small while preserving position post‑flop. I could min‑raise to 200 to try to steal, but Flash defends and plays aggressively a lot in these heads‑up spots; with such a junk hand and deep stacks a raise would create unnecessary post‑flop risk. Plan: call to see a flop in position, fold to a large 3‑bet or to heavy postflop pressure if I miss. This line minimizes risk and preserves my stack for better opportunities over the remaining hands.

In Hand #120, I am in the Big Blind with 7c 8h. The opponent, Minni, has completed the small blind, making the pot 200. My hand is a decent middle-connector but offsuit. In heads-up play, checking behind is a standard and safe play with this type of hand to see a flop for free. Raising is an option, but given my stack size and the desire to see how the board develops without committing more chips with a marginal hand, checking is the most prudent action.

In Hand #120, I am holding 7c 8h in the Big Blind. The flop comes 9d Jc Ks. This board is quite coordinated and contains three overcards to my pair and several potential draws. Since I was in the Big Blind and checked the pre-flop action, the pot is small at 200 chips. Minni has not yet acted on the flop. If she checks, I will also check to take a free turn card, as my hand currently has no showdown value and only a very weak backdoor straight draw. If she bets, I will likely fold given the lack of connectivity and the strength of the board relative to my range. As the player acting first, I will check to see her reaction.

I have 2♣7♦ on a K♠ J♣ 9♦ rainbow flop — essentially no showdown value and almost zero equity (only tiny outs to pair 2 or 7). Flash checked to me; he could be weak but also is a sticky/aggressive opponent who calls and raises often. A bluff bet here can win the pot sometimes, but against this player it frequently gets called or turned into a costly battle. With position and a junk hand my long‑term +EV line is to avoid bloating the pot: check and fold to a reasonable bet. If Flash checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the turn (only bluff or commit if I pick up a real improvement or a much stronger story).

I am holding 7c 8h on a board of 9d Jc Ks 9c. I have no pair and no real draw. The pot is very small (200 chips) because both players checked the flop. The turn card pairs the board. Since I have no showdown value and the board is quite connected, there is no reason to bet and risk being called or raised. Checking is the standard play here to see a free river card and keep the pot small.

I have absolute air with 2♣7♦ on K♠ J♣ 9♦ 9♣ (board pair). Flash checked flop and turn; given his sticky/aggressive tendencies a bluff here will often get called and can turn into an expensive battle. I have only six clean outs to make a lone pair (~13% to improve on the river), and even if I pair I'm frequently second-best to Kx, Jx, or any 9. Pot is only 200 and stacks are deep — my long‑term +EV line is to avoid bloating the pot with nothing. I’ll check and conserve chips; I will fold to a bet on the turn and re‑evaluate the river only if I pick up a real improvement or a highly favorable runout/spot to bluff.

On this board of 9d Jc Ks 9c 5s, I hold 8h 7c, which is just 8-high with no showdown value. Both players have checked the flop and the turn, indicating very weak ranges. As the first to act on the river, I have the option to bluff to try and take down the 200-chip pot, but given that the pot is so small and I have zero equity if called, checking is the most prudent play. If Minni checks behind, we go to showdown where I likely lose, but if she bets, I can easily fold without further loss.

Board: 9♦ J♣ K♠ 9♣ 5♠. I hold 2♣7♦ — essentially no showdown value and almost zero equity. Pot is tiny (200) relative to our deep stacks (~22k/17.6k). Flash has shown to be a sticky/aggressive caller; bluffing to win 200 is low‑EV and will often be called by Kx/Jx/9x. There’s no value to bet and a bet is unlikely to fold out better hands. I’ll check and conserve chips; if Flash bets the river I will fold.
I am on the button with Ace-Six of clubs. This is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. I will raise to take control of the hand and build the pot.

Heads-up vs Flash (SB) who opened to 300. Pot is 400; I need to add 200 to call — break-even equity ~33%. K♠8♦ is a reasonable hand heads-up and has >33% vs a wide SB opening range. I’m also out of position postflop, so 3-betting a marginal hand to take the initiative is unattractive — I’d be OOP on every street and could face a lot of resistance from stronger hands. With deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot manageable and see a flop where top pair or good draws can be played aggressively, otherwise fold to heavy action. Calling preserves chips, extracts value when I hit, and keeps the pot controllable against an aggressive opener.

I’m out of position with K♠8♦ on Q♦ J♣ 2♣ — just one overcard and no club draw. Flash opened from the SB, so his range is wide, but I don’t want to fire OOP with a marginal hand and commit to larger pots on later streets. Checking keeps the pot under control, lets me see how he reacts, and preserves my options: call small continuation bets (I have decent equity vs bluffs/worse pairs) but fold to heavy aggression. If he checks back I get a free turn to improve. Play for information and pot control rather than bluff-leading here.

I have Ace-high with the Ace of Spades and a backdoor club flush draw (6c, 2c, Jc). The flop is Qd-Jc-2c. My opponent, Minni, has checked to me. While I haven't paired up, I have two overcards to the board's lower cards and a gutshot straight draw (requiring a King or Ten, though Ten would only give me a weak straight). More importantly, I have a range advantage as the pre-flop raiser. A continuation bet here is standard to apply pressure and potentially win the pot immediately, or to build a pot in case I improve on the turn.

Pot is 900 and Flash has bet 300 — I need to call 300 for pot odds of 25%. I hold K♠8♦: one clean overcard (K) and no club draw, roughly 6 simple outs (3 Ks + 3 eights) and some extra equity vs bluffs. Flash has been opening and c-betting a wide range from the SB heads-up, so I expect a fair number of bluffs/weaker hands in his range; raising OOP with a marginal hand is unattractive and will commit me to bigger pots where I’m out of position. Calling keeps the pot controlled, realizes equity, and lets me fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss — if I pair the K on turn I can start extracting value. Action: call 300.

I have K♠8♦ on 2♣ Q♦ J♣ 7♦ — no pair, no club draw and only King-high. I’m out of position versus an opponent who opens and continuation-bets a wide range, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a naked bluff that will often be called by better hands (Qx/Jx/Kx) or lead to bigger turns/river commitment. Checking controls the pot size, lets me see how Flash reacts on the river, and preserves my stack to continue only when I improve or when I have clearer fold equity. If he bets the river I can fold to large bets or call small bets with the K-high if pot odds justify it; if he checks back I get a free card. Action: check to keep the pot manageable and avoid playing a bloated OOP pot with a marginal hand.

On the turn, the Seven of Diamonds does not significantly change the board texture. I still have Ace-high, but I've also picked up a backdoor flush draw on the flop with the 6c and 2c on the board (wait, I hold the 6c, so I have the 6c and the 2c/Jc are on board, giving me three clubs). My opponent, Minni, has checked again. While my hand has little showdown value, checking back allows me to take a free card and potentially realize my equity if an Ace or a club (improving my flush draw potential, although I only have one club in hand) falls. However, since I represent a strong range as the pre-flop raiser and flop bettor, I can continue to represent a Queen or a Jack. A second barrel here puts pressure on her marginal hands like small pairs or straight draws. Given the stack depths and the board, a bet of roughly 60% of the pot keeps the pressure on.

Fold. I have K♠8♦ with no pair and only one card to come. The pot is 1950 and I must call 750, so I need ~28% equity to break even. I have only ~6 clean outs (3 Ks + 3 8s) ≈ 13% to pair on the river, well below the required equity. I’m out of position and Flash’s line (open, flop c-bet, turn bet) is consistent with many value hands (Qx, Jx, KQ, two-pair) as well as some bluffs, but the bet size and poor odds make calling incorrect. Raising as a bluff OOP is unattractive — it will often get called by better hands or force a costly decision. Folding preserves my stack and avoids chasing an underpriced draw with insufficient equity.

6♣8♣ on the button in heads-up is a very playable, deceptive hand — good equity and great postflop potential in position. With deep stacks and the SB already posted, I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure rather than limp; a standard 2×BB raise (to 200 total) gives fold equity, narrows Flash’s range, and lets me play a favorable positional postflop. If Flash folds I win the pot; if he calls I can realize implied odds on straights/flushes. If he 3-bets large I’ll usually fold to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal holding, but I’ll defend small 3-bets depending on size and tendencies.

I have Ace-King offsuit (AKo) in the Big Blind, which is a premium starting hand. Minni has opened to 200 from the Small Blind (Button). In a heads-up situation, AKo is a very strong hand that I should almost always raise for value and to take initiative. I will put in a 3-bet. A standard 3-bet size here would be around 3 to 4 times the open, so I'll raise to 700 total.

I opened to 200 on the button and Flash 3-bet to 700. I have 6♣8♣ — a suited connector that plays extremely well in position with deep stacks. Calling 500 more into the 900-pot gives ~35.7% break-even equity; versus a typical wide heads‑up 3‑bet range this hand has reasonable equity and excellent implied odds to make straights/flushes. 4‑betting as a bluff would bloat the pot with a hand that I prefer to realize equity with postflop, and folding is too tight heads‑up. Plan: call to keep the pot manageable, use position to probe/see flops cheaply, barrel when I have strong equity or fold/control on dry, Ace‑heavy boards.

I have flopped Top Pair, Top Kicker (TPTK) with Ace-King on a board of Ad-Td-5h. This is a very strong hand in a 3-bet pot heads-up. The board is somewhat wet with the diamond draw and broadway straight possibilities (QJ, KQ), so I should bet for value and to deny equity to draws. A bet of around 40-50% of the pot is appropriate here to keep her range wide while still charging drawing hands. I will bet 600 into the 1400 pot.

I opened preflop and called Flash’s 3‑bet with 6♣8♣ to realize equity in position. The flop A♦ T♦ 5♥ is very bad for my holding — I have no pair, no clean straight draw, and only a backdoor club possibility. Flash leads 600 into a ~2000 pot; calling 600 requires ~23% equity to break even, but my actual equity here (with only backdoor outs) is well below that. Raising would be a pure bluff against a range that contains many A/T and broadway combos and would bloat the pot with almost no equity. Given the dry, Ace‑heavy board and deep stacks, the correct play is to preserve chips and fold to this c‑bet rather than chase a low‑probability backdoor plan.

I am on the button with Ace-Nine offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. My current stack is roughly equal to Minni's. Historically, I have been aggressive from the button with standard raises to 300. This hand warrants a raise to put pressure on Minni and build a pot with a superior range.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with Tc8h — a marginal but playable hand heads-up. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (total commit = 300). Pot odds: 200 to win ~600 => ~33% equity required. Against a wide SB open-range my hand has reasonable equity and postflop playability, especially with deep stacks, so calling to see a flop is +EV. I’m out of position postflop, so I don’t want to 3-bet narrow or bluff-raise with this offsuit hand (a small raise to 500 won’t buy much fold equity and will put me OOP). Plan: call, see the flop, and proceed cautiously — fold to heavy pressure on bad textures, continue with top pairs and good draws. Call to 300 total.

I missed the flop with Tc8h on 3♦-6♥-A♥. I have no pair and only a lone heart (virtually no realistic flush/straight equity), and I’m out of position. Flash opened from the button so his c‑bet frequency is high and his range contains many Ax and value hands I’m behind to. Best play is pot control: check and fold to any significant continuation bet. I can consider calling a very tiny probe (cheap bet ≤ ~1/4 pot) for pot odds, but otherwise preserve my stack and see a turn for free to re-evaluate if I pick up a pair or a real draw.

I have top pair with an Ace and a nine kicker on a board of A-6-3. This is a strong hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. I will place a continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot, to extract value from weaker hands like middle pairs or draws and to protect my hand against potential flush draws.

I checked the flop as planned and now face a 300 bet into a 900 pot. Calling 300 requires ~25% equity (300/(900+300)). With Tc8h on 3♦-6♥-A♥ I have no pair and only a very remote backdoor heart draw that needs both turn and river hearts (~4%); runner‑runner straights are effectively negligible. The button’s c-bet range contains many A‑x and value hands that dominate me, and a bluff‑raise out of position is too thin. My actual equity is well below the threshold, so folding and preserving my stack for better spots is the +EV play here.

Heads-up on the button (also SB) with pocket 4s — a made pair that fares well against a very wide BB range. I already have positional advantage postflop and have 20500 behind, so this is a straightforward raise-for-value/isolation spot rather than a limp. A min-raise to 2xBB (200 total) builds the pot, gains fold equity, keeps the pot manageable, and preserves flexibility postflop to set-mine or apply pressure. Calling 50 would be passive and allow him to see a free-ish flop more often; a larger open isn't necessary right now. Raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with J6s. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, requiring me to call 100 more into a 300 pot (offering 3-to-1 odds). While J6s is a marginal hand, in heads-up play, it is strong enough to defend against a minimum raise from the button to see a flop, especially given the stack depths and the pot odds.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-high on a board of King-Nine-Eight. This board is relatively coordinated and likely interacts with a wide range of hands that Minni would raise from the Small Blind. I have no pair and very little equity, aside from a thin backdoor straight draw. In heads-up play, checking to the aggressor is the standard play here to see how they react. Leading out (donking) into this board without a strong hand or a significant draw would be poorly balanced and likely ineffective. I will check and see if Minni bets.

I’m the preflop aggressor in position with 4♠4♣ on a K♣ 9♠ 8♦ rainbow flop. I’ve missed but hold an underpair with set potential and some showdown value vs pure overcards. Flash’s calling range from the BB is wide (broadways, Kx, 9x, middling pairs, floats), so a continuation-bet will fold out many overcards and deny equity while still keeping the pot controllable. A half-pot c-bet (~200 into 400) balances fold equity and allows me to proceed cautiously if called — I’ll check the turn for pot control unless I hit a set or the turn gives a clear reason to barrel. If I face a sizable check-raise, that will usually indicate too strong a range and I’ll fold.

On a board of K-9-8, I have Jack-high and no real draws besides a very weak backdoor straight draw. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 1/3 of the pot (200 into 400). This board hits a lot of pre-flop raising ranges (Kings, Nines, Eights, and straight draws like QJ, JT, 76). While I have a Jack, I don't have enough equity to justify a call here against a bet, as I am likely behind and have few ways to improve to a winning hand on the turn. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.
I am on the button with Ace-Trey offsuit. In heads-up play, any Ace is a strong opening hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage.

Heads-up, deep stacks (~206 BB). Facing a standard open to 300 from the button and I’m in the big blind with J♣4♣ — a suited Jack with some playability but not strong enough to 3-bet OOP as a bluff. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) is cheap relative to the stacks and keeps a wide opener’s range in check. I’ll see a flop and play based on texture: continue aggressively if I hit pair/top pair or good club/straight draws; fold or give up to heavy pressure when I miss and face strong action. A 3-bet here would risk too many chips with poor blockers and bad OOP playability, so defend by calling and exercising pot-control post‑flop.

Check. I defended preflop with J♣4♣ but on 7♥ T♠ 9♣ I have no pair — only a J overcard and a backdoor club draw (runner‑runner). This board heavily favors the raiser’s range (lots of T/9/connected combos and broadways), so betting OOP as a pure bluff has poor fold equity and can get me raised into difficult decisions with ~200bb stacks. I’ll keep pot control: check to see how Flash reacts. If he bets small I can float/call to realize equity (look to hit a J or a club on the turn and/or bluff the river); if he barrels big I can fold and preserve stack. If he checks back I get a free turn to improve. This line minimizes risk while preserving options on later streets.

On a board of 7h-Ts-9c, I have Ace-high with a backdoor straight draw. This board texture is very coordinated and favors a calling range more than a raising range, as it hits many of the 8-9-T-J combinations Minni likely defends with from the Big Blind. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, a small continuation bet is often effective on boards where I have an equity advantage or can represent overpairs. While this board is wet, checking back to realize equity with my Ace-high is a viable defensive play, but betting 300 (half-pot) allows me to maintain initiative and potentially fold out better non-paired hands or weak draws from Minni's range. Given the current stack sizes and hand history, staying aggressive while mindful of resistance is key.

Call 300. Flash’s c-bet is small (300 into 900, ~25% pot) and represents a wide heads‑up range — calling is cheap and keeps the pot controlled with deep stacks (~20450 vs 18650). With J♣4♣ I have a live overcard (J), a backdoor club possibility (need both turn+river), and useful blockers to some Jx/QJ combos. Raising OOP here is too ambitious with poor blockers and playability; folding is too tight given the pot odds and implied odds. Plan: float/call to see a turn and continue only if I pair the J, pick up meaningful club/straight equity, or opponent shows weakness; otherwise give up to heavy turn aggression to protect my stack.

Check. I missed pair but the 6♣ gave me a 4‑card club draw (Jc4c + 9c6c) plus runner/river outs (8s for the straight and remaining Js). Unique outs ≈15 → ~32–33% to improve on the river. Pot is 1,200 and stacks are very deep (~200bb), so I don’t want to build a big OOP pot with only a draw and an overcard. Checking controls the pot and lets me realize equity; it also keeps weaker parts of his range in so I can call a reasonable barrel. Plan: call small–medium turn bets (pot odds are favourable — with ~33% equity I can profitably call up to roughly a pot‑sized bet ≈1.1–1.2k), fold to very large overbets, and if he checks back take a free river where I bet for value when I hit and consider a targeted bluff if the river and line allow it.

On this extremely connected board (7-10-9-6), my Ace-high has very little equity against Minni's calling range. The turn card (6c) completes several potential straights (any 8 or 5-8) and adds a flush draw. Since Minni called both pre-flop and on the flop, she likely has a piece of this board or a strong draw. Checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity and avoid being check-raised on a texture that heavily favors the defender's range.

I have J♣4♣ and missed — no pair, no flush. Flash checked both turn and river after c-betting the flop, which looks like weakness or a marginal holding. I have essentially zero showdown value, so checking back just gives up. A small, credible river bluff maximizes fold equity while limiting risk: 1/3 pot (≈400 into 1200) looks like a thin value bet (Kx or a delayed club/straight) and only needs ~25% fold frequency to be +EV. If Flash raises, I’ll fold — I’m not calling a raise with J4. If he calls I lose a small amount; if he folds I pick up the pot. Bet 400 to steal the pot with a low‑risk bluff.

On a board of 7-10-9-6-K, the texture is incredibly coordinated. Any 8 makes a straight, and many of the hands Minni would defend with (like 8x, J8, 89, 87) have completed. My Ace-high (Ace-Trey) has effectively zero showdown value on this board against anything Minni would lead into me with on the river for 400. While the price to call is small (400 into 1200), I am losing to every pair, every straight, and even some bluffs that might have evolved into a pair of Kings. Since I checked the turn, Minni's lead on the river indicates either a value bet or a calculated bluff on a scary board; however, my hand is too weak to be a 'bluff catcher' here. I will fold and preserve my chips.

Heads-up on the button with J♦2♠. In heads-up play I should be opening a very wide range — this hand is weak but playable in position: it has a high-card (jack) and some postflop playability with the advantage of acting last. I want to apply pressure to the BB and take the initiative, so make a standard min-open to 200. If Flash folds I scoop the 150 pot. If he calls I plan to use position: c-bet on many dry/advantageous flops, check and pot-control or float on wet boards, and look to realize equity with backdoor draws or pair-ups. If he 3-bets, J2o is usually behind; I will fold to a sizable 3-bet (anything materially above a small squeeze) and only entertain a call to a very small, likely exploitable 3-bet. Raising to 200 keeps the range wide, preserves deep-stack maneuverability, and maximizes fold equity against the BB.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s5c. Minni, who is on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 200. This is a standard 2x open. I only need to call 100 more to see a flop in a pot of 300, giving me 3-to-1 odds. While my hand is weak, 65o has some playability in a heads-up situation and the price is right to defend. Folding is too tight here, and raising is unnecessary with a speculative hand. I will call to see a flop and re-evaluate.

On the flop of As-8h-4c, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) and a very weak hand. The board is quite dry but heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, as Minni will have all the strong Aces and pocket pairs. My plan is to check and see how Minni reacts. If Minni bets small, I might consider calling depending on the price, but generally, I'm looking to fold if faced with significant aggression on this texture.

Flop A♠ 8♥ 4♣ (rainbow). I opened wide preflop, but J♦2♠ has almost no equity here — no realistic backdoor (can’t make a 5-card spade flush from this situation) and only ~6 clean outs to pair. The Ace on board heavily helps the BB’s calling range (Ax, some 8x/4x, many hands that will call a c-bet). Flash checked, which can be either a slow-played Ace, a medium pair, or a sticky marginal hand. Rather than c-betting into an Ace-heavy range where I’ll be called too often and face difficult decisions, I’ll check to control the pot and try to realize equity. Plan: check-call small probe bets to see a cheap turn, fold to large bets or check-raises, and only apply pressure/bluff on turns that give me improved equity or clear fold equity from villain.

The turn card is the King of Spades, making the board As-8h-4c-Ks. I currently hold 6-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7). Minni checked back on the flop, which suggests she might have a weak hand, a marginal pair, or is giving up on the pot. However, leading into the pre-flop aggressor after they check back a dry flop can often be seen through. I will check to see if Minni continues to show weakness. If she checks again, I may have an opportunity to bluff on the river or potentially realize my equity if I hit a 7. If she bets, I will likely fold as my hand is too weak to continue.

Heads-up I opened wide preflop with J2s and checked the flop to control the pot vs an Ace-heavy calling range. The turn K♠ further helps calling ranges (AK, Kx, some slow-played Aces) and there are now two spades on board — I still only have 6 clean outs (three J’s and three 2’s) and very little showdown value. Flash checked the turn; that can be weakness but also a disguised strong hand. Betting here would be a very thin bluff into a range that calls many turn/river bets with A/K or medium pairs, so my fold equity is limited. Better to keep the pot manageable and realize equity: check to see the river, plan to fold to large barreling aggression, call small probes, and only fire as a bluff on rivers that improve my story or as a value bet when I actually pair up.

The board is As-8h-4c-Ks-Kc and I have 6-high. The action has been checked all the way through. While Minni's checks on the flop and turn suggest weakness, she could easily be holding a small pocket pair (22-77) or a weak 8 which beats my 6-high. In a small pot of 400, a bluff is unlikely to be highly profitable as players often call down with any pair in these spots. Since I have no showdown value but the pot is very small, I will check and concede the hand if she checks back, or fold if she bets, rather than risking more chips on a low-frequency bluff.

Check. My J♦2♠ missed — the river paired the board (As 8h 4c Ks Kc) and I have no showdown value. Flash checked every street, which is consistent with both slow-played Ks/As or medium-strength hands that will call a river bluff; fold equity is therefore very thin. The pot is small (400) relative to deep stacks, so a bluff here is unlikely to be profitable and voluntarily bloating the pot with junk is unnecessary. I’ll check to avoid getting called by any K/A/pair holdings and plan to fold to any sizable river bet, only entertaining a call to a very tiny probe if it looks like a pure bluff.

I have been dealt pocket Kings (Ks Kc) on the button (Small Blind). This is the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em and a massive premium. In a heads-up situation against Minni, I want to build a large pot immediately. Standard opening raise size in this game has been 300, so I will raise to that amount to extract value and narrow the field.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a standard 3x open to 300 from the button. I hold 8♣6♥ — a marginal but defensible hand heads-up with some flop playability (backdoor straight/club, chance for two pair). Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (total commitment = 300), giving me ~33% break-even equity to continue. With deep stacks I can realize implied odds and I don’t want to 3-bet bluff with a hand that plays poorly OOP and lacks strong blockers. Folding would be too tight given the opener’s likely wide range. I’ll call to see a flop, plan to proceed cautiously out of position (check and fold to large c-bets on dry A/K-high boards unless I improve; play aggressively if I pair, make two pair/straight or get strong draws).

Check. I have a marginal but usable middle pair (8♣6♥) on Q♣ 8♥ 4♦. Against the preflop raiser’s wide heads‑up range this hand has decent showdown value but is vulnerable to any Qx, higher pairs, or broadway overcards. There are no real flush draws (only one club on board + my club), and only remote backdoor straight outs, so there’s little reason to build a big pot out of position. Checking controls the size of the pot, lets the aggressor barrel with air, and keeps weaker hands and bluffs in his range to call later. Plan: call a standard small/medium c‑bet and re‑evaluate on the turn (fold to heavy pressure unless I improve to two pair/trips or pick up clear equity); if he checks back I’ll consider a small stab on safe turn cards or a value bet only if I improve.

I have pocket Kings (Ks Kc) and the flop comes Qc 8h 4d. This is a very favorable board for my hand as there is only one overcard (Queen) that connects with Minni's calling range, and even if she has a Queen, my Kings are significantly ahead. I want to bet for value and to protect against potential straight or runner-runner draws. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, a standard continuation bet size that encourages calls from weaker pairs or draws.

Heads‑up, I have second pair (8♣6♥) on Q♣‑8♥‑4♦. Flash opened wide preflop and his 300 flop bet into the ~600 pot is a standard small/medium c‑bet. There are virtually no realistic draws for me (no possible 5‑card flush with my single club and only two board cards left), so this hand’s value is showdown equity and the ability to improve. Calling 300 into a 900 pot means I’m getting to win 1200 after calling — break‑even equity ≈25%. Versus a wide heads‑up c‑bet range (many Qx, overcards and bluffs) my pair of eights has enough equity to justify a call, and calling keeps worse hands and bluffs in while controlling the pot OOP. I don’t want to inflate the pot with a raise OOP with second pair and a weak kicker, and folding is too tight given the pot odds and hand strength. Plan: call the 300; on the turn I’ll reevaluate — if I improve (two‑pair/trips) isolate/value‑raise; if a blank or a scary queen/ace/king comes and Flash applies heavy pressure, I will fold unless I pick up clear additional equity.

I have 8♣6♥ on Q♣‑8♥‑4♦‑5♣ — second pair with a weak kicker. Flash was the preflop raiser and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn, which reads like pot control with Qx/medium pairs or missed overcards rather than a huge value hand. Raising OOP with second pair is too thin (it gets called by better hands and only folds the worst), and a small bet mainly prices in hands that still beat me (Qx) while not protecting meaningfully vs the few two‑club flush draws that can only hit the river. Checking controls the pot and lets me realize showdown equity with a hand that will be good often enough against his wide range. Plan: check and evaluate the river — call small/standard river bets (or any bet if I improve), but fold to large river aggression unless I improve to two pair/trips.

I have an overpair (pocket Kings) on a relatively safe board. The turn 5 of clubs doesn't significantly help Minni's likely range, which mostly consists of Queens, mid-pocket pairs, or possibly an 8. Since Minni has shown a tendency to call multiple streets with one-pair hands in previous encounters (like Hand #106 and #110), I will bet for value to build the pot. I'll bet 800, which is 2/3 of the pot, to extract maximum value from a Queen or a draw.

I have 8♣6♥ on Q♣‑8♥‑4♦‑5♣: second pair with a weak kicker, no real draws. Pot is 2000 and the turn bet is 800, so I need ~28.6% equity to break even (800/(2000+800)). My improvement outs to make two‑pair/trips on the river are: remaining 8s (2), any 6 (3), any 4 (3) or any Q (3) = ~11 outs → ~24% to improve. Add that to the hand’s current showdown value vs a wide, aggressive betting range (he opens and barrels fairly often), and my realistic equity vs his continuing range is around or slightly above the break‑even threshold.

Given stacks are deep and this is a marginal spot OOP, folding would be too tight and raising is thin (it will get called by better hands and won’t gain much fold equity vs his likely range). Calling keeps bluffs and worse hands in, controls the pot size, and preserves the option to fold to heavy river aggression if I don’t improve. Plan: call the 800 to see the river; if I pair/up (8/6/4/Q) I can extract value or consider a raise depending on the texture and his sizing — if the river is a scary card or he barrels big and I don’t improve, I’ll fold.

River: Q♣ 8♥ 4♦ 5♣ 7♥. With 8♣6♥ I now have a completed 8‑high straight (4‑5‑6‑7‑8). On this texture there is no flush and no higher straight possible — the only realistic way I can be worse is if Flash also holds a 6 (tie). Flash has been barreling flop and turn with a wide, aggressive range (Qx, 8x, pocket pairs, missed overcards and bluffs), and most of those hands are now behind my straight. I should be extracting value rather than checking and letting him take the lead. A value sizing around ~2/3 pot will get called by Qx, 8x, pocket pairs and many weaker holdings while still leaving room for him to commit if he raises. If he check‑raises big I can comfortably call/shove given my near‑nut strength (ties with a 6 are rare). Action: bet 2000 to maximize value from worse hands and bluffs.

I hold an overpair (pocket Kings) on a board that has become slightly more connected but doesn't have an obvious made flush or high straight besides 69 (which is unlikely). Minni has check-called two streets and is now leading out for 2,000 into a 2,800 pot. This 'donk' bet on the river after calling the turn often signifies either a missed draw trying to bluff, a polarized range containing value like a set (88, 55, 44, or 77), or a two-pair hand (like 87 or 75). However, Minni has also shown aggression with top pairs in the past. Given the strength of my hand (overpair) and the pot odds (calling 2,000 to win 6,800), I have enough equity to call, but raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me. I will call to see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button with A♠6♣ — this is a clear open. I already have 50 in; calling 50 is passive and lets the BB see a cheap flop with a wide defending range. With deep stacks (my 24,900 vs Flash 14,950) and table tendencies favoring 3x opens, I want to seize initiative and use position postflop. A standard 3x open to 300 gives good fold equity vs weak hands, isolates the BB, and keeps the pot manageable if called. Plan: c-bet favorable flops; re-evaluate if he 3-bets (fold vs large 3-bets, consider calling small ones).

I am in the big blind with AQo, a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. AQo is clearly ahead of a standard button opening range and is a premium hand to 3-bet for value and to take control of the pot. Given the stack sizes and current dynamic, a raise to 1000 is a standard size to put pressure on Minni while building a pot with a superior hand.

Flash 3-bet to 1,000 after my 3x open to 300. Heads‑up in position with A♠6♣, I prefer to call the extra 700 (total 1,000) rather than 4‑bet or fold. Calling keeps the pot manageable, uses my positional advantage to realize equity and exploit post‑flop, and avoids turning a marginal hand into a bluff 4‑bet with poor blockers. Pot math is reasonable (calling 700 into a 1,300 pot needs ≈35% equity), and A6o plays well IP against a wide 3‑betting range — I can c‑bet favorable flops, float and apply pressure, or fold to heavy action on very hostile flops. If Flash 4‑bets big I will re‑evaluate (likely fold unless I pick up strong equity). So: call to 1,000.

I have Top Pair, Top Kicker (TPTK) with Ace-Queen on a board of Qs-Th-7s. This is a very strong hand in a 3-bet pot heads-up. The board is quite coordinated, with several straight draws (KJ, J9, 98) and a spade flush draw possible in Minni's range. I need to bet here for value and to protect my hand against these draws. A bet of around 40% of the pot (800 chips) is standard for a continuation bet in a 3-bet pot, allowing me to build the pot while making it expensive for draws to continue.

Call — total commit 1800. Flash 3‑bet preflop to 1000 and now leads 800 into a 2800 pot (small ~28% probe). Calling 800 into 2800 requires ~22% equity. I have A♠6♣: Ace‑high with useful blocker (A♠) and some backdoor possibilities; in position against a 3‑betting player this has sufficient showdown value and equity vs a wide 3‑bet/c‑bet range. Raising here would bloat the pot with no made hand and limited fold equity versus a polarized 3‑bet range, while folding surrenders good implied‑odds spots. Plan: call to see the turn and use position to barrell, check‑raise, or fold as appropriate. If the turn brings an A or a spade (gives real river outs), I’ll play aggressively for value/with the draw; if it’s a blank and Flash shows heavy aggression I’ll fold. Calling keeps the most +EV options open.

The turn is a 2h, which is a complete brick and doesn't change the strength of my Top Pair, Top Kicker (TPTK). I'm still ahead of most of Minni's calling range from the flop, including weaker Queens (KQ, QJ), pocket pairs like JJ or 99, and various draws (KJ, J9, 89, and spade flush draws). I want to continue betting for value and to protect my hand against these draws. A bet of 2000 in a 3600 pot (roughly 55%) is a good sizing to keep the pressure on and build the pot for a potential river shove if the board stays relatively clean.

Pot math: Flash has bet 2,000 into a 5,600 pot — calling 2,000 to win ~7,600 requires ~26% equity. My holding A♠6♣ on Q♠ T♥ 7♠ 2♥ is now only ace‑high; the flop backdoor spade possibility is dead after a heart turn, so I have no real draw and only showdown value. Flash is an aggressive 3‑better who led the flop and now barrels the turn — that line is heavily weighted toward value (Qx, Tx, sets, overpairs) which dominate my hand. A thin call is unlikely to have enough equity vs his continuing range and a raise would be a very marginal bluff (I lack the necessary blockers and fold equity). Consistent with my earlier plan (blank turn + heavy aggression → fold), I preserve stack and fold this marginal spot.

I have Ace-Trey of different suits (A3o) on the Button/Small Blind. In a heads-up situation, an Ace is a strong starting hand. Raising to 300 is a standard open from this position to put pressure on the Big Blind and build the pot when I have a range advantage.

Fold. I’m in the big blind with 3♠5♥ facing a button/SB open to 300. Calling 200 into a 400 pot requires ~33% equity, and 3‑5 offsuit has very poor equity and postflop playability out of position versus an aggressive opener who C‑bets and barrels frequently. A light 3‑bet is also a bad idea here — I’ll be playing a big pot OOP with a weak hand. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with pocket 3s and deep stacks (23k). This is a hand I want to play aggressively preflop to take the initiative, build the pot, and get some fold-equity while preserving set-mining implied odds. The min-raise is 200, but I'll open to a standard 3x BB (300) — big enough to charge broad BB defense and consistent with my table image. If called I'll c-bet dry, proceed cautiously on highly coordinated boards, and look to realize/set-mine with position.

I am holding 5h 6h, which is a solid suited connector. In a heads-up match against Minni, this hand has sufficient equity and playability to defend from the big blind against a standard button raise. The pot odds are favorable (200 to call into a pot of 400), and folding would be too tight. I will call to see a flop and evaluate my options based on how well it connects with my hand.

On a flop of 4c-Jd-3s, I have an open-ended straight draw (3-4-5-6) needing a 2 or a 7. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the button and see how they react. I have good equity with the draw and can comfortably call a continuation bet or potentially check-raise to put pressure on their range, but checking first is the correct tactical starting point.

I have a set of threes (3h3d) on 4c Jd 3s — a very strong hand heads‑up but only behind a few combos (pocket J or pocket 4). Flash checked the flop, which can be weakness or a disguised monster, but my priority is to extract value from worse pairs (Jx, weaker pocket pairs, Kx) and to deny free cards to unlikely straight/connector draws. I’ll bet for value ~2/3 pot so worse hands will call and I can still comfortably call a reasonable check‑raise. If Flash jams an enormous overbet I’ll re‑evaluate, but default plan is to build the pot and get value now.

I have an open-ended straight draw with 5h 6h on a board of 4c Jd 3s. Minni has bet 400 into a 600 pot. I need to call 400 to win a total pot of 1400, giving me 2.5:1 pot odds. With 8 outs to a straight, I have approximately a 17% chance to hit on the turn and a 32% chance to hit by the river. Given the deep stacks and the potential to extract more value if I hit my straight (implied odds), a call is the mathematically sound and strategic choice.

On a board of 4c-Jd-3s-7h, my 6h-5h has completed a straight (3-4-5-6-7). This is currently the nuts or extremely close to it. Being out of position, I want to maximize value. Since Minni was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, the best strategy is to check to her, allowing her to continue her narrative and either bluff or value bet with her holding (likely a Jack or a high pocket pair). This keeps the pot deceptive and sets up a potential check-raise.

I have a very strong hand — a set of threes (3h3d). Flash called my flop bet, then checked the turn (7h), which is a harmless card that doesn’t complete relevant draws. With one opponent and deep stacks I should be extracting value rather than slowing down. A turn bet also denies free cards to the few backdoor combos and builds the pot for river. I’ll size to get called by worse hands (Jx, small pocket pairs, maybe 4x) but not shove them off — target ~2/3–3/4 pot. If I face a large check‑raise I’ll re‑evaluate, but with trips I’m happy to continue. Bet 1000 into the 1400 pot to maximize value while keeping weaker calling hands in.

On the turn, the 7 of hearts completes my straight (3-4-5-6-7). This is currently the absolute nuts as no higher straight or flush is possible. Minni has continued her aggression from the flop with a bet of 1000 into a 1400 pot, suggesting she either has a strong made hand (like a set of 7s, 4s, or 3s, or a top-pair Jack) or is committed to a multi-street bluff. Since the board is relatively disconnected, my straight is very well-disguised. I will check-raise to 3000 to build the pot immediately while I have the best hand, targeting her value range which is unlikely to fold given the strength she has shown.

I have a very strong made hand — trips (3h3d) on 4c Jd 3s 7h. Flash check‑called the flop (wide/calling range) then check‑raised my turn bet big to 3,000. Very few hands currently beat me (primarily pocket Js, pocket 4s, or pocket 7s — a small number of combos); most of his range (single pair Jx, weaker pairs, bluffs) I crush. Calling 2,000 more (3,000 total) into a 5,400 pot gives excellent pot odds (~27%) and lets me realize equity and extract value on the river. Re‑raising would bloat the pot and needlessly commit more when I can be behind to those few dominating combos. I’ll call and re‑evaluate on the river — extract value from worse hands or fold to insurmountable action if the river changes things.

On the river, the board is 4c-Jd-3s-7h-5s and I hold 6h-5h, giving me a straight (3-4-5-6-7). This hand is exceptionally strong, only losing to a higher straight (8-6). Given Minni's pre-flop raise from the button and her willingness to call a large check-raise on the turn, she likely has a strong value hand such as a set (JJ, 77, 44, 33), two-pair, or a high overpair (AA, KK). While 8-6 is in her range, it is statistically less likely than the many sets and two-pairs I currently beat. I will lead with a value bet of 3700 (approximately half the pot) to extract maximum value from these second-best hands, as checking might allow her to check back marginal value hands that would otherwise call a moderate bet.

I have trip 3s (3h3d) on 4c Jd 3s 7h 5s — a very strong hand. Flash’s line (check-call flop, huge check-raise on the turn) is polarized toward strong value hands (pocket J/4/7 sets/overpairs) or occasional big bluffs/semi‑bluffs. The river 5s only adds a few new hands that can beat me (notably any 6 that now completes 3-4-5-6-7 straights, or very specific A2 straights); most of Flash’s range (single-pair Jx, smaller pairs, bluffs) I still crush. Pot is 11,100 and it costs 3,700 to call — about 25% immediate pot odds (11100+3700 = 14,800 total if I call). That price is excellent to call for value vs a wide/Polarized range and to realize my equity against bluffs. Raising here would both fold out hands I want to extract value from and unnecessarily bloat the pot against the small subset of hands that beat me. I’ll call 3,700 and go to showdown to collect value from worse pairs and bluffs.

Holding 93o in the small blind (Heads-Up) is a very weak hand. While I have a significant stack advantage over Minni (24300 vs 15550), 93o doesn't play well post-flop and doesn't have much potential even in a blind-vs-blind situation. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chips.

Heads-up on the button with Qh4c I should be opening very wide. I have position postflop and deep stacks, so raising to take the initiative and generate fold equity is the best line. A 3x BB sizing (300 total) is standard here — big enough to discourage cheap floats but small relative to our stacks so we can fold to a big 3-bet if necessary. If Flash calls I can use my positional advantage to realize equity or apply pressure; calling 50 is too passive and folding is too tight. I’ll raise to 300 total (i.e. 250 more).

I am in the Big Blind with 9s8c, a decent suited connector-type hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. In a heads-up situation, 98o is strong enough to defend the Big Blind against a 3x open. Calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has good playability and straight potential without bloating the pot out of position. I'll call the 200 more.

On this flop of 8d-3d-3c, I have top pair (Eights) with a Nine kicker on a paired board. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Since I am out of position, checking is the standard play to see how Minni reacts to the flop. I plan to check-call or potentially check-raise depending on the bet size, but starting with a check is best to control the pot and induce bluffs.

I opened preflop and have the range advantage heads-up. The flop (8♦ 3♦ 3♣) is very dry and low — most of the BB’s calling range missed, and a lot of hands will check back. With Q♥4♣ I have nothing but a good bluff candidate: a small continuation bet will take down the pot often, charge any two-diamond draws, and keep the pot controllable if called. I’ll use a ~1/3-pot c-bet (200 into 600) to maximize folding equity while leaving room to fold to significant aggression. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn (check behind blank turns or barrel selectively with equity); if he check-raises large I give it up—this is a standard positional bluff to exploit his check.

On the flop of 8d-3d-3c, I have top pair (Eights) with a Nine kicker on a paired board. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Minni has made a small bet of 200 into a pot of 600. I have multiple options here: calling to keep her bluffs in and control the pot size, or raising to protect against various draws (like diamonds) and get value from smaller pairs or Ace-high. Since the board is quite dry aside from the flush draw and my hand is vulnerable to overcards, a call is standard to keep her range wide. However, Minni has been aggressive, and I want to start building the pot with what is likely the best hand. I will call to see how the turn develops and evaluate my position then.

On the turn, the board is 8d-3d-3c-2d. I have top pair (Eights) with a nine kicker. The deuce of diamonds completes potential flushes, which is a concern. Minni has checked behind on the turn after I checked to her. This check-back from Minni often suggests a medium-strength hand, a marginal draw, or a surrender. Given the board texture, my pair of Eights is still likely the best hand. I will check again to control the pot size and see if she wants to bluff the river or if we go to a cheap showdown.

Preflop I opened wide (300) which is standard heads-up. On the dry flop (8♦ 3♦ 3♣) I c‑bet 200 as a small fold‑equity bluff; Flash called, which narrows his range to showdown‑worthy hands (8x, 3x/trips, some broadways) plus diamond draws. The turn 2♦ is a problem for my bluff — it brings a third diamond to the board, meaning any two‑diamond combo in his range now has a made flush and many more of his calling combos still have decent showdown value. I have Q♥4♣ (no diamonds, no pair) and very little equity vs his likely calling range. Betting here would risk getting called or check‑raised by hands that beat me and my fold equity is significantly reduced by the turn diamond. Best play is to control the pot and realize equity: check behind, keep the pot manageable, and re‑evaluate the river. If he barrels the river I’ll fold to large aggression and consider a call to small/medium bets depending on the sizing and river texture. Action: check.

On the river, the board is 8d-3d-3c-2d-8s. I hold 8c-9s, giving me a Full House (Eights full of Threes). This is an exceptionally strong hand. Minni checked back on the turn when the third diamond appeared, suggesting she likely doesn't have a flush or a monster hand like a 3. However, with the river pairing the 8, I have improved significantly. I will lead with a value bet of 600 (60% of the pot) to get called by any pair she might have (like a pocket pair or a deuce) or potentially an Ace-high that she thinks might be good for a hero call on this board texture.

Recap: I opened pre, c‑bet the dry flop (8♦ 3♦ 3♣) 200 into ~600 and Flash called. Turn 2♦ completed a possible diamond flush and both of us checked. River paired the board (8♠) and Flash bets 600 into a 1000 pot (pot now 1600). I hold Q♥4♣ — no pair, no diamond, essentially air. Pot odds: I must call 600 to win 1600, so I need ~37.5% equity to justify a call. Range analysis: Flash’s line (call flop, check turn) narrows to lots of medium/strong holdings — 8x, 3x/trips, some pocket pairs that will now value‑bet, plus completed flushes from turn diamonds. He also has missed diamond draws and some air/overcards that could be bluffing or making a small block/value bet on the river. A raise here (min to 1200) would be a pure bluff without good blockers and would often be called by worse 8x or crushed by trips/flush/full houses, so it’s high variance with poor EV. Given the smallish river sizing and decent pot odds, calling is the lowest‑variance +EV option: I’m unlikely to be ahead vs his value hands, but the call is cheap enough to catch his bluffs/missed draws while preserving stack depth for future hands. I’ll call 600 and show down.

I am on the button with King-high (Kd 2h). In heads-up play, a King is generally strong enough to open for a raise to put pressure on the big blind. I have a significant stack lead over Minni and have been successful with 3bb (300) opening raises in recent hands. I will raise to 300 to try and take the pot pre-flop or play the hand with positional advantage.

Facing a button open to 300 from the SB, I have 6♠3♦ in the big blind. To call is 200 into a 400 pot (pot odds = 25%). 6-3 offsuit is very weak, is often dominated by a typical SB opening range, and I’ll be out of position postflop with little implied odds or fold equity. A 3-bet is pure bluff with poor blockers and unnecessary risk. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with K♠4♥. In heads-up play I should be opening a wide range — K4o is playable in position and has good fold/blocker value. Flash in the BB has shown aggression but also defends widely, so I want a size that generates meaningful fold equity and discourages light 3-bets. I'll raise to 300 total (I’ve already posted 50, so I’m adding 250). If Flash 3-bets big I will fold this hand; if he calls I’ll use positional advantage to c-bet favorable boards, check dry/unfavorable flops, and look to extract value when I hit. This sizing balances aggression and protection of my range and maximizes expected value heads-up.

I am holding Ten of Clubs and Six of Spades in the Big Blind. Minni, who is currently the only other player at the table, has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. While T6o has some potential in a heads-up environment due to pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), it is generally a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive opponent. Given my significant stack lead and the fact that we are approaching the final hands of the game, I prefer to fold marginal hands and wait for better spots rather than risking chips out of position with a weak range.

On the button (SB) with Ace-Deuce offsuit (A2o) in a heads-up situation. My stack is considerably larger than Minni's (25,350 vs 14,500). A2o is a strong enough hand to raise for value and to maintain aggression from the button. I'll raise to the standard 300 to build the pot and apply pressure.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300; I’m in the big blind with J♥3♣. With deep stacks (145/251 BB) and wide heads-up opening ranges, J3o is a defendable but marginal hand. Folding is too nit‑tight and a 3‑bet OOP with this junk will bloat the pot without much equity or playability. Calling 200 (total commitment 300) gives me correct pot odds (200 to win ~600 → ~33% required equity) and keeps the pot manageable while preserving post‑flop play options. Plan: proceed cautiously OOP — check/float on favorable flops, fold to heavy action on completely missed boards, and look to realize equity when I pair my J or pick up backdoor draws.

Check. Flop is very dry (9h 6c 4d) and I’m out of position with Jh3c — only an overcard and a weak backdoor club. Flash opens very wide from the button and cbets a lot, so my plan is to keep the pot small and choose spots to float rather than bloat the pot with a marginal hand. By checking I do three things: (1) allow him to bluff/cbet so I can call small-to-medium bets and realize my equity with an overcard, (2) fold to large, polarized pressure which I’m usually crushed by OOP, and (3) keep the option to take the pot away on a favorable turn with a delayed c-bet. Concrete thresholds: if Flash bets small (roughly ≤½ pot, ≲300) I will call and re-evaluate on the turn; if he fires a large pot-sized or near-pot bet I’ll fold. Checking retains flexibility and protects my stack deep SPR.

On the button, I raised pre-flop with A2o and was called by Minni. The flop is 9h-6c-4d, which is relatively dry. Minni has checked to me. While I haven't hit the board, Ace-high often still holds the best hand in heads-up play, and I have some backdoor potential. I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to maintain my aggression, represent a strong hand (like a higher pair or a set), and attempt to take down the pot immediately.

Flash opened wide from the button and his flop stab (300 into 900, ~1/3 pot) is a small c‑bet representing a very wide range. I have J♥3♣ on 9♥ 6♣ 4♦ — a weak hand but with 6 immediate pair outs (three Js + three 3s) and backdoor club/runner possibilities. Calling 300 into a pot that will be 1500 after my call requires ~20% equity, which is reasonable against a wide c‑betting range. Plan: keep the pot small OOP, call the small c‑bet to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn. Fold to large, polarized turn pressure unless I improve (pair a J/3 or pick up meaningful backdoors); if the turn is a club, a J/3, or a scare card that gives me a good bluffing window I can continue. Deep stacks make 3‑betting/raising with this junk undesirable. Action: call 300.

I called the small flop c‑bet with J♣3♣ to realize equity; the turn (2♣) is a blank for my hand. I no longer have any realistic flush outs (only one card left), and my only clean outs are three Js + three 3s = 6 outs (~6/46 ≈ 13% to hit the river). Pot is 1,200 and stacks are deep (14k effective) — I should not bloat the pot OOP with a one‑card, ~13% chance to improve. Betting here would be a thin bluff against an opponent who c‑bets wide but will also call barrels too often; checking controls the pot and lets me fold comfortably to aggressive turn pressure or see a cheap river where my pair can be evaluated. Plan: check and evaluate Flash’s action on the river — call small river bets if I hit, fold to large pressure if I miss.

On the turn, the Deuce of Clubs (2c) pairs my bottom card, giving me bottom pair with an Ace kicker. While I have improved, this is a marginal hand. Minni checked again, which could indicate a weak hand or a trap with a Nine. Checking back here allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and potentially catch a bluff on the river or reach showdown cheaply. It also prevents me from being check-raised off a hand that has decent showdown value in a heads-up pot.

The river (9♥ 6♣ 4♦ 2♣ 5♣) gives me a 2-3-4-5-6 straight using my 3♣ — a very strong but somewhat disguised hand. Flash’s line (button open, small 300 flop c-bet, check on the turn) looks like a wide range of single-pair hands, overcards, and missed draws; many of those will call a river value bet. The main hands that beat me are backdoor two-club combos (a nut flush) or a very unlikely higher straight/full-house line, both low-probability given his passive turn. I want to extract value from worse pairs (9x,6x,5x,4x, etc.) while keeping the pot controlled against the small chance of being crushed. Bet ~2/3 pot to maximize calls from inferior hands but avoid bloating the pot against possible strong club combos.

I hold bottom pair (deuces) with an Ace kicker on a board of 9h-6c-4d-2c-5c. While Ace-high was strong on the flop, and pairing the deuce on the turn improved my hand to a pair, the river 5c is a very coordinated card. The board now contains 9, 6, 5, 4, 2. My pair of deuces is the lowest possible pair on this board. Minni's lead for 800 into a 1200 pot represents significant strength or a well-timed bluff. Considering any 4, 5, 6, or 9 beats me, and any 3 makes a straight, my hand is only a bluff-catcher against total air like 7-8 or J-T. Given that even some of her potential 'bluffs' like a 3 or a 5 would have improved to beat me on the river, the most profitable move here is to fold and preserve my chip lead.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind). Opening very wide is standard — I have a weak hand (6♥2♠) but position and initiative matter a lot heads-up. A raise to 200 (2×BB) is a small, standard steal size: it gives fold equity, lets me play the rest of the hand in position, and keeps the pot manageable if Flash defends. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable dry flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated boards. Stack sizes are deep enough that this is a low-commitment, high-expected-value open.

I am in the Big Blind with J9o. The Button (Minni) has raised to 200, which is a min-raise. I need to call 100 more to see a flop. My hand has decent playability in a heads-up pot, especially getting 3:1 on the call. Minni's stack is shorter (150bb) while I am deep (247bb). Folding is too tight given the price, and raising isn't necessary with a mid-strength hand out of position. I will call and see a flop.

On the flop of Ad-3c-Js, I have middle pair (Jacks) with a Nine kicker. This is a solid check-calling hand in this situation. Minni raised pre-flop and the board contains an Ace, which is well within her range. However, she only min-raised pre-flop and the pot is small (400 chips). I will check to see how she reacts. If she bets a standard amount, I will likely call to see a turn, as middle pair is too strong to fold immediately on this board texture against a single bet.

Heads-up on the button I opened wide preflop and have position/initiative. My hand (6♦2♠) has no pair and no backdoor draw on A♦–3♣–J♠, but a large portion of the caller’s range missed this flop (Kx, Qx, small pairs, many broadways). A small c-bet leverages initiative, folds out a lot of those holdings, and keeps the pot manageable if I run into resistance (there’s also some chance Flash checked with a medium hand or a hand without an Ace). I’ll make a compact bluff — ~1/3–40% pot — to maximize fold equity while leaving room to fold to a sizable check‑raise. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and barrel only when I pick up equity or see a favorable card; if he check‑raises big I’ll release. Betting 150 chips achieves that balance.

On a board of Ad-3c-Js, I have middle pair with Jack of Clubs. Minni's continuation bet is very small (150 into a pot of 400), offering me excellent odds of about 3.6:1 to call. Middle pair is easily strong enough to defend against such a small sizing, as Minni could be betting with a wide range of hands including gutshots (like KQ or QT), smaller pairs, or total air. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate.

On the turn, the board has paired the Jack, giving me Three of a Kind. This is a very strong hand. Since Minni was the pre-flop and flop aggressor, I will check to her to allow her to continue betting her bluffs or her weaker made hands like an Ace. Leading out here might cause her to fold hands that she would otherwise bet herself. If she checks behind, I will plan to bet for value on the river.

I c-bet the flop with a wide heads-up range and achieved the usual result — villain called, showing a range of A‑x, J‑x, broadways and medium pairs. The turn (Jh) actually makes the board worse for my bluffing prospects: it pairs the J (giving any J in villain’s hand trips) and turns Ax into a comfortable two‑pair holding that will often call or check‑raise a turn bet. I hold 6♥2♠ — no pair, no realistic draw and virtually zero showdown value. With pot ~700 and no fold equity worth buying vs a caller who has shown willingness to call the flop, betting here is thin EV and exposes me to big check‑raises or being called down by Ax/Jx. I’ll check back, keep the pot controllable and use position to evaluate river (look to bluff only when a highly favorable scare card or a clear free card presents itself; fold to significant aggression).

On the river, the board is Ad-3c-Js-Jh-5h and I have Trip Jacks (J9). Minni checked back the turn, which likely caps her range. She likely has a weak Ace (Ax) that is now playing cautiously against the Jack, or a smaller pair. If I check, she is very likely to check back for showdown value. By betting, I can extract value from her Aces or any other marginal made hands. A bet of 500 into the 700 pot (roughly 71% pot) is a standard value sizing that looks like it could be a missed draw or a thin value bet, potentially enticing a call from her holding an Ace.

I checked the river as planned because 6♥2♠ has essentially no showdown value on A♢–3♣–J♠–J♥–5♥. Flash’s line (call flop, check the paired turn, then lead the river) is exactly what I’d expect from value hands: A‑x for top pair or J‑x that slow‑played/checked for pot control — both beat my holding. The river sizing (500 into the ~700 pot before the bet) requires ~29–30% equity to justify a call (500 to win ~1,700), and I have virtually none versus a value-heavy range. A raise would be a pure bluff with very thin fold equity against this opponent, who has shown he calls down frequently. Fold and conserve chips for better spots.

I am holding 9h 2d in the small blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker. While we are playing heads-up and I have a significant chip lead, there is no reason to play such a poor hand against Minni, who has shown willingness to defend and play aggressively. Folding here is the standard and most disciplined play to avoid unnecessary losses.

Heads-up on the button with Q♦5♥ — this is a hand I want to play for initiative.
