In heads-up the opening range is wide, and raising to the min (200 total, 2x BB) seizes initiative, leverages my positional advantage post-flop, and often wins the 150-chip blind pot immediately. If Flash calls I can use position to c-bet favorable flops or control the pot when I miss; I will fold to a substantial 3‑bet since Q5o doesn't warrant getting into a big OOP pot. Raising to 200 is the balanced +EV line here.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s7h. Minni, who is on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 200. This is a small raise (min-raise), and I only need to call 100 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 400. 67o is a reasonable hand to defend against a wide button opening range, especially getting 3:1 pot odds. I will call to see a flop and evaluate my post-flop play.

I defended my big blind with 6s7h against Minni's min-raise. The flop comes Ks-8d-2s. This board is generally better for the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes more strong kings and high cards. I have missed the flop completely, with only a very weak backdoor straight draw and no overcards or direct draws. My opponent, Minni, is likely to place a continuation bet here. I have no reason to lead out or build a pot with this hand. I will check and, most likely, fold to any significant bet unless I pick up a draw on the turn.

Heads‑up I was the preflop aggressor and have position — that gives me initiative to continuation‑bet. The flop K♠ 2♠ 8♦ is fairly dry and the opponent checked, so a c‑bet will often pick up the pot or charge draws. My Q♦5♥ has no pair and no spade, but Q♦ is a useful blocker to some Qx hands and I still have fold equity. I’ll bet a standard ~1/2 pot to apply pressure (200). Plan: fold to a large check‑raise; if called proceed cautiously — continue only if I improve or can credibly barrel turns that are safe for my range.

I defended the big blind with 76o and the flop is K-8-2 rainbow. I have no pair and no real draw. Minni has made a continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 400. This is a common play for the pre-flop aggressor on a king-high board. Since I have zero equity and no high-card value, there is no reason to continue in the hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Qs Jd, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up game. It has good equity and playability against Minni's likely range. Raising is the standard play here to seize the initiative and build a pot with a hand that is well above average. I will raise to 300.

Flash opened to 300 from the button (SB). I'm in the big blind with J♣5♦. Pot is 400, I must call 200. In heads‑up play I should defend a wide range — J5o has some showdown equity and blocks some Jx holdings. Pot odds: calling 200 into the 400 pot (~33% breakeven) and vs a wide open range my equity is around or above that, so a call is +EV. A small 3‑bet to 500 is possible as a bluff, but J5o is too weak to build a bigger pot out of position against an opponent who opens very frequently and can 4‑bet or apply pressure. I’ll call to see a flop, keep the pot manageable, and plan to proceed based on flop texture (check‑call small c‑bets on dry boards, look to raise/lead only with real equity).

Board is 9♣ 7♥ 9♦ and I have J♣5♦ — no pair, only 6 outs to make a pair (≈25% to improve by river) and no meaningful backdoor draws. I’m out of position and Flash is a frequent, wide opener who will c‑bet many hands that currently beat me (pocket 9s, 7x, overpairs) as well as some bluffs. I want to keep the pot small and preserve stack depth for later streets when I have more information. Checking controls the pot, allows me to call small continuation bets to realize equity, and fold to large ones — or take a free card if he checks back. Plan: check and re‑evaluate on the turn (call small c‑bets, fold to large pressure, or pursue a hit).

On the flop of 9c 7h 9d, I have two overcards to the 7. While this board doesn't hit my Qs Jd directly, I have the range advantage as the pre-flop raiser, especially since Minni checked. A continuation bet here is effective for folding out hands that have missed and for maintaining the initiative. The board is somewhat dry, though it contains a pair of nines, making it less likely she has a 9. I will bet 300 to put pressure on her air and weak holdings.

The flop 9♣ 7♥ 9♦ with J♣5♦ gives me ~6 outs (~24% to pair by the river). Flash’s 300 into the ~600 preflop pot means I’m getting about 25% break‑even equity (300 to win 1200 → 25%), so raw equity is essentially a tossup. Given Flash is a wide, frequent opener who will c‑bet a lot and my stacks are deep, the lowest‑variance +EV line is to call: I keep the pot small, realize equity, and preserve fold equity on later streets. Raising OOP would be a thin bluff that unnecessarily bloats the pot; folding to a small c‑bet here is too tight. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve, call small turn bets, and extract value or bluff only when I pick up a real hand or a clear read.

Check. Board is 9♣ 7♥ 9♦ 6♦; I hold J♣5♦ and we’re out of position with a weak hand and limited showdown equity. My clean outs: 3 J + 3 fives + 4 eights = 10 outs to a pair/straight on the river ≈ 10/46 ≈ 21.7% equity. Pot is 1,200 — checking keeps the pot under control and lets me realize equity or call a small bet with correct pot odds instead of bluffing OOP into a wide but often‑made range. Break‑even math: with P=1,200 calling B=300 requires ~16.7% equity, B=400 requires 20%, B=500 requires ~22.7%. So I’m happy to check and call modest turn bets (roughly ≤ ~400–450) but will fold to large turn bets or raises. If Flash checks back I get a free river; if I hit a J/5/8 on the river I can proceed for value; otherwise I preserve stack and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

Minni called the flop bet on a paired board, which suggests she has either a pair, a nine, or a straight draw. The six on the turn doesn't improve my Queen-Jack, and it's a card that could have potentially connected with her range. Since she checked again, I will check behind to control the size of the pot and realize my equity. This allows me to see the river for free and potentially win at showdown against her missed draws or high-card hands that I currently beat, while avoiding a check-raise.

River is 9♣ 7♥ 9♦ 6♦ K♥; I hold J♣5♦ — no pair, no backdoor. Flash opened, c‑bet the flop and then checked the turn; that line is consistent with either a flop value hand pot‑controlling (9x, 7x, a medium pocket) or a missed c‑bet/bluff he gave up on. With the K on the river many parts of his range get at least a decent showdown (Kx) or remain ahead (9x, 7x); my raw showdown equity as J‑high is very low. I could attempt a small river bluff, but to make that +EV I need a fairly high fold rate (e.g. ~25–33% for a 1/3–1/2 pot bet) and Flash has shown tendencies to call down and to exercise pot control rather than fold many of these lines. Given the small pot (1,200) relative to stacks and my weak holding, the lowest variance, highest EV line is to check and go to showdown rather than risk additional chips on a thin bluff.

On a board of 9c-7h-9d-6d-Kh, I have Queen-high. Minni called my continuation bet on the flop but checked the turn and river. While the King on the river is a potential scare card that I could represent to bluff, my check on the turn makes a bluff look less like a value play. Furthermore, my Queen-high has some marginal showdown value against her missed draws (such as 10-8 or 5-4) that might have floated the flop. Since Minni is unlikely to fold a pair of sevens or better to a standard river bet, and I'd rather avoid a check-raise, I will check behind to see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button with A♠9♦ and deep stacks (~144bb). This is a strong hand in heads-up play and we have positional advantage postflop, so I want to seize the initiative, build the pot and apply pressure to the BB. A min-raise to 200 accomplishes that (gets fold equity, isolates Flash and lets us play postflop in position). If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate (call versus small/standard 3-bets to realize equity and play in position; fold or proceed cautiously against large 3-bets). If called, plan is to c-bet most Ace-high or dry flops, and slow-play/top-pair when appropriate.

In Hand #140, I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. Minni, on the Button (Small Blind), has raised to 200. This is a standard heads-up opening raise. Ace-high hands are generally strong in heads-up play and are well within the calling or even 3-betting range. Given the pot odds (calling 100 to win a pot of 300) and the strength of an Ace against a Button opening range, calling is a solid, low-variance play to see a flop. Alternatively, a 3-bet could be used for value and to take control of the hand, but since I have a significant stack lead and want to maintain a steady profit, calling is a safe and effective way to play this hand.

On a paired board of 2-4-4, my Ace-Seven offsuit is currently likely the best hand, as it is difficult for my opponent to have a four. In heads-up play, Ace-high has significant showdown value. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see if they want to continuation bet. This keeps the pot manageable and allows me to either catch a bluff or see a turn card cheaply.

Flop is 2♦-4♦-4♠, pot 400, I have A♠9♦ and Flash checked. Heads‑up I have position and I'm ahead of a large portion of his preflop calling range (missed broadways, Kx, weaker Ax) while also blocking some strong A‑x combos. This board is vulnerable to single‑card turns (diamond draws, one‑card trips), so I want to use my initiative to both gain fold equity and protect my hand. A half‑pot c‑bet to 200 balances extracting value from worse A/Kx hands and folding out floats, while keeping the pot manageable if he shows strength. Plan: bet 200; if he folds we win; if he calls we re‑evaluate on the turn (continue with diamonds/A turns, exercise pot control or fold to large raises that polarize to trips/monster draws).

On a paired board of 2d-4d-4s, I hold Ace-high (Ac7h). This is a strong hand in a heads-up pot, especially as it beats most of Minni's air and semi-bluffs. Minni's 200 bet into a 400 pot is a standard continuation bet on a board where it is unlikely I have improved. I have the best hand frequently here and also have the nut no-pair hand. Calling is the best option to keep her bluffs in and minimize losses if she actually has a piece of the board (like a pocket pair or a 4). I'm not looking to raise yet, as that might fold out hands I currently beat.

On the turn, the board has become significantly wetter with the 5 of diamonds, completing potential flushes and straights (A-2-3-4-5 or 2-3-4-5-6). My Ace-high (Ac7h) still has some showdown value, but it is now a marginal hand. I will check to control the size of the pot and see how Minni reacts. Betting into this board would likely only result in being called or raised by better hands, whereas checking allows me to potentially see a cheap river or catch a bluff.

Heads‑up on the button with A♠9♦. Board (T): 2♦ 4♦ 4♠ 5♦, pot 800. I c‑bet the flop and was called, so Flash’s range is wide: Ax/Kx, some pocket pairs, suited connectors and diamond combos. The turn 5♦ is double‑edged — it gives anyone holding two diamonds a made flush, but it also gives me 9 river outs to a diamond and I still have the best high‑card (Ace) vs much of his range. Flash checked the turn, which can be weakness or a slow‑play. I want to charge worse Ax/Kx and unpaired hands while keeping the pot manageable vs hands that currently beat me (trips, 5x, or a made flush). A half‑pot continuation (400) applies pressure, folds out a lot of marginal hands, and preserves options: fold to a large check‑raise (polarized to monsters), call a small raise only with a read that he’s light, and if he simply calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river (value bet on Ace/diamond rivers, check‑control blanks). Betting 400 balances protection and pot control here.

On the turn, the board has become extremely coordinated with the 5 of diamonds, completing potential flushes and straights (any 3 now makes a straight). While I have Ace-high, I do not hold a diamond, which significantly limits my equity and ability to continue if another diamond falls on the river. Minni's bet of 400 into 800 (half-pot) on the turn indicates she is either value betting a made hand or continuing a bluff on a scare card. Given the high probability that she has improved her hand or is holding a diamond, and considering my strong stack lead, I will fold this marginal hand to avoid a potentially larger loss on the river.

I have been dealt a pocket pair of sixes (6s 6c) on the button (Small Blind). In a heads-up situation against Minni, a pocket pair is a very strong starting hand. I am currently in position and have a significant chip lead (25,050 to 14,800). I want to build the pot and take control of the hand pre-flop. A standard open-raise is effective here. I will raise to 300, which is my consistent sizing for pre-flop raises.

Flash opened to 300 from the button (SB). I’m in the big blind with K♣8♣ and 14800 behind (deep stacks). K8s is a perfectly reasonable defend heads-up: it has top-card value, suitedness for flush/backdoor equity and decent playability postflop against a wide opening range. Calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity out of position; a 3-bet with K8s is a marginal bluff and I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a speculative hand. The call is cheap: I need to call 200 more into ~400 (pot after my call ~600), so I’m getting ~33% pot odds which K8s should have vs a wide SB opening range. Plan: call, see the flop, continue when I hit top pair or strong draws, fold to heavy action when I miss. Total to commit after my action will be 300.

I missed the flop — K♣8♣ has no pair and only backdoor club equity on Q♥2♥5♦. This is a very dry board and Flash is a frequent preflop opener who will c-bet a wide range. OOP I don’t want to bluff-c-bet and inflate the pot with a marginal hand. Checking preserves pot control, allows me to call small-to-medium continuation bets to realize equity or fold to large-sized aggression, and keeps my options for a cheap turn card (a K or club). Plan: check now; call a small/standard c-bet to see a turn but fold to heavy sizing or multi-barrel pressure since I have no pair and limited equity.

I have pocket sixes (6s 6c) on a board of Qh 2h 5d. I am ahead of most of Minni's range, although the Queen is a scare card. Since Minni checked to me, I will place a continuation bet to protect my hand and potentially end it now. A standard bet of half the pot (300) is appropriate here.

I checked the flop with K♣8♣ and Flash c‑bet 300 into a ~600 preflop pot (pot now 900). I have no pair but I have backdoor club equity and two overcards; versus a wide SB c‑bet range my equity (~20–30%) plus implied odds to hit a king or a club make a call profitable here. The c‑bet size gives me ~3:1 pot odds (need ≈25% equity), so I’ll call to see a turn and keep the pot under control. Plan: call the 300, fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve (pair a K, pick up a club draw, or get very good odds to continue).

I have K♣8♣ on Q♥–2♥–5♦–A♦ after calling the flop. I missed — the turn Ace heavily favors Flash’s c-bet range (Ax, some Qx, sets, two-pairs) and my backdoor club equity is gone. I only have king-high (3 clean outs to a K ≈12%), so there’s no value in barreling OOP and no equity to call a large turn bet. Pot control is the priority: check and see what Flash does. If he bets a meaningful size I’ll fold (won’t call large or medium turn bets with only 3 outs); I would only consider calling a very tiny probe that gives correct odds. If he checks back I get a free river to potentially pick up a king or bluff-collapse the pot on a safe river card.

On the turn, an Ace has appeared on the board (Qh 2h 5d Ad). My pocket sixes are now even more vulnerable, as many hands in Minni's calling range (like AX or better) have now improved. Minni checked again, which could be a trap or a sign of weakness with a middle pair or a draw. Since I have showdown value but the board is becoming increasingly dangerous, checking back to see the river for free is the most prudent play. This allows me to control the pot size while retaining the possibility of winning at showdown if the river is safe.

I defended K♣8♣ preflop and checked the flop/turn because I had no pair and wanted pot control. Flash checked the turn and checked behind on the river — a clear sign of weakness or pot-control with a marginal hand. I have no showdown value, so checking just concedes the pot; a small polarizing river bluff can win the pot outright. The pot is 1,200; a bet of 400 requires a break-even fold frequency of 400/(1,200+400)=25%. Given Flash’s line (flop c-bet then passive turn+river), I estimate he will fold enough of his missed/weak Qx and small pair hands to clear that threshold. I choose a small sizing to maximize fold equity while keeping risk limited if called. If he raises I will fold.

On the river, the board is Qh 2h 5d Ad 7c. I hold pocket sixes (6s 6c). The action went: I raised pre-flop and was called; I bet the flop and was called; the turn went check-check after an Ace appeared. Minni has now led out for 400 into a 1,200 pot. My hand is a marginal bluff catcher. Minni's line (calling flop, checking turn, betting small on river) could represent a weak Queen, a pair of sevens, or a missed heart draw. Given the pot odds (I need to be right 20% of the time to break even calling 400 to win 1,600), and the fact that I checked the turn showing weakness, it is likely Minni is betting a wide range including some bluffs or weaker pairs. I will call to see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button (also the small blind) I should be opening a wide range — T6o is marginal but perfectly reasonable to raise from this position. Raising takes the initiative, uses my positional advantage postflop, and often wins the pot outright. I’ll make a standard 2x BB open to 200 (total committed) — big enough to apply pressure but small enough to keep weaker hands and folds in the BB’s range. Plan: if Flash folds we pick up the blind; if he calls we’ll use position to continuation-bet favorable flops and control pot size on dangerous boards; if he 3-bets to a sizeable amount I’ll fold T6o (only consider calling very small 3-bets where pot odds justify it).

I have a pocket pair of sevens (7s 7d) in the Big Blind. Minni raised from the Small Blind to 200. This is a very strong hand heads-up, and I want to build a pot while I likely have the best hand. A 3-bet is the standard play here to take control of the hand and gain value. I will raise to 700 to put pressure on Minni.

Flash just 3-bet me to 700, so I must put in 500 more to call into a 900 pot. That call requires roughly 500/(900+500) ≈ 35.7% equity to break even. Ts6c offsuit is frequently dominated by a typical BB 3-bet range (high pairs, A/Kx, broadway combos) and rarely has >35% vs that range. 4-betting is a bad look with this hand — little fold equity and poor postflop playability — so the +EV line is to preserve my stack for better spots. I’ll fold and wait for a stronger holding or a better situation to apply aggression.

I am on the button with J7o, a marginal hand in heads-up play but often worth an open or a call of the small blind to see a flop given the stack depth. My stack is significantly larger than Minni's (26250 vs 13600). Minni has been playing a mix of tight and aggressive, but J7o is within a standard opening range from the SB. Raising to 300 is a standard move here to take control of the hand pre-flop.

Flash opened to 300 from the button and I'm in the big blind with Tc5s (offsuit). Heads-up vs a button open this is a marginal but defendable hand: it has decent playability (can hit top pair, two pair, straights) and I get good pot odds to see a flop. 3-betting with T5o is a losing frequency (poor blockers, no SD value) and folding is too nitty given the size of the raise and deep stacks. I’ll flat-call to 300 total to keep his wide opening range in and plan to fold to extreme pressure on dry flops, and to continue when I hit top pair/strong draw or get favorable runouts.

Flop: 5c Ac Qc. My hand Tc5s is second pair (pair of 5s) plus a strong club flush draw (9 outs). Versus a wide button opening range this is a very strong combination — good made hand plus big equity. Flash checked the flop which shows some weakness or pot control; this is exactly the spot to take the initiative and get value/protection. I want to pricingly extract calls from worse pairs, broadway hands and club draws while denying free cards to pure overcards. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot to achieve that without bloating the pot too much. If Flash check-raises big I’ll strongly consider calling (pair + 9 outs has a lot of equity) and re-evaluate on the turn; if it’s a small raise I’m committing to continue. Bet size chosen: 300 into the 600 pot.

On a board of 5c Ac Qc, I have Jack-high with no clubs. This board is heavily coordinated and favors a range that contains many Aces, Queens, and club draws. Minni lead out with a 300 bet (a 'donk' bet) into the pre-flop aggressor. Given that I have zero equity on this board and no draw, calling or raising would be highly speculative and likely unprofitable against a range that connects well with these cards. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play here.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 6♠7♦ — a marginal but playable hand in position. Against a single big blind I want to take the initiative, apply pressure and leverage position post‑flop. A raise to the min (200 total) is small, preserves pot control, generates fold equity, and keeps my range wide so I can continuation-bet favorable flops. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold most of the time; if he calls I’ll play aggressively on connected/low-card flops and pot-control on dry/overcards. Raising to 200 also costs only a small fraction of my stack (I’ve posted 50, so +150 more) while maximizing long-term profitability heads-up.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 6h. The Small Blind (Minni) has raised to 200. The cost to call is 100 into a pot that will be 400. This provides 4:1 pot odds. While 86o is a weak hand, in a heads-up situation with these odds, it's generally a marginal call to defend the blind, especially given our deep stacks and the need to occasionally see a flop with middle connectors. I will call and see a flop.

I have bottom pair (Sixes) on an Ace-high board. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, has a range advantage on this board given the presence of the Ace and the clubs. However, I have a piece of the board and checking to the aggressor is the standard play here to see how they react. If Minni bets, I will have to evaluate based on the size, but for now, checking is the most appropriate action.

I have middle pair (6♠) on A♣-2♣-6♦. Heads-up the preflop caller’s range is wide (many A-x, broadways, small pairs and draws), so an unchecked flop often contains a lot of air and hands I can fold out, but it also contains many Ax combos that already beat me. With position and a checked flop I should C‑bet to get value from worse pairs, pocket 2s/6s, and some draws while applying fold equity to overcards. I’ll use a standard half‑pot continuation bet (200 into 400) — big enough to charge/deny equity and extract value, small enough relative to stacks so I can fold to a significant check‑raise. If called I’ll pot‑control on most turns and fold to strong aggression if an Ace or scary card arrives.

I have middle pair (sixes) with an 8 kicker on an Ace-high board. Minni's 200 chip bet into a 400 chip pot is a standard continuation bet. With pot odds of 3:1 and a piece of the board, I have enough equity to call one street to see how the hand develops. Raising would likely only isolate me against stronger hands, and folding is too weak given the heads-up context and my hand strength.

On this turn (Jack of Spades), the board is Ace-Deuce-Six-Jack. I have a pair of sixes. I was the one who called a bet on the flop. As the player out of position, I will check to the aggressor (Minni) to see how they respond to the new card. Leading out into an Ace-high board where the opponent has shown aggression usually doesn't make sense with a marginal pair.

Flash checked the turn after calling my flop c-bet. I hold middle pair (6♠) with a weak kicker — heads‑up his flop call is weighted to Ax, broadways, small pairs and floats, many of which still beat me. The J♠ on the turn gives him additional Jx combos and doesn’t significantly remove A‑x combos, so betting here would bloat the pot and get called by many better hands while only extracting thin value from rare worse hands. I’ll check to control the pot, keep worse hands and bluffs in, and see a cheap river. If he barrels the river small I can call for showdown value or fold to large aggression unless I improve.

On the river, the board has paired the deuces (2c Ac 6d Js 2h). My best hand is two pair, sixes and deuces, with an ace kicker from the board (Ac 6d 6h 2c 2h). Minni checked the turn after I called her flop bet, which suggests she doesn't have a very strong hand like a top-tier Ace or a set. However, an Ace or a Jack still beats my pair of sixes. By checking, I can potentially see a cheap showdown or catch a bluff if she decides to bet with missed clubs or a lower value hand. Betting myself doesn't achieve much as I likely only get called by hands that beat me (Aces or Jacks).

Final board A♣–2♣–6♦–J♠–2♥. I have middle pair (6♠) with a weak kicker. Flash called my flop c‑bet and then both of us checked the turn; his line is weighted toward A‑x, Jx and medium+ pairs that beat me, with a subset of small pairs and missed broadways that I beat. The river paired the deuce, creating trips/full‑house possibilities for any 2‑x hands. Given Flash’s passive check‑call tendencies earlier, a river bet would extract thin value from very few worse hands and will often be called (or raised) by hands that already beat me. Pot is small (800) versus our stacks, so there’s limited fold equity and unnecessary risk in firing a value/bluff. I’ll take the low‑variance line and check to showdown to realize my equity.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with J9o. This is a standard opening range hand in heads-up play. Minni is in the Big Blind. I have a significant stack advantage (25,950 vs 13,900). Raising to 300 is a standard open here to apply pressure and take control of the hand.

Facing a 300 open from the SB with Qh6s in the BB, I want to defend but avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Q6o is too weak to 3‑bet for value and folding is too passive vs a wide heads‑up open. Calling keeps the pot controllable, realizes equity against a wide opening range, and lets me play postflop (c‑bet or check/raise depending on texture). I'll call and reassess on the flop.

Q6o has no pair and no meaningful draw on K‑9‑8 with two diamonds on board (I hold no diamond). Flash checking to me narrows his range toward lots of Kx/9x/8x and diamond draws — hands that will call a small probe and will often continue vs a bluff. I have very little equity and poor fold equity, so betting risks bloating the pot or facing a raise. I’ll keep the pot controlled: check and re‑evaluate on the turn, folding to significant pressure unless I improve.

I have middle pair (Nines) with a Jack kicker on a King-high board. As the pre-flop aggressor, after Minni checks, a continuation bet is standard here to protect my hand against various draws (diamonds, straight draws like QT or JT) and to get value from weaker hands or 8x. A bet of 300, which is half the pot, is a balanced sizing that maintains control of the pot while applying pressure.

Heads‑up vs a wide button opener who c‑bet small (300 into a 900 pot) on Kc‑9d‑8d (two diamonds). I have Qh6s — no pair, no diamonds and very little showdown value, but the bet is only one‑third pot. Calling 300 gives me ~25% pot odds to see a turn, and vs a reasonable c‑bet frequency my equity + implied odds justify a single‑street call. Plan: keep the pot under control, fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve, but continue if I pair the Q/6 or pick up a backdoor. A raise is too thin here and folding is too passive given the price. Call 300.

Heads‑up on the turn: I have Qh6s on Kc‑9d‑8d‑2s. I missed — no pair, no diamond blocker, very little showdown value. Flash is a wide button opener who c‑bet small on the flop, so his range contains a lot of Kx/9x/8x and diamond draws that are happy to continue. The 2s is a blank that doesn’t help my hand or block his draws. There is currently no bet to call, so the prudent line is pot control: check and keep the pot small. Betting as a bluff here has poor fold equity against his likely calling range and risks bloating the pot out of position with almost no equity. Plan: check, fold to significant turn aggression unless I pair on the river or can see a cheap showdown; call small bets only when price is right. Action: check.

On this King-high board, I have second pair (Nines) with a Jack kicker. The turn 2s is a complete blank that doesn't change the board texture. After Minni checks a second time, I have the option to bet for value or check back for pot control. Since my hand has decent showdown value but is unlikely to get three streets of value from worse hands, checking back is a solid play. It prevents me from getting check-raised by a King or a slow-played monster and allows me to see a river for free, potentially inducing a bluff or keeping the pot manageable for a showdown.

Check. I missed — Q6o has no pair, no diamond blocker and no meaningful showdown equity on K‑9‑8‑2‑J. Flash opened pre and continued on the flop, then both checked the turn; that line is weighted to at least a one‑pair holding (Kx/9x/8x), some Jx/TJ combos and the occasional missed diamond draw. A river bluff here has poor fold equity versus a button who has shown aggression and willingness to call — to make a ~800 chip bluff immediately profitable I’d need ≈40% folds, which I don’t expect. Pot control and preserving my 13.4k stack is the +EV line: check and go to showdown (or fold to heavy river aggression), rather than bloating the pot with a very weak hand.

I have improved to two pair (Jacks and Nines) on the river. The board texture is somewhat dangerous with a possible straight (QT), but Minni's repeated checking suggests she likely holds a marginal hand like a King or a pair of eights, or she missed a diamond draw. Since she checked the river, I should bet for value. A bet of 800 into the 1200 pot is a strong value sizing that can still be called by top pair (Kings) or even a stubborn middle pair.

I checked the river with Qh6s and missed — I have no pair, no diamond blocker and essentially zero value. The board is Kc-9d-8d-2s-Jc and Flash opened pre, c‑bet small on the flop, then checked the turn before making a modest 800 into a 2000 pot on the river. Calling 800 to win 2800 requires ~28.6% equity. My hand only beats pure air/bluffs and a few very thin lines — against Flash’s line (Kx/9x/8x, some Jx/Tx combos, and the occasional missed diamond) my realistic showdown equity is well below that threshold. Raising as a bluff to 1600 is also unattractive: I have no diamond blocker and very little fold equity versus Kx/9x/8x, so I’d risk more without a realistic chance to fold him out. With my stack size and the remaining hands to play, preserving chips and avoiding marginal, negative‑EV spots is correct. Fold.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with K♦4♦. In heads-up Kx suited is a perfectly reasonable open — I want the initiative and position postflop. A standard 2xBB open to 200 (total committed) gives fold equity, keeps my range wide, and lets me play position against Flash who often defends but also makes big turns/river plays. With deep stacks (133BB) I can comfortably continue on many flops with K-high + backdoor diamonds, and I’ll fold to a large, polarized 3-bet (call only vs light/small 3-bets if I think he’s bluffing). Raise to 200 to open the pot and take control.

I am currently holding 5h 3s, which is a very weak hand even for heads-up play. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, requiring me to call 100 more. While the pot odds are technically acceptable, the hand has poor playability out of position. Given that I am significantly ahead in chips and there are only five hands remaining in the session, I will choose to fold this trash hand and avoid unnecessary losses or complex post-flop situations.

I am on the button with 8d 7c. In heads-up play, this is a reasonable hand to open for a raise to maintain pressure and potentially take the blinds. I will raise to 300, which is consistent with my previous betting patterns.

Heads-up in the big blind with 4♠5♠ vs a button raise to 300. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call, so I’m getting ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity). 4-5 suited has good equity and excellent postflop playability against a wide button opening range, and stacks are deep enough to realize implied odds. A tiny 3-bet to the min (500) gives little fold equity and is inefficient with a speculative hand; a larger 3-bet would commit too much with a non-premium. I’ll call to 300 total to see a flop and play postflop (bet/call on favorable draws/pairs, pot-control or fold to heavy aggression when completely missed).

I’m out of position on A♠‑9♣‑4♣ with 4♠5♠ — I have a single pair (fours), which is a made but vulnerable hand (behind any A or 9 and dominated by many button openers). Flash opened a wide button range preflop and will c‑bet this texture frequently, often with A‑x or broadways. Leading out OOP gains little fold equity vs Ax and just bloats the pot when I’m likely behind; checking keeps the pot under control and lets me react. Plan: check and evaluate — call small/medium c‑bets to realize equity and keep worse hands in, fold to large polarized overbets, and if Flash checks back consider a turn stab on safe runouts or take a delayed value line. Therefore I’ll check here to control the size and follow a check‑call/pot‑control plan.

I raised pre-flop with 8d 7c and the flop comes Ace-high. As the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture heavily favors my perceived range. Since Minni checked, I will follow through with a standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to put pressure on her non-Ace hands and attempt to take the pot down immediately.

I flopped a vulnerable but made hand — pair of fours (4♠5♠ on A♠‑9♣‑4♣). Heads‑up the button opens a very wide range and just c‑bet a small half‑pot 300 into the 600 pot, which often contains a lot of air or A‑x/broadway denial hands. Calling 300 into the 900 pot gives correct pot odds to continue (keeps worse hands in, lets me realize equity and control pot size). Raising OOP here would bloat the pot when I’m frequently behind to any Ace or 9 and would commit too much with a marginal holding. Folding is too weak given I’ve made a pair and the price is reasonable. Plan: call 300, see the turn, and fold to large, polarized aggression or extract value from medium/weak hands on appropriate runouts.

Board A♠‑9♣‑4♣‑7♥; I hold 4♠5♠ — a made but vulnerable pair of fours with a weak kicker. Flash checked the turn, showing weakness or a wide missed holding. I want to extract thin value from missed broadway hands (KQ/KJ/QJ/Tx) and charge club draws while keeping the pot controllable against hands that beat me (Ax/9x). A small probe ~1/3 pot (400 into 1200) is the most efficient line: it folds pure air, gets called by worse hands, and keeps my options open with deep stacks. Plan: bet 400; fold to a large polarized check‑raise, re‑evaluate if called and play the river cautiously (check back most unsafe runouts, extract on safe ones).

On the turn, I have improved to a pair of sevens. Minni has led out with a small bet of 400 into a 1200 pot. This 'donk' bet often indicates a marginal hand, a draw, or a blocking bet with a weak Ace. Given that I have some showdown value now and am receiving 4-to-1 pot odds, I will call to see the river in position. This allows me to see if my pair holds up or if the board texture changes significantly on the final card.

I have a one-pair hand (pair of 4s, 4♠5♠) with a weak kicker on a final board 4♣‑A♠‑9♣‑7♥‑K♥. Flash’s line (button open pre, small c‑bet on the flop and then called my modest turn probe) is exactly the sort of range that contains lots of A‑x, 9‑x, Kx and medium broadways — many of which either already beat me or just rivered a King. The Kh is a very bad river card for my holding; it turns many of his reasonable hands into clear value hands and doesn’t add meaningful equity to my hand. There’s very little thin value to extract (worse Qx/Tx hands likely folded earlier or will fold to a bet) and betting just risks being called or raised by hands that beat me. I’ll exercise pot control and take the showdown: check and call a small to medium showdown bet if he bluffs, but otherwise show down my pair. Action: check.

On the river, the board is 4c-As-9c-7h-Kh. I hold 8d-7c for a pair of sevens. Minni has checked to me after leading on the turn. My pair of sevens has some showdown value, but it is unlikely to be the best hand if I am called after betting. Minni's turn lead and river check suggest she might have a marginal hand or missed draw. However, since I have some showdown value and the pot is 2000, checking back is the safest and most profitable play. It allows me to realize my equity and win if she was bluffing or has a lower pair without risking more chips.

Heads-up on the button (I am SB) with Jc9d — a standard open-raise hand heads-up. Calling 50 is too passive; raising takes the initiative, applies pressure to Flash's wide BB defending range, and lets me play postflop in position. I’ll make a minimum raise to 200 total: enough to get value/fold equity while keeping pot controllable. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold most of the time (J9o is weak vs a strong 3-bet); if he calls I’ll use positional advantage to continue/realize equity.

I am in the Big Blind with 2h7h, a weak suited hand. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, leaving me with 100 to call into a pot of 300. While 2-7 suited is a very weak hand, the pot odds (3:1) and being heads-up against Minni suggest a call can be marginally profitable if played carefully post-flop, especially given my significant stack lead and the fact that we are nearing the end of the session.

The flop is extremely dry with triple Tens. My 2h7h means my best hand is three-of-a-kind with a Seven kicker. This is a very weak holding in terms of kickers. I will check to see how the pre-flop aggressor, Minni, responds. There is no reason to lead out here as I'm likely either way ahead of total air or way behind a better kicker or a pocket pair.

Flop is T♠ T♣ T♦. I hold J♣9♦ — no pair, no straight or flush draws. I opened preflop and Flash flat‑called, so his continuing range is wide but includes many pocket pairs (22–QQ/KK/AA) that already make full houses on this board and the rare T in hand would be quads. My hand has essentially zero equity vs those hands and very poor bluff/blocker value. Betting here risks a check‑raise from a full house/quad and a multi‑street commitment with nothing; checking keeps the pot small and lets me fold to aggression. Plan: check, give up to meaningful bets, only entertain calling very tiny probes. Action: check.

The board has tripled tens and a jack. My hand, 7-2, is currently three-of-a-kind tens with a jack and a seven as the kickers. Given Minni's pre-flop raising range from the Small Blind, she is very likely to hold at least one card higher than a seven (Ace, King, Queen, or even an eight or nine), which would give her a better kicker. If she has a Jack, she has a full house. Since the pot is small and my hand is essentially a bluff-catcher with a very weak kicker on a board where many cards beat me, I will check and look to get to showdown as cheaply as possible.

Turn gave me a huge improvement: with J♣9♦ on T♠ T♣ T♦ J♥ I now have a full house (Jacks full of Tens). Villain checked flop and checked the turn, which keeps a lot of hands in his range — crucially many pocket pairs (22–QQ/KK/AA) that are full houses I beat, some single J combos (which will tie), and the relatively rare combos that beat me (the one remaining Ten in his hand making quads, or the tiny combo of both remaining Jacks making the superior full house). My hand is therefore extremely strong vs his continuing range and this is a clear value-betting spot rather than giving a free river. I want a size that extracts value from the many inferior full houses/pocket pairs but doesn’t scare them all off; I’ll bet ~3× the pot to build value and charge any draws. If he check‑raises very large, that strongly indicates one of the few hands that beat me (quads/JJ), and I’ll fold to an extreme shove or re‑evaluate against a smaller raise.

On a board with triple tens (Ts, Tc, Td) and a Jack (Jh), my hand (7h 2h) is effectively just three-of-a-kind tens with a Jack and Seven kicker. In this heads-up scenario, Minni's 3x pot overbet on the turn is extremely polarizing. Given her pre-flop raising range, it is highly probable that she holds a higher kicker (Ace, King, Queen, or Nine) or a Jack for a full house. Since almost any face card or mid-range card in her hand beats me, and I am essentially holding a very weak bluff-catcher against a massive bet, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my significant stack lead as we approach the end of the session.

I am on the button with JT offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. With a stack of 27,250 against Minni's 12,600, I have a significant chip lead. Standard strategy here is to raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. A raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play style throughout the match.

I’m in the big blind with 4♥3♣ facing a button open to 300 (pot 400, to call 200). Calling 200 to make it 300 gives break-even equity ≈ 200/(400+200)=33%. Flash is opening extremely wide from the button, so this hand has reasonable equity and playability heads-up with deep stacks. 3‑betting to 500 as a light bluff is unattractive — I have poor blockers and will be OOP postflop — so I’ll defend the blind. Plan: call to see the flop, continue with any pair, strong draw or favourable board texture, and fold to heavy pressure on dry boards.

I have 4♥3♣ on a 3♦ A♠ 9♥ flop — bottom pair with a poor kicker. Flash opened very wide preflop heads‑up, so his range contains many worse hands (Kx/Qx/Jx/T x, some floats) as well as Ax combos. The flop is fairly dry and there are few real draws, so a small value bet is the best way to extract from worse hands, deny free cards and avoid giving the initiative away. I’ll make a half‑pot probe (300 into 600): it gets thin value from hands that call but fold to larger pressure, keeps the pot manageable if he shows strength, and allows me to fold to a sizable raise (since I’m likely behind to A‑x or 9x). If he just calls I’ll reassess on the turn and proceed cautiously; if he raises big I’ll fold.

Minni has led into me for 300 on an Ace-high board (3d-As-9h). I have Jack-Ten offsuit, which provides me with no pairs and no significant draws. Since I am nearing the end of the match with a substantial chip lead, there is no strategic reason to float or bluff into a lead on a board that doesn't favor my specific holding. Folding is the most prudent play to protect my stack.

Heads-up on the button with pocket deuces and very deep stacks (~130BB). Pot is small and it’s only 50 to call, but 22 is a strong heads‑up hand to open — it has set-mining value and performs well with position. I want fold equity, to define the pot, and to deny cheap equity to random BB hands, so I'll open to a standard 3BB sizing. Plan: raise to 300 total; if Flash calls I’ll play postflop for sets and fold/cautiously continue when I miss; if he 3-bets large I’ll give credit and fold to preserve the stack (or call small 3-bets to set-mine).

This is the final hand of the match. I have a significant chip lead over Minni (26,900 vs 12,700). Holding 7c 5s out of position, there is no strategic reason to enter a pot and risk unnecessary chips when the priority is to preserve my lead and conclude the game. Folding here is the most risk-averse play to secure my final standing.
